# Section 1: Introduction 

1
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:03,180
<v ->As mentioned earlier, the course is highly practical</v>
2

2

00:00:03,180  -->  00:00:05,880
and you're gonna learn everything by example,
3

3

00:00:05,880  -->  00:00:08,220
by actually hacking and discovering bugs
4

4

00:00:08,220  -->  00:00:11,220
into real life examples, real life websites.
5

5

00:00:11,220  -->  00:00:13,860
But before we go ahead and start bug hunting,
6

6

00:00:13,860  -->  00:00:15,900
I want to cover some terminology
7

7

00:00:15,900  -->  00:00:18,750
that I think is crucial for you to understand.
8

8

00:00:18,750  -->  00:00:20,160
Now, you might already know this,
9

9

00:00:20,160  -->  00:00:22,440
so if you do, you can skip this lecture,
10

10

00:00:22,440  -->  00:00:24,660
but I highly recommend not skipping it,
11

11

00:00:24,660  -->  00:00:26,400
because it's only a few minutes,
12

12

00:00:26,400  -->  00:00:29,490
and I'm sure you're gonna learn a thing or two.
13

13

00:00:29,490  -->  00:00:31,980
So the main thing that I wanna cover in this lecture
14

14

00:00:31,980  -->  00:00:34,590
is what do we mean by bug hunting,
15

15

00:00:34,590  -->  00:00:37,650
and how does it compare to pen testing,
16

16

00:00:37,650  -->  00:00:40,828
which is something that I cover in my other courses.
17

17

00:00:40,828  -->  00:00:45,480
So let's focus on bug hunting and let's focus on the goal.
18

18

00:00:45,480  -->  00:00:47,160
And the goal of a bug hunt,
19

19

00:00:47,160  -->  00:00:51,750
is to discover bugs in a target application or a website.
20

20

00:00:51,750  -->  00:00:53,640
These bugs could be very simple.
21

21

00:00:53,640  -->  00:00:55,620
For example, here the bugs that fall
22

22

00:00:55,620  -->  00:00:58,260
under the information disclosure category,
23

23

00:00:58,260  -->  00:01:00,630
will simply just show you information
24

24

00:01:00,630  -->  00:01:02,910
that you're not supposed to be able to see.
25

25

00:01:02,910  -->  00:01:05,310
So for example, you might be able to see
26

26

00:01:05,310  -->  00:01:08,310
parts of the source code of the application.
27

27

00:01:08,310  -->  00:01:10,890
Now that's not gonna allow you to hack the server,
28

28

00:01:10,890  -->  00:01:13,020
It's not gonna compromise the server,
29

29

00:01:13,020  -->  00:01:15,360
but it can possibly be used
30

30

00:01:15,360  -->  00:01:17,040
combined with other information
31

31

00:01:17,040  -->  00:01:19,620
and other bugs, to hack the server.
32

32

00:01:19,620  -->  00:01:22,140
So the fact that you have an information disclosure,
33

33

00:01:22,140  -->  00:01:25,140
the fact that you're able to see parts of the source code,
34

34

00:01:25,140  -->  00:01:27,570
is enough to be considered as a bug.
35

35

00:01:27,570  -->  00:01:29,070
And in a bug bounty program,
36

36

00:01:29,070  -->  00:01:31,623
you will be paid a bounty or money for it.
37

37

00:01:32,460  -->  00:01:35,580
Another example is broken access control vulnerabilities,
38

38

00:01:35,580  -->  00:01:37,440
which will allow you to see information
39

39

00:01:37,440  -->  00:01:40,980
that belong to other users and possibly modify it.
40

40

00:01:40,980  -->  00:01:43,950
Again, that's not gonna allow you to hack into the server.
41

41

00:01:43,950  -->  00:01:45,990
It's not gonna compromise the server,
42

42

00:01:45,990  -->  00:01:47,940
but being able to do that,
43

43

00:01:47,940  -->  00:01:50,970
will cause loss of customer trust.
44

44

00:01:50,970  -->  00:01:53,460
So customers that use this application,
45

45

00:01:53,460  -->  00:01:54,750
are not gonna want to use it,
46

46

00:01:54,750  -->  00:01:57,600
because hackers can see their private information
47

47

00:01:57,600  -->  00:01:58,740
and if they know that,
48

48

00:01:58,740  -->  00:02:00,900
they're gonna lose trust in this application.
49

49

00:02:00,900  -->  00:02:02,760
So it's bad to the business owners
50

50

00:02:02,760  -->  00:02:06,180
or to the people that are running the target application.
51

51

00:02:06,180  -->  00:02:07,170
And that's why,
52

52

00:02:07,170  -->  00:02:10,020
if you discover a broken access control vulnerability
53

53

00:02:10,020  -->  00:02:11,190
and report the bug,
54

54

00:02:11,190  -->  00:02:14,880
you will probably get a bounty for it or cash.
55

55

00:02:14,880  -->  00:02:17,310
Another example is injection vulnerabilities.
56

56

00:02:17,310  -->  00:02:19,230
And these are very, very dangerous
57

57

00:02:19,230  -->  00:02:21,960
and actually can allow you or allow a hacker
58

58

00:02:21,960  -->  00:02:25,650
to gain full control and hack the target server.
59

59

00:02:25,650  -->  00:02:27,720
So the point that I'm trying to get across
60

60

00:02:27,720  -->  00:02:30,521
is these bugs will not always
61

61

00:02:30,521  -->  00:02:33,510
allow you to hack into the target website,
62

62

00:02:33,510  -->  00:02:36,600
sometimes they'll simply disclose some information,
63

63

00:02:36,600  -->  00:02:39,270
and that is enough for you to submit a report
64

64

00:02:39,270  -->  00:02:41,070
and get a reward for it.
65

65

00:02:41,070  -->  00:02:43,200
And don't worry about all of these names
66

66

00:02:43,200  -->  00:02:45,420
and all of these categories and what they mean.
67

67

00:02:45,420  -->  00:02:48,990
We will cover them in details in the future step by step.
68

68

00:02:48,990  -->  00:02:52,473
So, I'm just using this in here to get the point across.
69

69

00:02:53,490  -->  00:02:56,190
Once you master this, the rest is very easy.
70

70

00:02:56,190  -->  00:02:58,110
So you're gonna have to find a target
71

71

00:02:58,110  -->  00:03:00,540
and I'm gonna show you how to find many targets
72

72

00:03:00,540  -->  00:03:02,430
that have valid bug bounty programs,
73

73

00:03:02,430  -->  00:03:04,950
so you can discover bugs in them legally.
74

74

00:03:04,950  -->  00:03:06,360
You're gonna apply the information
75

75

00:03:06,360  -->  00:03:07,590
that you learned in this course
76

76

00:03:07,590  -->  00:03:09,510
to discover these bugs.
77

77

00:03:09,510  -->  00:03:12,120
And once you discover a bug, you're gonna report it.
78

78

00:03:12,120  -->  00:03:14,760
And I'm also gonna cover the reporting process
79

79

00:03:14,760  -->  00:03:17,124
and how to write a valid report.
80

80

00:03:17,124  -->  00:03:20,220
If the report is valid, you're gonna get a reward.
81

81

00:03:20,220  -->  00:03:22,560
And once you have your reward, you're gonna go ahead
82

82

00:03:22,560  -->  00:03:25,050
and discover more bugs in the same target,
83

83

00:03:25,050  -->  00:03:26,760
or you could actually go ahead
84

84

00:03:26,760  -->  00:03:28,350
and find another target,
85

85

00:03:28,350  -->  00:03:30,663
and discover bugs in it and repeat.
86

86

00:03:32,010  -->  00:03:35,670
So finding targets and reporting is very, very easy.
87

87

00:03:35,670  -->  00:03:38,070
I'm gonna be covering that at the end of the course,
88

88

00:03:38,070  -->  00:03:41,050
because it's so easy. And we're gonna be spending most
89

89

00:03:42,486  -->  00:03:44,340
of this course learning how to discover these bugs
90

90

00:03:44,340  -->  00:03:46,620
and vulnerabilities in different scenarios,
91

91

00:03:46,620  -->  00:03:49,740
in different examples, and how to bypass security,
92

92

00:03:49,740  -->  00:03:52,320
and actually get them to work.
93

93

00:03:52,320  -->  00:03:54,780
So as you can see in bug bounty programs,
94

94

00:03:54,780  -->  00:03:56,160
you are a freelancer
95

95

00:03:56,160  -->  00:03:58,170
and you have the freedom with many things.
96

96

00:03:58,170  -->  00:04:00,077
So you have the freedom of selecting your target.
97

97

00:04:00,077  -->  00:04:02,310
'Cause like I said, there is a lot of websites
98

98

00:04:02,310  -->  00:04:04,170
that already have bug bounty programs.
99

99

00:04:04,170  -->  00:04:07,260
So you can go ahead and test them whenever you want.
100

100

00:04:07,260  -->  00:04:08,940
You have the freedom of your own time,
101

101

00:04:08,940  -->  00:04:11,370
you select the time that you wanna do the tests,
102

102

00:04:11,370  -->  00:04:13,830
so you're not tied up to a work schedule.
103

103

00:04:13,830  -->  00:04:15,720
You can test them at any time you want.
104

104

00:04:15,720  -->  00:04:17,700
You can work at night, you can work in the morning,
105

105

00:04:17,700  -->  00:04:19,650
you can do it part-time if you want.
106

106

00:04:19,650  -->  00:04:21,960
It's completely up to you.
107

107

00:04:21,960  -->  00:04:23,340
You also have the freedom
108

108

00:04:23,340  -->  00:04:25,740
of selecting which bugs you want to discover.
109

109

00:04:25,740  -->  00:04:26,700
So throughout this course,
110

110

00:04:26,700  -->  00:04:27,660
we're gonna be covering
111

111

00:04:27,660  -->  00:04:29,880
a large number of bugs and vulnerabilities.
112

112

00:04:29,880  -->  00:04:33,030
And we're covering the top 10 most common ones as well.
113

113

00:04:33,030  -->  00:04:35,370
But at the end of the course, you might decide
114

114

00:04:35,370  -->  00:04:38,070
that I like injection vulnerabilities the most,
115

115

00:04:38,070  -->  00:04:40,260
they are not boring, they are a lot of fun,
116

116

00:04:40,260  -->  00:04:42,810
and I wanna always just test for them.
117

117

00:04:42,810  -->  00:04:45,480
So you can go ahead and test all of the websites
118

118

00:04:45,480  -->  00:04:46,920
that have bug bounty programs
119

119

00:04:46,920  -->  00:04:48,390
for injection vulnerabilities.
120

120

00:04:48,390  -->  00:04:50,490
You don't have to test them for everything.
121

121

00:04:50,490  -->  00:04:52,410
So you have the freedom of selecting the target,
122

122

00:04:52,410  -->  00:04:54,090
You have the freedom of your own time,
123

123

00:04:54,090  -->  00:04:56,340
you select the time that you wanna do the tests,
124

124

00:04:56,340  -->  00:04:58,170
and you also have the freedom
125

125

00:04:58,170  -->  00:05:00,420
of selecting the vulnerability that you want,
126

126

00:05:00,420  -->  00:05:02,703
or the bug that you want to look for.
127

127

00:05:04,230  -->  00:05:08,130
Now in a pentest, you actually have only one target,
128

128

00:05:08,130  -->  00:05:11,100
because a company or a website owner
129

129

00:05:11,100  -->  00:05:13,740
or an application owner is gonna approach you
130

130

00:05:13,740  -->  00:05:16,140
and tell you, I want you to test the security
131

131

00:05:16,140  -->  00:05:17,760
of my application.
132

132

00:05:17,760  -->  00:05:20,400
And the goal is not to discover bugs.
133

133

00:05:20,400  -->  00:05:23,640
The goal is actually to hack that target.
134

134

00:05:23,640  -->  00:05:26,430
And to do that, you're gonna be discovering bugs
135

135

00:05:26,430  -->  00:05:29,100
and vulnerabilities and exploit it.
136

136

00:05:29,100  -->  00:05:32,130
So you have another step, the exploitation step.
137

137

00:05:32,130  -->  00:05:33,630
You have to exploit the bugs
138

138

00:05:33,630  -->  00:05:35,580
and vulnerabilities that you find
139

139

00:05:35,580  -->  00:05:38,403
in order to hack into the target server.
140

140

00:05:39,240  -->  00:05:41,940
So in a pentest, you have an extra step in here,
141

141

00:05:41,940  -->  00:05:45,060
which is to exploit the bugs to hack into the target.
142

142

00:05:45,060  -->  00:05:47,640
And that's why I actually have a website hacking course,
143

143

00:05:47,640  -->  00:05:49,170
because the goal is different.
144

144

00:05:49,170  -->  00:05:50,970
The goal is actually to hack the target.
145

145

00:05:50,970  -->  00:05:53,250
It's not to simply discover bugs in it,
146

146

00:05:53,250  -->  00:05:54,630
because like I said some bugs
147

147

00:05:54,630  -->  00:05:57,090
are not even gonna allow you to hack into the target.
148

148

00:05:57,090  -->  00:06:00,510
<v ->So they're not that important when it comes to a pentest.</v>
149

149

00:06:00,510  -->  00:06:02,130
Now, as you can see in a pentest
150

150

00:06:02,130  -->  00:06:04,290
you actually do not have a lot of freedom.
151

151

00:06:04,290  -->  00:06:06,990
So you have only one target and you can't select it.
152

152

00:06:06,990  -->  00:06:09,360
The target approaches you, the website owner,
153

153

00:06:09,360  -->  00:06:11,490
or the application owner approaches you,
154

154

00:06:11,490  -->  00:06:14,790
and asks you to pen test their application.
155

155

00:06:14,790  -->  00:06:16,320
You also don't have the freedom
156

156

00:06:16,320  -->  00:06:17,880
of doing it in your own time.
157

157

00:06:17,880  -->  00:06:20,730
So there is usually a time limit on the contract,
158

158

00:06:20,730  -->  00:06:22,350
a week or two or a month,
159

159

00:06:22,350  -->  00:06:25,020
but you can't just do it whenever you want.
160

160

00:06:25,020  -->  00:06:27,120
And finally, you also don't have the freedom
161

161

00:06:27,120  -->  00:06:28,470
of selecting your bugs.
162

162

00:06:28,470  -->  00:06:30,270
So you have to test the application
163

163

00:06:30,270  -->  00:06:32,940
for all of the possible bugs and vulnerabilities.
164

164

00:06:32,940  -->  00:06:35,040
You have to guarantee full cover.
165

165

00:06:35,040  -->  00:06:37,830
So again, that's different compared to bug bounty programs,
166

166

00:06:37,830  -->  00:06:41,040
because you can select the bug, you can select the target,
167

167

00:06:41,040  -->  00:06:43,323
and you can do it in your own time.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
1

00:00:00,630  -->  00:00:02,490
<v Instructor>Just like all of my other courses,</v>
2

2

00:00:02,490  -->  00:00:04,530
this course is highly practical,
3

3

00:00:04,530  -->  00:00:06,750
but it does not neglect the theory,
4

4

00:00:06,750  -->  00:00:08,970
so we'll start with the basics just to make sure
5

5

00:00:08,970  -->  00:00:11,820
that you really understand what web applications
6

6

00:00:11,820  -->  00:00:14,730
are and how they work, the technologies used,
7

7

00:00:14,730  -->  00:00:16,954
and how these technologies work together
8

8

00:00:16,954  -->  00:00:20,943
to produce these platforms that we love and use every day.
9

9

00:00:21,811  -->  00:00:26,074
So a website is basically just a web application installed
10

10

00:00:26,074  -->  00:00:30,240
on a server, and a server is simply just a computer.
11

11

00:00:30,240  -->  00:00:31,950
So right now we have this computer,
12

12

00:00:31,950  -->  00:00:34,533
it could be any computer, it could be a personal computer.
13

13

00:00:34,533  -->  00:00:36,990
And when we say it's a server,
14

14

00:00:36,990  -->  00:00:39,330
it just has certain applications
15

15

00:00:39,330  -->  00:00:42,300
on it that allow it to act as a server.
16

16

00:00:42,300  -->  00:00:45,840
Now, generally, servers on the internet are accessible
17

17

00:00:45,840  -->  00:00:47,940
from any computer on the internet,
18

18

00:00:47,940  -->  00:00:51,060
so they're hosted on the cloud as they call them now,
19

19

00:00:51,060  -->  00:00:53,160
and basically it has a real IP,
20

20

00:00:53,160  -->  00:00:54,630
and then anybody connected
21

21

00:00:54,630  -->  00:00:56,556
to the internet is able to communicate
22

22

00:00:56,556  -->  00:00:58,320
with this computer.
23

23

00:00:58,320  -->  00:00:59,550
Because this computer has
24

24

00:00:59,550  -->  00:01:03,000
a specific application that allow it to act as a server,
25

25

00:01:03,000  -->  00:01:04,410
we call it a server.
26

26

00:01:04,410  -->  00:01:06,360
But if you strip everything down,
27

27

00:01:06,360  -->  00:01:07,770
it's basically just a computer
28

28

00:01:07,770  -->  00:01:10,110
with special programs installed on it.
29

29

00:01:10,110  -->  00:01:12,900
Then we make that computer accessible over the cloud,
30

30

00:01:12,900  -->  00:01:16,590
over the internet, which makes it very appropriate to use
31

31

00:01:16,590  -->  00:01:19,140
as a web server or a server that can be accessed
32

32

00:01:19,140  -->  00:01:21,924
from other computers or other locations.
33

33

00:01:21,924  -->  00:01:25,560
Now, a very common application that could make it act
34

34

00:01:25,560  -->  00:01:28,039
as a server is a web server.
35

35

00:01:28,039  -->  00:01:29,790
Now, what is a web server?
36

36

00:01:29,790  -->  00:01:32,070
It's basically an application that you can install
37

37

00:01:32,070  -->  00:01:33,030
on any computer.
38

38

00:01:33,030  -->  00:01:35,249
So the computer doesn't even have to be exposed
39

39

00:01:35,249  -->  00:01:36,420
to the internet.
40

40

00:01:36,420  -->  00:01:38,910
You can install it on your own local computer,
41

41

00:01:38,910  -->  00:01:41,040
even if it's not connected to the internet.
42

42

00:01:41,040  -->  00:01:42,750
It's just an application.
43

43

00:01:42,750  -->  00:01:44,580
And what that application does,
44

44

00:01:44,580  -->  00:01:48,418
it specifies a certain location within your file system,
45

45

00:01:48,418  -->  00:01:51,420
and in that location you can host files.
46

46

00:01:51,420  -->  00:01:54,270
So anybody connected to the same network as you,
47

47

00:01:54,270  -->  00:01:56,246
or if this computer is exposed
48

48

00:01:56,246  -->  00:01:57,780
to the internet and anybody
49

49

00:01:57,780  -->  00:01:59,220
that is on the internet
50

50

00:01:59,220  -->  00:02:01,973
will be able to access the files stored
51

51

00:02:01,973  -->  00:02:04,530
within the web root or the file
52

52

00:02:04,530  -->  00:02:08,220
that the web server uses to serve files to the users.
53

53

00:02:08,220  -->  00:02:10,511
Now, web servers usually also contain
54

54

00:02:10,511  -->  00:02:12,720
the web application files.
55

55

00:02:12,720  -->  00:02:16,110
So a web application is similar to any application.
56

56

00:02:16,110  -->  00:02:18,570
The only difference is, it's designed to run
57

57

00:02:18,570  -->  00:02:21,930
on a web server and designed to be served
58

58

00:02:21,930  -->  00:02:24,840
over the internet to another computer.
59

59

00:02:24,840  -->  00:02:28,389
So the web server can execute web application languages
60

60

00:02:28,389  -->  00:02:31,080
similar to PHP, Python, and Ruby.
61

61

00:02:31,080  -->  00:02:33,788
So you would have the web application files stored
62

62

00:02:33,788  -->  00:02:36,420
within the web server, 'cause like I said,
63

63

00:02:36,420  -->  00:02:38,250
the initial goal of the web server
64

64

00:02:38,250  -->  00:02:41,730
is to be able to serve files to remote computers
65

65

00:02:41,730  -->  00:02:45,089
using a web browser using the HTTP protocol.
66

66

00:02:45,089  -->  00:02:47,970
The web application files contain code,
67

67

00:02:47,970  -->  00:02:50,280
so the website files could be images,
68

68

00:02:50,280  -->  00:02:51,900
they could be normal HTML files,
69

69

00:02:51,900  -->  00:02:53,354
so they don't really contain code,
70

70

00:02:53,354  -->  00:02:55,289
but the web application files
71

71

00:02:55,289  -->  00:02:58,410
they actually contain code written in a programming
72

72

00:02:58,410  -->  00:03:01,620
language that can process information
73

73

00:03:01,620  -->  00:03:04,470
and manipulate it and do useful tasks.
74

74

00:03:04,470  -->  00:03:05,670
So it would be written
75

75

00:03:05,670  -->  00:03:08,850
in languages like PHP, Python, Ruby, and so on.
76

76

00:03:08,850  -->  00:03:11,428
And most websites that actually do something useful
77

77

00:03:11,428  -->  00:03:13,020
other than websites just
78

78

00:03:13,020  -->  00:03:14,700
that just simply display information,
79

79

00:03:14,700  -->  00:03:17,160
like a shop website, they would actually be written
80

80

00:03:17,160  -->  00:03:20,318
in a programming language such as these.
81

81

00:03:20,318  -->  00:03:22,770
Now another very common component
82

82

00:03:22,770  -->  00:03:25,649
of a website is the database.
83

83

00:03:25,649  -->  00:03:29,010
So, the web server contains the files,
84

84

00:03:29,010  -->  00:03:31,950
like images, styling files, HTML files,
85

85

00:03:31,950  -->  00:03:34,620
these are all just used to display the website
86

86

00:03:34,620  -->  00:03:35,730
to render the website,
87

87

00:03:35,730  -->  00:03:37,560
and then you have the web application files
88

88

00:03:37,560  -->  00:03:41,460
which is the ones run on the server side to process data.
89

89

00:03:41,460  -->  00:03:45,270
But the data is usually stored in a different application
90

90

00:03:45,270  -->  00:03:46,634
which is called a database.
91

91

00:03:46,634  -->  00:03:49,044
Now the database contains a number of tables
92

92

00:03:49,044  -->  00:03:51,951
and we have in here a sample of a table.
93

93

00:03:51,951  -->  00:03:53,520
And we're assuming
94

94

00:03:53,520  -->  00:03:55,930
that this table contains products that are being sold
95

95

00:03:55,930  -->  00:03:57,780
on a shop website.
96

96

00:03:57,780  -->  00:04:01,200
So you can see we have the ID of the products,
97

97

00:04:01,200  -->  00:04:02,100
we have the title,
98

98

00:04:02,100  -->  00:04:05,310
so this is an iPhone, an iPad, a mouse, and the price,
99

99

00:04:05,310  -->  00:04:08,748
we have the price in here for each of these products.
100

100

00:04:08,748  -->  00:04:12,300
So, the web server contains the HTML pages,
101

101

00:04:12,300  -->  00:04:14,700
the beautiful pages that you actually see.
102

102

00:04:14,700  -->  00:04:17,010
The ones with the, it contains the images,
103

103

00:04:17,010  -->  00:04:19,170
so the images for the iPhones, the iPads,
104

104

00:04:19,170  -->  00:04:21,510
the mouses that this website is selling.
105

105

00:04:21,510  -->  00:04:25,191
It also contains a web application that interacts
106

106

00:04:25,191  -->  00:04:29,970
with the database to display all of these products.
107

107

00:04:29,970  -->  00:04:32,850
So the web application itself doesn't contain the products,
108

108

00:04:32,850  -->  00:04:35,961
the product information is stored in the database.
109

109

00:04:35,961  -->  00:04:39,090
Now, the database contains a number of tables.
110

110

00:04:39,090  -->  00:04:41,415
This is just one example of a table,
111

111

00:04:41,415  -->  00:04:43,887
but it'll contain tables, for example, for the users
112

112

00:04:43,887  -->  00:04:46,350
and it'll contain the usernames and the passwords.
113

113

00:04:46,350  -->  00:04:48,300
And then users, when they log in,
114

114

00:04:48,300  -->  00:04:50,670
the web application will communicate
115

115

00:04:50,670  -->  00:04:53,070
with the database to check if the username you
116

116

00:04:53,070  -->  00:04:55,650
supplied is the same one as the password.
117

117

00:04:55,650  -->  00:04:57,690
So it's very important to understand the role
118

118

00:04:57,690  -->  00:04:59,010
of the database.
119

119

00:04:59,010  -->  00:05:01,260
Web applications stored on the web server,
120

120

00:05:01,260  -->  00:05:04,950
it just communicates with the database, gets information,
121

121

00:05:04,950  -->  00:05:08,220
and then displays it or does something useful with it,
122

122

00:05:08,220  -->  00:05:11,280
but the data itself is not stored on the web server,
123

123

00:05:11,280  -->  00:05:13,500
it's not stored within the web application.
124

124

00:05:13,500  -->  00:05:15,813
It's actually stored on the database.

# Section 2: Information Disclosure vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,270  -->  00:00:04,980
The first bug or vulnerability that we're gonna cover is information disclosure.
1

2

00:00:05,320  -->  00:00:08,980
This falls under the cryptographic failure category in the
2

3

00:00:09,260  -->  00:00:13,260
top 10, which is the second most common security threat.
3

4

00:00:13,610  -->  00:00:14,170
Now
4

5

00:00:14,630  -->  00:00:20,320
is an independent non profit organization that is concerned about web security.
5

6

00:00:20,330  -->  00:00:23,550
So they have no reason to skew these results.
6

7

00:00:23,550  -->  00:00:27,320
So the results you see in there are based on tests that they've done and
7

8

00:00:27,320  -->  00:00:30,620
information they gathered themselves and based on that
8

9

00:00:30,630  -->  00:00:33,550
they rated the most common security threats,
9

10

00:00:33,550  -->  00:00:37,340
bugs and vulnerabilities and information disclosure falls within the
10

11

00:00:37,340  -->  00:00:40,670
cryptographic failure which is second on their list.
11

12

00:00:41,050  -->  00:00:42,500
As the name suggests,
12

13

00:00:42,510  -->  00:00:46,120
these vulnerabilities allow you to access information that
13

14

00:00:46,120  -->  00:00:48,980
should not be attainable or visible to you.
14

15

00:00:49,140  -->  00:00:51,780
I'm choosing to start with these bugs because they are
15

16

00:00:51,780  -->  00:00:54,470
simple but at the same time they are very common.
16

17

00:00:54,480  -->  00:00:59,480
So they're great to serve as a warm up to get you used to the basics and to
17

18

00:00:59,480  -->  00:01:03,315
get your mind thinking like a hacker also because
18

19

00:01:03,315  -->  00:01:07,365
these type of bucks tend to expose sensitive information,
19

20

00:01:07,415  -->  00:01:10,835
they can actually point you to other more dangerous box.
20

21

00:01:10,945  -->  00:01:16,305
And they can also help you discover hidden parts of the target application which
21

22

00:01:16,305  -->  00:01:18,925
will increase the attack surface and therefore
22

23

00:01:18,935  -->  00:01:21,905
increase your chances of discovering other bugs.
23

24

00:01:22,190  -->  00:01:26,310
Companies like Shopify Snapchat and Starbucks were vulnerable
24

25

00:01:26,310  -->  00:01:29,400
to some sort of an information disclosure and
25

26

00:01:29,400  -->  00:01:32,340
they paid anything from hundreds to thousands of
26

27

00:01:32,340  -->  00:01:35,500
dollars to the hunters that discovered these bugs.
27

28

00:01:35,510  -->  00:01:39,070
So keep your eyes open to everything that I'm gonna show you,
28

29

00:01:39,080  -->  00:01:41,590
make sure you understand it and practice it in the
29

30

00:01:41,590  -->  00:01:44,250
labs that I'm going to give you throughout this section,
30

31

00:01:44,260  -->  00:01:46,760
and you will be discovering your first buck and
31

32

00:01:46,760  -->  00:01:49,370
cashing out your first bounty in no time.

00:04:23,940  -->  00:04:28,140
In this section, we're looking for information disclosure vulnerabilities.

91

00:04:28,150  -->  00:04:29,320
So anything,
91

92

00:04:29,330  -->  00:04:33,880
any glitch or any bug or anything that might leak or display information
92

93

00:04:34,010  -->  00:04:37,900
that the developers that created this website do not want you
93

94

00:04:37,900  -->  00:04:40,350
to see or do not want normal users to see.
94

95

00:04:40,840  -->  00:04:43,430
You can use tools to do that and tools are actually better
95

96

00:04:43,430  -->  00:04:46,180
at doing this because this is a bit of a repetitive process.
96

97

00:04:46,190  -->  00:04:48,410
I don't like using tools and I will be showing
97

98

00:04:48,410  -->  00:04:50,730
you how to do everything manually throughout the course.
98

99

00:04:50,780  -->  00:04:54,060
But for this particular task tools can be better.
99

100

00:04:54,540  -->  00:04:57,510
But I'm just going to show you a real quick trick that you
100

101

00:04:57,510  -->  00:05:01,220
can do on most websites that will help you maybe find some secret
101

102

00:05:01,230  -->  00:05:04,200
paths and then in the next lecture I'll show you how to use
102

103

00:05:04,210  -->  00:05:09,060
a really useful tool to discover possibly hidden paths or sensitive data.
103

104

00:05:09,740  -->  00:05:13,030
The trick is to simply load the robots the txt file.
104

105

00:05:13,040  -->  00:05:16,510
So we're simply going to go to whatever the website is. It doesn't really matter.
105

106

00:05:16,520  -->  00:05:21,350
We're going to put a forward slash robots dot txt.
106

107

00:05:21,740  -->  00:05:23,260
So this is a text file
107

108

00:05:23,370  -->  00:05:26,790
that a lot of websites include in their home directory.
108

109

00:05:26,960  -->  00:05:32,420
And the reason why they included because it tells search engines such as google,
109

110

00:05:32,420  -->  00:05:37,030
bing and yahoo which paths they should avoid and should not index.
110

111

00:05:37,540  -->  00:05:40,140
Therefore if you load it and if you actually get a file.
111

112

00:05:40,140  -->  00:05:42,290
So as you can see we've got some content in here.
112

113

00:05:42,490  -->  00:05:47,460
So basically this robots dot txt file is telling google and other search engines
113

114

00:05:47,760  -->  00:05:53,760
to not index whatever data that is included in the following path in the backup path.
114

115

00:05:54,240  -->  00:05:58,270
So whatever user agent it is star means everything. It's a wild card.

R. L. In here.

38

39

42

00:02:13,840  -->  00:02:18,270
This is because whatever number we're putting in here after the equal
42

43

00:02:18,270  -->  00:02:23,650
sign is being sent as a get request to the web application,
43

44

00:02:23,660  -->  00:02:26,740
to the cloud or to the server where this application is
44

45

00:02:26,740  -->  00:02:30,150

45

46

00:02:30,150  -->  00:02:31,660
D. Of number three.

47

00:02:32,440  -->  00:02:37,440
So anything that you put after the equal sign in the U. R. L. Bar.
47

48

00:02:37,450  -->  00:02:44,350
So whatever value you have will be sent as a get method to the target website.
48

49

00:02:44,940  -->  00:02:46,590
Now this U. R. L.
49

50

00:02:46,600  -->  00:02:50,470
Could take a number of shapes so you should forget about the looks of it.

51

00:02:50,470  -->  00:02:53,060
You should forget about the PHP party in here.

52

00:02:53,070  -->  00:02:55,180
The main thing you want to be concerned about is
52

53

00:02:55,180  -->  00:02:57,540
the way we have an equal sign and a value
53

54

00:02:57,550  -->  00:03:00,490
after the equals sign and whatever value that we have
54

55

00:03:00,490  -->  00:03:03,450
in here will be sent to the target website.
55

56

00:03:04,640  -->  00:03:07,300
This is really important to understand because
56

57

00:03:07,300  -->  00:03:10,100
we know now by manipulating this value,

58

58

59

00:03:14,490  -->  00:03:18,010
the web application on the cloud and therefore we might
59

60

be able to cause to misbehave to either reveal information
60

61

62

on when we talk about other vulnerabilities and bugs.

64

00:03:31,570  -->  00:03:33,810
But the main thing that we want to understand right
64

65

00:03:33,810  -->  00:03:37,690
now is the value after the equal sign will be sent
65

66

00:03:37,690  -->  00:03:41,200
to the target web application which will process that value
66

67

00:03:41,210  -->  00:03:45,060

67

68

00:03:46,240  -->  00:03:50,050
Now, don't worry about what comes before the equal sign.
68

69

00:03:50,050  -->  00:03:52,670
So this could take a number of forms and even
69

70

00:03:52,670  -->  00:03:55,310
don't worry about the PHP or the name in here.

71

00:03:55,320  -->  00:03:59,610
So you might go on websites and see them to be dot jsp.
71

72

You might not even have a file extension or you might not even have a file name.
72

73

00:04:04,330  -->  00:04:06,440
You might just simply have a question mark.
73

74

00:04:06,450  -->  00:04:08,260
Or you might not even have a question mark.
74

75

00:04:08,640  -->  00:04:10,980
If you see a value after the equal sign,
75

76

00:04:10,990  -->  00:04:16,589
you should know that this value will be sent as a get request to the target
76

77

00:04:16,690  -->  00:04:17,630
application.
77

78

00:04:17,640  -->  00:04:21,140
The target to a publication will process this value and
78

79

00:04:21,140  -->  00:04:24,560
therefore you can use this value to manipulate this application.
79

80

00:04:24,810  -->  00:04:27,450
And that's why in here when we're changing the number,
80

81

00:04:27,640  -->  00:04:31,550
we are able to load different products because the web application is
81

82

00:04:31,550  -->  00:04:34,680
getting this value is getting number three and it's saying okay,
82

83

But let's see what happens if for example,

85

86

And perfect as you can see we cause the application to throw an air

and from this error message were able to deduce a number of things.

Also, if you look at the end,

And assuming you don't know what that is.

And employing the bug hunter mentality or the
93

hacker mentality that we spoke about earlier,

95

we can simply
95

0
1

00:00:00,340  -->  00:00:02,850
now that we have burp suite installed
1

2

00:00:03,010  -->  00:00:07,040
in this lecture. I'm going to show you how to use its proxy functionality
2

3

00:00:07,180  -->  00:00:11,540
to intercept requests before they go to the web application.
3

4

00:00:11,730  -->  00:00:16,630
And as a result will be able to see and manipulate inputs
4

5

00:00:16,810  -->  00:00:21,250
that cannot be seen or manipulated using D R L or
5

6

00:00:21,250  -->  00:00:25,060
using normal input boxes that we see on the website.
6

7

00:00:25,840  -->  00:00:30,210
This will obviously allow us to test more features and therefore increases
7

8

00:00:30,210  -->  00:00:33,140
our chances of discovering bugs and
8

9

00:00:33,140  -->  00:00:36,160
vulnerabilities in the target web application.
9

10

00:00:37,040  -->  00:00:39,700
So as you can see here on the top, we have a proxy
10

11

00:00:39,820  -->  00:00:44,920
and if I click on it, it's already set to on. So the proxy is turned on
11

12

00:00:45,100  -->  00:00:49,730
and you can click on open browser to open up a normal chromium browser
12

13

00:00:49,930  -->  00:00:53,150
that this proxy will intercept its requests.
13

14

00:00:53,540  -->  00:00:56,600
Let me show you what I mean. I'm going to click on open browser
14

15

00:00:56,830  -->  00:01:01,660
and let's go to a website. So for example, let's go to z security dot org.
15

16

00:01:03,950  -->  00:01:08,660
And as you can see the website is not loading, it seems like the website is stuck
16

17

00:01:09,040  -->  00:01:13,720
and the reason why it's stuck because the request for the security dot org
17

18

00:01:13,870  -->  00:01:17,760
got intercepted by our proxy in here.
18

19

00:01:18,640  -->  00:01:23,350
And as you can see in here we have the full request that is being sent to the website.
19

20

00:01:24,340  -->  00:01:27,360
So you can see the type of request, it's a get request.
20

21

00:01:27,740  -->  00:01:32,100
It's being sent to forward slash So it's to the route to the home directory because
21

22

00:01:32,100  -->  00:01:35,080
we haven't really specified any files or directories
22

23

00:01:35,080  -->  00:01:37,290
or end points after the domain name.
23

24

00:01:37,460  -->  00:01:40,240
You can see the host is the security dot org.
24

25

00:01:40,240  -->  00:01:42,860
And you can actually see it in here on the top as well.
25

26

00:01:43,340  -->  00:01:46,850
You could see the cookies that are being sent to the website.
26

27

00:01:47,140  -->  00:01:50,010
These are small files that get exchanged between
27

28

00:01:50,010  -->  00:01:52,480
the website and the target web server.
28

29

00:01:52,490  -->  00:01:53,950
The website uses them
29

30

00:01:54,070  -->  00:01:57,170
to enhance your experience and to set up certain options.
30

31

00:01:57,180  -->  00:01:58,730
And you will see in the future we can
31

32

00:01:58,730  -->  00:02:02,660
manipulate these cookies to again discover bugs and vulnerabilities.
32

33

00:02:03,540  -->  00:02:06,580
So these cookies are not visible in the U R L or in
33

34

00:02:06,580  -->  00:02:09,860
any text boxes but you'll be able to modify them in here.
34

35

00:02:10,840  -->  00:02:15,890
You can also see the other headers that are being sent to the target web application.
35

36

00:02:15,900  -->  00:02:18,990
But again, you don't really need to worry about them too much. Right now.
36

37

00:02:19,000  -->  00:02:21,250
We will be talking about them later on.
37

38

00:02:21,940  -->  00:02:25,960
So right now we have options in here. You can either forward this request.
38

39

00:02:25,960  -->  00:02:28,860
So if we click on forward burp proxy is gonna
39

40

00:02:28,860  -->  00:02:31,750
let that request go to the target web server.
40

41

00:02:32,240  -->  00:02:34,240
If you click on drop, it's going to drop it.
41

42

00:02:34,240  -->  00:02:37,760
So it's basically gonna stop it from reaching its destination
42

43

00:02:38,340  -->  00:02:41,190
and here intercept. You can't really see the rest of it.
43

44

00:02:41,190  -->  00:02:43,940
It's basically saying intercept is on which means that
44

45

00:02:43,940  -->  00:02:46,560
we are actually intercepting all of the requests.
45

46

00:02:47,240  -->  00:02:49,600
So I can simply either forward to forward this
46

47

00:02:49,600  -->  00:02:51,860
request and I'm going to get the next request.
47

48

00:02:51,870  -->  00:02:54,820
I can forward again, I'll get the next request.
48

49

00:02:54,850  -->  00:03:01,050
So as you can see sometimes a large number of requests is sent in order to load
49

50

00:03:01,050  -->  00:03:06,170
one single website or what it seems like one single request in here in your browser.
50

51

00:03:06,170  -->  00:03:09,600
So as soon as you had entered, you think only one request is being sent to the
51

52

00:03:09,710  -->  00:03:13,760
application. But in real life there is much more happening in the background.
52

53

00:03:14,540  -->  00:03:18,560
So you can keep clicking forward until it loads or you can turn
53

54

00:03:18,560  -->  00:03:23,510
off the interceptor to stop intercepting packets and load the target website.
54

55

00:03:24,440  -->  00:03:26,680
Now because you're loading our own websites,
55

56

00:03:26,680  -->  00:03:30,520
the security dot org we have very strict security settings.
56

57

00:03:30,540  -->  00:03:33,400
That's why you're being stopped by this web page.
57

58

00:03:33,410  -->  00:03:37,040
So even if you solve this capture, it's not going to let you go through it.
58

59

00:03:37,060  -->  00:03:40,660
So to bypass this, you're gonna have to go to the options.
59

60

00:03:40,750  -->  00:03:43,850
We're gonna scroll down to the match and replace
60

61

00:03:44,340  -->  00:03:49,360
and we're going to take the one that specifies the Moselle a five user agent.
61

62

00:03:49,520  -->  00:03:52,840
So we're basically saying we want to replace our user agent
62

63

00:03:52,850  -->  00:03:56,510
with a normal Mozilla user agent which is used by Firefox.
63

64

00:03:56,610  -->  00:03:59,710
And therefore this website will hopefully not think that
64

65

00:03:59,710  -->  00:04:02,850
we are using some security tools like Burp suite.
65

66

00:04:03,910  -->  00:04:07,410
We will talk about the match under a place feature later on in the course.
66

67

00:04:07,410  -->  00:04:11,460
So don't worry about this because we're just using a preset rule in here.

68

00:04:12,040  -->  00:04:15,460
So now we can simply just say I am a human
68

69

00:04:17,850  -->  00:04:20,410
and it's gonna let me through to my target website.
69

Now watch this. We're going to scroll down.

And before I click on page three

72

I'm gonna turn on my interceptor in here.
72

73

00:04:30,140  -->  00:04:34,790
So now any request or anything I click in here will have to pass by burp suite
73

74

00:04:35,050  -->  00:04:38,590
and it will only go to the target website if I click on forward
74

75

00:04:38,910  -->  00:04:42,440
and remember when we clicked on three before all we sell

is only one parameter in here that we could manipulate.

again you have the method type. So this time the method type is post, it's not get

79

and that's why whatever values that gets sent in here
79

R. L. Bar.

So as mentioned earlier,

everything we see after an equal sign is a value that
89

90

91

92

34

this is eight different inputs that we should test for all of the
98

99

box and vulnerabilities that you will learn later on in the course.
