00:08:20,640  -->  00:08:21,560
to the cart
139

140

00:08:23,340  -->  00:08:25,460
if you click on the query parameters
140

141

00:08:25,840  -->  00:08:29,950
you will see the names of the parameters. So remove item
141

142

00:08:30,640  -->  00:08:31,660
and its value
142

143

00:08:32,039  -->  00:08:32,360
Yes
143

144

00:08:33,039  -->  00:08:34,780
and the WP announce
144

145

00:08:34,890  -->  00:08:37,260
and its value and we have them right here.
145

146

00:08:38,440  -->  00:08:42,929
So you can modify them from here by double clicking it and type in whatever you want
146

147

00:08:42,929  -->  00:08:48,060
to type and hit enter or you could modify it directly in here in plain text,
147

148

00:08:49,440  -->  00:08:53,580
we also have a category here for the body parameters.
148

149

00:08:53,590  -->  00:08:57,770
So this is similar to the values that gets sent in here at the bottom.
149

150

00:08:57,770  -->  00:09:00,060
Similar to the first example I showed you,
150

151

00:09:00,070  -->  00:09:02,350
you'll be able to see them in here and you'll be
151

152

00:09:02,350  -->  00:09:05,060
able to modify them as well by double clicking them.
152

153

00:09:05,540  -->  00:09:07,530
You can see all of the cookies that we saw
153

154

00:09:07,530  -->  00:09:10,390
the cookies in here before you can see them in here
154

155

00:09:10,400  -->  00:09:13,720
also and you have the cookie name and its value
155

156

00:09:13,730  -->  00:09:16,550
and again you can simply modify it by double clicking it
156

157

00:09:17,040  -->  00:09:18,360
and then hit an enter
157

158

00:09:18,840  -->  00:09:21,390
and finally we have the request headers.
158

159

00:09:21,400  -->  00:09:25,250
So again, this is pretty much everything that is being sent in here,
159

160

00:09:25,260  -->  00:09:28,760
categorized nicely with the name and the value.
160

161

00:09:29,640  -->  00:09:33,140
Now we will be using Burp suite and the proxy in
161

162

00:09:33,140  -->  00:09:36,980
pretty much every single lecture that we're gonna do from now onwards
162

163

00:09:36,990  -->  00:09:40,330
and I'm gonna walk you through a lot of its features as
163

164

00:09:40,330  -->  00:09:43,410
I show you and explain to you other bugs and vulnerabilities.
164

165

00:09:43,420  -->  00:09:46,350
So you're going to be learning a number of things in every lecture.
165

166

00:09:46,540  -->  00:09:48,720
So don't worry at all about all of the
166

167

00:09:48,720  -->  00:09:51,950
other tabs and don't get overwhelmed about this application.
167

168

00:09:51,960  -->  00:09:55,310
You're not going to have to figure out anything by yourself.
168

169

00:09:55,320  -->  00:09:58,060
I will walk you through everything step by step.
169

170

00:09:58,640  -->  00:10:01,470
But the main thing that I wanted to show you in this lecture
170

171

00:10:01,680  -->  00:10:06,910
is how to use this program to intercept requests and how useful that is.
171

172

00:10:06,920  -->  00:10:08,130
Because, like I said,
172

173

00:10:08,140  -->  00:10:12,240
it's gonna radically increase the size of your attack surface,
173

174

00:10:12,250  -->  00:10:16,660
allowing you to test more parameters and therefore increasing your
174

175

00:10:16,660  -->  00:10:20,860
chances of discovering bugs and vulnerabilities in your target.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Secction 3: Broken Access Control Vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,170  -->  00:00:05,220
the next bug or vulnerability that we're going to cover is broken access control.
1

2

00:00:05,230  -->  00:00:07,790
According to the OaS top 10 list.
2

3

00:00:07,800  -->  00:00:13,520
This is the most common security threat out there with 94% of the
3

4

00:00:13,520  -->  00:00:19,245
websites tested being vulnerable to some sort of a broken access control.
4

5

00:00:19,505  -->  00:00:23,235
Broken access control is actually a really large category
5

6

00:00:23,265  -->  00:00:27,175
that contains a number of subcategories such as path,
6

7

00:00:27,175  -->  00:00:32,244
traversal vulnerabilities, C S R F I door and much more.
7

8

00:00:32,575  -->  00:00:38,185
It's basically a general term that encapsulates any bug or vulnerability that
8

9

00:00:38,185  -->  00:00:43,505
allows you to access or modify data beyond your limits or permissions.
9

10

00:00:43,770  -->  00:00:46,830
A simple example would be to be able to access
10

11

00:00:46,840  -->  00:00:49,760
or modify data that does not belong to you.
11

12

00:00:49,770  -->  00:00:51,870
That belongs to a different user
12

13

00:00:52,430  -->  00:00:53,970
platforms like Uber
13

14

00:00:54,380  -->  00:00:56,670
and Shopify were vulnerable to such
14

15

00:00:56,670  -->  00:01:00,120
vulnerabilities and they paid anything between 500
15

16

00:01:00,420  -->  00:01:05,370
tens of thousands of dollars to the hunters that discovered such vulnerabilities.
16

17

00:01:05,379  -->  00:01:11,290
Obviously the amount of bounty depended on the impact and the severity of the bug.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,740  -->  00:00:04,420
So when looking for broken access control vulnerabilities.
1

2

00:00:04,470  -->  00:00:08,590
We're trying to see if we can access or modify information
2

3

00:00:08,760  -->  00:00:10,780
that does not belong to us.
3

4

00:00:11,020  -->  00:00:16,250
We can do this by trying to access or modify data without logging in.
4

5

00:00:16,740  -->  00:00:21,480
And then we can create an account on the target to have application if it allows you
5

6

00:00:21,480  -->  00:00:24,500
to create accounts and see if you can access
6

7

00:00:24,510  -->  00:00:28,050
or modify data that belongs to another user.
7

8

00:00:28,640  -->  00:00:31,770
And we'll have a lot of examples of this as we go through this section.
8

9

00:00:31,770  -->  00:00:33,050
So don't worry about it.
9

10

00:00:33,380  -->  00:00:37,490
But let's start with something very simple just to get you warmed up and
10

11

00:00:37,490  -->  00:00:43,060
also to get you used to intercepting and modifying data using burp proxy.
11

12

00:00:43,940  -->  00:00:48,130
I'll include a link to the example in the resources of the lecture as usual.
12

13

00:00:48,290  -->  00:00:52,000
And usually I would test every single functionality
13

14

00:00:52,000  -->  00:00:54,430
and press every single button within the website
14

15

00:00:54,620  -->  00:00:56,230
as a normal user.
15

16

00:00:56,370  -->  00:01:02,060
And then I would go to my account and create a user on the target website.
16

17

00:01:02,840  -->  00:01:07,280
Because usually once you create a user on the target website and login,
17

18

00:01:07,290  -->  00:01:10,210
you will have access to more features.
18

19

00:01:10,210  -->  00:01:13,520
So for example you might be able to add comments or reviews
19

20

00:01:13,790  -->  00:01:17,880
and you'll also probably be able to edit your information on your profile.
20

21

00:01:17,890  -->  00:01:21,870
So that opens up a lot more functionality and therefore opens up
21

22

00:01:21,880  -->  00:01:26,260
a larger attack surface and therefore increasing my chances of discovering bugs.
22

23

00:01:26,540  -->  00:01:30,010
Now we'll talk more about my hacking methodology later
23

24

00:01:30,010  -->  00:01:31,770
on in the course in the last section.
24

25

00:01:32,340  -->  00:01:36,220
But right now I'm talking about it because also once you log in,
25

26

00:01:36,230  -->  00:01:40,790
you'll be able to test for access control vulnerabilities and see if
26

27

00:01:40,790  -->  00:01:44,850
you can access or modify data that belong to other users.
27

28

00:01:45,440  -->  00:01:49,360
Now in this website you can't create an account because it's a test website.
28

29

00:01:49,430  -->  00:01:53,800
But they are giving us an account that's already created on the target website.
29

30

00:01:53,870  -->  00:01:57,160
So the user name is Weiner and the password is peter.
30

31

00:01:57,740  -->  00:01:59,110
So we're going to copy this
31

32

00:02:00,040  -->  00:02:02,660
and we're gonna paste it here in the user name
32

33

00:02:02,960  -->  00:02:06,160
and in the password we're gonna log in
33

34

00:02:06,330  -->  00:02:10,370
and perfect as we log in as you can see and like I said earlier,
34

35

00:02:10,380  -->  00:02:15,360
you already have an extra feature in here which allows you to update your email.
35

36

00:02:15,640  -->  00:02:18,890
So that's why when you create an account you should go ahead and
36

37

00:02:18,890  -->  00:02:23,640
go through the whole website and see what extra features opened up.
37

38

00:02:23,650  -->  00:02:24,580
Once done with that,
38

39

00:02:24,580  -->  00:02:27,960
you should test for access control vulnerabilities and see if
39

40

00:02:27,960  -->  00:02:31,150
you can access data that belong to other users.
40

41

00:02:31,740  -->  00:02:34,500
Now we're not doing a full pen test or a full bug hunt.
41

42

00:02:34,510  -->  00:02:37,680
We will talk more about that in the last section of the course.
42

43

00:02:37,690  -->  00:02:42,450
I'm gonna go directly to where the bug is and that is in the admin page.
43

44

00:02:43,040  -->  00:02:46,890
So we saw earlier how to use fireworks poster to discover such paths.
44

45

00:02:46,940  -->  00:02:48,630
We're assuming you've already done that.
45

46

00:02:48,640  -->  00:02:50,440
And we're going to the admin page,
46

47

00:02:50,450  -->  00:02:54,750
we're gonna load it as is usually before intercepting the requests
47

48

00:02:54,980  -->  00:02:56,720
and as you can see it's telling you you can
48

49

00:02:56,720  -->  00:02:59,760
only access the admin if you're logged in as administrator
49

50

00:02:59,900  -->  00:03:00,560
makes sense.
50

51

00:03:01,550  -->  00:03:03,530
Now let's turn on our interceptor
51

52

00:03:03,860  -->  00:03:06,190
and let's go back to the admin page.
52

53

00:03:06,300  -->  00:03:07,010
Enter
53

54

00:03:07,290  -->  00:03:10,590
and perfect as usual we're going to intercept the request in here.
54

55

00:03:10,600  -->  00:03:15,030
We can see it's a get request to the admin end point to the admin page
55

56

00:03:15,150  -->  00:03:18,300
and usually you would actually have disclosed because
56

57

00:03:18,300  -->  00:03:19,860
I opened it in the last lecture.
57

58

00:03:20,140  -->  00:03:24,640
So if you click in here you'll be able to see all of the requests
58

59

00:03:24,790  -->  00:03:28,920
and I have all the categories unfolded as well because I manually unfolded them.
59

60

00:03:28,930  -->  00:03:32,210
So in a real life scenario it will look like this.
60

61

00:03:32,530  -->  00:03:33,230
And
61

62

00:03:33,360  -->  00:03:36,000
if you click on the request parameters you can see
62

63

00:03:36,000  -->  00:03:38,760
the get request and the path it's going to.
63

64

00:03:39,040  -->  00:03:40,960
You have nothing in the equity or in the
64

65

00:03:40,960  -->  00:03:43,360
body parameters so nothing to test them there.
65

66

00:03:43,540  -->  00:03:45,550
But we have two cookies being sent.
66

67

00:03:46,300  -->  00:03:47,950
And as you can see one of the cookies
67

68

00:03:47,950  -->  00:03:51,040
is very interesting because the name of it is admin
68

69

00:03:51,420  -->  00:03:53,090
and its value is false.
69

70

00:03:53,840  -->  00:03:57,050
So let's see what happens if we set this value to true.
70

71

00:03:58,240  -->  00:03:59,350
We're gonna hit enter
71

72

00:03:59,740  -->  00:04:02,050
and we're going to forward this request
72

73

00:04:02,940  -->  00:04:05,620
and perfect now that we set that cookie
73

74

00:04:05,780  -->  00:04:06,630
to True
74

75

00:04:06,760  -->  00:04:09,260
as you can see we got access to the admin page.
75

76

00:04:10,020  -->  00:04:11,680
This admin page right here as you can
76

77

00:04:11,680  -->  00:04:15,070
see it's very simple just for testing but nevertheless
77

78

00:04:15,070  -->  00:04:18,959
this is a real bug that you would be able to submit in a real bug hunt
78

79

00:04:19,540  -->  00:04:22,960
and in a pen test. You should definitely include that in your report.
79

80

00:04:23,540  -->  00:04:27,920
Now, usually admin panels allow you to do so much more than simply deleting users.
80

81

00:04:27,930  -->  00:04:32,860
But in here if you go ahead and try to delete a user, for example the user Carlos
81

82

00:04:33,540  -->  00:04:35,970
and let's turn off our interceptor because we want this
82

83

00:04:35,970  -->  00:04:38,670
to go ahead because we don't want to intercept anything.
83

84

00:04:38,810  -->  00:04:41,070
You'll see you're going to get the error again.
84

85

00:04:41,080  -->  00:04:43,950
Admin interface only available to logged in users.
85

86

00:04:44,340  -->  00:04:47,900
You're getting this error because when we clicked on the delete
86

87

00:04:48,130  -->  00:04:51,300
our cookie was set to false again.
87

88

00:04:51,310  -->  00:04:56,550
Therefore the request was executed as a normal user and not as admin.
88

89

00:04:57,240  -->  00:05:00,380
So you'll have to intercept it again and set it to True.
89

90

00:05:00,390  -->  00:05:03,330
And if this admin page offered more functionality,
90

91

00:05:03,340  -->  00:05:07,290
you'll have to intercept every single request and manually
91

92

00:05:07,300  -->  00:05:09,860
change the value of the cookie to True.
92

93

00:05:10,540  -->  00:05:15,660
Alternatively, we can use defined under place functionality of Burp suite.
93

94

00:05:16,340  -->  00:05:18,620
So if we go on the options in here
94

95

00:05:18,800  -->  00:05:21,100
and go to the match under place.
95

96

00:05:21,110  -->  00:05:26,560
You can see we have already a number of rules preset by the developers of Burp suite.
96

97

00:05:27,040  -->  00:05:30,250
But what we want to do is we want to add our own rule
97

98

00:05:30,940  -->  00:05:35,130
and we want to say that every time you see a certain string
98

99

00:05:35,260  -->  00:05:37,880
we want to replace it with something else.
99

100

00:05:38,440  -->  00:05:43,860
Now for us to get that string we actually have to go and intercept the request again.
100

101

00:05:43,870  -->  00:05:48,760
So I'm gonna turn on the interceptor. I'm gonna go back to the admin page
101

102

00:05:50,640  -->  00:05:54,730
and I'm gonna find the cookie which sets the admin to false.
102

103

00:05:54,740  -->  00:05:59,250
So it's this cookie right here. So it's saying admin is equal to false.
103

104

00:05:59,570  -->  00:06:01,000
I'm gonna copy this.
104

105

00:06:01,150  -->  00:06:04,330
I'm going to go to my options to define in their place.
105

106

00:06:04,440  -->  00:06:06,860
I'm going to click on add to add a rule
106

107

00:06:07,140  -->  00:06:11,160
and I'm going to say every time you see admin is equal to false,
107

108

00:06:11,440  -->  00:06:15,160
I want you to replace it with admin is equal to true.
108

109

00:06:15,940  -->  00:06:18,860
We're going to say okay and we're gonna make sure
109

110

00:06:18,870  -->  00:06:21,960
that this rule that we just created is ticked.
110

111

00:06:22,940  -->  00:06:25,750
So now I won't even need to intercept anything.
111

112

00:06:25,760  -->  00:06:28,290
So let me just drop this package whatever it was.
112

113

00:06:28,460  -->  00:06:30,410
I'm gonna turn off my interceptor
113

114

00:06:30,590  -->  00:06:34,170
and once I load the page in here the data is
114

115

00:06:34,170  -->  00:06:37,480
gonna go through burp proxy but it's not gonna be intercepted.
115

116

00:06:37,600  -->  00:06:42,690
It's just gonna automatically replace every time it sees admin equal to
116

117

00:06:42,690  -->  00:06:45,750
false it's gonna replace it with admin is equal to true.
117

118

00:06:46,340  -->  00:06:48,560
So let me show you I'm gonna hit enter in here
118

119

00:06:49,040  -->  00:06:51,680
and perfect I'm already at the admin page.
119

120

00:06:51,890  -->  00:06:56,700
If I go to home I already have access to the admin page because burp
120

121

00:06:56,710  -->  00:07:01,550
is automatically replacing admin is equal to false with admin is equal to true.
121

122

00:07:02,140  -->  00:07:06,110
So if I have an admin page that has a lot of features in here.
122

123

00:07:06,120  -->  00:07:09,960
I'll be able to use it just like an admin user without
123

124

00:07:09,960  -->  00:07:14,520
having to intercept every single request and manually modifying it in here.
124

125

00:07:14,530  -->  00:07:15,060
In Burp.
125

126

00:07:15,940  -->  00:07:20,010
Now this match and replace feature is very useful in so many scenarios.
126

127

00:07:20,020  -->  00:07:24,040
So I'm just using this as an example to show you how to use this feature.
127

128

00:07:24,060  -->  00:07:27,300
But as you go ahead and start pen testing and bug hunting,
128

129

00:07:27,310  -->  00:07:30,210
you're going to notice a lot of scenarios while you'll need to
129

130

00:07:30,210  -->  00:07:34,590
automatically find a certain string and replace it with something else.
130

131

00:07:34,600  -->  00:07:37,360
And this feature is going to come very very handy.
131

132

00:07:38,040  -->  00:07:42,260
So just to complete this I'm gonna have to delete the user called Carlos
132

133

00:07:42,640  -->  00:07:47,120
and perfect as you can see I'm able to delete this user because like I said,
133

134

00:07:47,130  -->  00:07:50,560
burp is automatically setting my privileges to admin.
134

135

00:07:51,440  -->  00:07:52,390
Now, once done,
135

136

00:07:52,400  -->  00:07:55,790
you want to make sure that you go back to your rule and either
136

137

00:07:55,800  -->  00:08:00,920
on ticket so that burp stops applying it to every packet that it intercepts.
137

138

00:08:01,340  -->  00:08:05,690
Or you can simply remove it from the lists if you're not going to use it anymore.
138

139

00:08:05,960  -->  00:08:10,180
This is very important so that it won't interfere with any further
139

140

00:08:10,180  -->  00:08:13,850
tests that you're going to carry on on your target web application


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,240  -->  00:00:04,790
in the last lecture we had an example of a broken access control bug or
1

2

00:00:04,790  -->  00:00:08,189
vulnerability that allowed us to literally gain access
2

3

00:00:08,189  -->  00:00:10,980
to the admin panel by modifying the cookies
3

4

00:00:11,220  -->  00:00:12,880
but as mentioned earlier,
4

5

00:00:12,910  -->  00:00:18,960
broken access control is a term that covers a large number of vulnerabilities.
5

6

00:00:19,340  -->  00:00:23,480
Any vulnerability that allows us to access or modify data that does
6

7

00:00:23,480  -->  00:00:27,860
not belong to us is considered a broken access control vulnerability.
7

8

00:00:28,640  -->  00:00:32,439
So another really good way of testing these kind of vulnerabilities
8

9

00:00:32,610  -->  00:00:36,110
is to create two accounts on the target website.
9

10

00:00:36,120  -->  00:00:38,660
Have different information for these accounts.
10

11

00:00:38,660  -->  00:00:42,300
So I have different names, different emails and different addresses.
11

12

00:00:42,300  -->  00:00:47,400
For example whatever information it asks for just submit different information.
12

13

00:00:47,600  -->  00:00:51,050
And then we're gonna log in as one of these accounts
13

14

00:00:51,340  -->  00:00:55,980
and see while I'm logged in as James bond for example can I
14

15

00:00:55,980  -->  00:01:00,860
access the name of a different account or their email or their address
15

16

00:01:01,070  -->  00:01:02,140
if I can.
16

17

00:01:02,230  -->  00:01:05,300
And if the website is designed to not
17

18

00:01:05,310  -->  00:01:08,580
share this information for example some blog websites
18

19

00:01:08,710  -->  00:01:11,950
do share on purpose the names of their authors.
19

20

00:01:12,140  -->  00:01:14,510
But let's say for example if I'm able to
20

21

00:01:14,510  -->  00:01:19,120
access their email or their address from another account,
21

22

00:01:19,260  -->  00:01:24,110
then that means I am able to access information that does not belong to me.
22

23

00:01:24,230  -->  00:01:26,150
And therefore it means that I discovered
23

24

00:01:26,160  -->  00:01:29,650
a broken access control vulnerability or bug.
24

25

00:01:30,040  -->  00:01:32,190
Let me show you this in a nice example.
25

26

00:01:32,200  -->  00:01:34,600
I'm going to share its link in the resources and
26

27

00:01:34,600  -->  00:01:37,130
as usually want to test everything within the website.
27

28

00:01:37,280  -->  00:01:40,760
And once done we're going to create two accounts and like I said,
28

29

00:01:40,760  -->  00:01:42,310
we do this for two purposes.
29

30

00:01:42,320  -->  00:01:44,680
First is that you'll be able to test more
30

31

00:01:44,680  -->  00:01:48,160
functionalities such as comments and reviews and so on.
31

32

00:01:48,340  -->  00:01:52,060
And second you'll be able to test for broken access control.
32

33

00:01:52,940  -->  00:01:56,160
Now because this is a test website similar to the previous one,
33

34

00:01:56,170  -->  00:02:00,350
you can't create accounts but you can log in with the following credentials.
34

35

00:02:00,840  -->  00:02:02,250
So we're going to copy
35

36

00:02:02,640  -->  00:02:05,250
and paste it in here to log in
36

37

00:02:06,340  -->  00:02:08,350
and perfect now that I'm logged in,
37

38

00:02:08,740  -->  00:02:11,100
you can see that it's giving me my user name
38

39

00:02:11,160  -->  00:02:15,180
and a certain api key which belongs to me only.
39

40

00:02:15,570  -->  00:02:18,420
And I also have an extra feature to update my email.
40

41

00:02:18,430  -->  00:02:21,150
Like I said, this is very useful usually when you log in,
41

42

00:02:21,160  -->  00:02:24,060
you get more features to test on the target website.
42

43

00:02:24,440  -->  00:02:28,580
So therefore you'd want to go home and start looking for new features that are
43

44

00:02:28,580  -->  00:02:32,960
only available to you as a user and test them for bugs and vulnerabilities.
44

45

00:02:33,740  -->  00:02:35,960
Now, if you view a post in here,
45

46

00:02:36,340  -->  00:02:39,190
you can see we have a user called Carlos.
46

47

00:02:39,940  -->  00:02:41,600
Now, if I click on Carlos,
47

48

00:02:41,870  -->  00:02:47,500
it's going to give me the blog posts written by him. And if you look closely to the U. R.
48

49

00:02:47,500  -->  00:02:51,460
L. In here, you'll see that we have his user I. D.
49

50

00:02:51,840  -->  00:02:55,260
If you see this in a target website, you should make a note of it.
50

51

00:02:55,940  -->  00:02:56,830
I'm going to copy it.
51

52

00:02:56,830  -->  00:03:01,660
However, because I know this is going to be useful and if you go back to your account
52

53

00:03:02,240  -->  00:03:04,920
you'll notice that on the top you're accessing a
53

54

00:03:04,920  -->  00:03:07,700
path or an endpoint that is called my account.
54

55

00:03:07,910  -->  00:03:13,790
And you're passing a value of an I. D. To it. This I. D. Is probably your own I. D.
55

56

00:03:13,800  -->  00:03:17,860
And it's different from Carlos I. D. That we just copied.
56

57

00:03:18,440  -->  00:03:22,660
So let's see what happens if we paste the idea of Carlos
57

58

00:03:23,140  -->  00:03:24,160
we had entered.
58

59

00:03:24,540  -->  00:03:29,080
And perfect right now it's telling us that our user name is Carlos and
59

60

00:03:29,090  -->  00:03:34,860
our api key is this so we're able to access Carlos api key.
60

61

00:03:35,340  -->  00:03:39,770
So because we're able to access information that belong to another user
61

62

00:03:39,780  -->  00:03:44,060
that means we discovered a broken access control bug or vulnerability.
62

63

00:03:44,200  -->  00:03:47,900
This is a valid bug that you should submit. If you're hunting for bugs
63

64

00:03:48,340  -->  00:03:51,660
and you should definitely include it in your report. If your pen testing.
64

65

00:03:52,740  -->  00:03:57,890
Now depending on your target, you might not be able to guess this I. D.
65

66

00:03:57,900  -->  00:03:59,850
Or get it from a blog post.
66

67

00:04:00,340  -->  00:04:02,850
Therefore it is really useful to follow
67

68

00:04:02,850  -->  00:04:05,520
this methodology where you create two accounts.
68

69

00:04:05,530  -->  00:04:09,790
And then because this account belongs to you, you know it's I. D.
69

70

00:04:09,830  -->  00:04:12,800
And you can log in from this account and try to access
70

71

00:04:12,800  -->  00:04:16,130
the data that belongs to this account using this guy's I.
71

72

00:04:16,130  -->  00:04:16,560
D.
72

73

00:04:16,940  -->  00:04:19,579
Another way of doing this is to simply guess it.
73

74

00:04:19,899  -->  00:04:23,960
So there is another example in here. I'm going to share it in the resources as usual.
74

75

00:04:24,740  -->  00:04:27,050
And in this example let's log in first
75

76

00:04:27,940  -->  00:04:29,760
same user name and password.
76

77

00:04:31,140  -->  00:04:33,930
And this time there is nowhere for you in the
77

78

00:04:33,930  -->  00:04:38,550
home directory to find other users on the same page.
78

79

00:04:38,640  -->  00:04:43,680
Therefore you'll either have to create another user yourself and see if you can
79

80

00:04:43,690  -->  00:04:48,960
access its information or you're going to have to guess another user name.
80

81

00:04:49,240  -->  00:04:52,640
So this time in the account I. D. You're actually not getting an I. D.
81

82

00:04:52,650  -->  00:04:54,550
You're getting an actual user name.
82

83

00:04:54,640  -->  00:04:58,150
So a really good user name to guess is admin for example.
83

84

00:04:58,840  -->  00:05:03,240
And that looks like it doesn't work because it brought us back to the login page.
84

85

00:05:03,250  -->  00:05:06,160
So maybe we should try administrator
85

86

00:05:08,040  -->  00:05:10,320
and perfect as you can see that worked
86

87

00:05:10,550  -->  00:05:13,050
and it's also showing us the password this time.
87

88

00:05:13,640  -->  00:05:17,340
Now this password appears like it's actually hashed.
88

89

00:05:17,350  -->  00:05:21,380
But you can very easily see it by inspecting the
89

90

00:05:21,380  -->  00:05:25,360
code that is responsible for rendering this part of the page
90

91

00:05:26,340  -->  00:05:29,310
regardless of the language that the target application
91

92

00:05:29,320  -->  00:05:31,700
is written in at the back end site,
92

93

00:05:31,710  -->  00:05:35,690
whether it's written in python, java, ruby, it really doesn't matter.
93

94

00:05:35,820  -->  00:05:37,180
At the end of the day,
94

95

00:05:37,190  -->  00:05:41,080
everything will have to be rendered as html because browsers
95

96

00:05:41,080  -->  00:05:45,390
can only interpret html and javascript so everything will have to
96

97

00:05:45,390  -->  00:05:49,150
be rendered in a text based format that the browser
97

98

00:05:49,160  -->  00:05:52,860
is able to understand and show you here as elements.
98

99

00:05:53,430  -->  00:05:57,300
Therefore you can right click any element that you see on the page
99

100

00:05:57,520  -->  00:05:59,360
and click on inspect
100

101

00:05:59,940  -->  00:06:02,150
to see the html code
101

102

00:06:02,250  -->  00:06:05,660
that is responsible for rendering this element.
102

103

00:06:06,140  -->  00:06:08,200
So as you can see if I highlight
103

104

00:06:08,470  -->  00:06:11,450
the password in here, it gets highlighted on the top.
104

105

00:06:12,140  -->  00:06:16,130
If I highlight the label for the password, it gets highlighted on the top.
105

106

00:06:16,140  -->  00:06:19,910
If I highlight the input box for the password in here,
106

107

00:06:19,920  -->  00:06:23,740
it's getting highlighted on the top because this is the html code
107

108

00:06:23,870  -->  00:06:28,400
that is responsible for rendering this element for displaying this element
108

109

00:06:29,140  -->  00:06:30,950
and you can see in here
109

110

00:06:31,050  -->  00:06:33,900
we actually have a value parameter
110

111

00:06:34,070  -->  00:06:38,860
that is specifying the password. So this is actually the password in plain text.
111

112

00:06:39,240  -->  00:06:42,700
So even though it seems like the password is invisible in here,
112

113

00:06:42,710  -->  00:06:46,350
you can easily see it in here by inspecting the element
113

114

00:06:47,140  -->  00:06:51,280
and now you can simply copy this and then go to the admin page.
114

115

00:06:51,280  -->  00:06:52,560
So we're going to log out,
115

116

00:06:53,140  -->  00:06:54,650
go to the admin page.
116

117

00:06:54,760  -->  00:06:56,560
I'm assuming there is an admin page
117

118

00:06:57,440  -->  00:06:59,930
and actually just log in with the user name,
118

119

00:07:00,440  -->  00:07:04,530
administrator and the password that I just copied.
119

120

00:07:04,940  -->  00:07:07,460
I'm going to close this and log in
120

121

00:07:08,040  -->  00:07:11,680
and perfect as you can see now we are logged in as admin,
121

122

00:07:11,730  -->  00:07:15,350
we can go to the admin panel and delete users
122

123

00:07:15,360  -->  00:07:20,250
or use any functionality that is only available to the admin


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,340  -->  00:00:01,290
in this lecture,
1

2

00:00:01,300  -->  00:00:04,030
we're gonna talk about a special case of
2

3

00:00:04,030  -->  00:00:07,960
broken access control vulnerabilities that is called a door
3

4

00:00:08,940  -->  00:00:13,760
ID or is short for insecure direct object reference.
4

5

00:00:13,920  -->  00:00:16,010
And even though the name is pretty long,
5

6

00:00:16,079  -->  00:00:19,250
it's actually a very simple vulnerability or a bug.
6

7

00:00:19,840  -->  00:00:24,760
It happens when the application directly accesses user
7

8

00:00:24,760  -->  00:00:29,210
data without verification based on user input.
8

9

00:00:29,460  -->  00:00:32,000
Therefore, by manipulating the user input,
9

10

00:00:32,009  -->  00:00:37,390
we will be able to directly retrieve data that does not belong to us
10

11

00:00:37,540  -->  00:00:42,700
and because we are able to see and possibly modify data that does not belong to us.
11

12

00:00:43,140  -->  00:00:46,470
I doors fall under broken access control because that
12

13

00:00:46,470  -->  00:00:49,950
is the definition of broken access control vulnerabilities.
13

14

00:00:50,840  -->  00:00:55,720
We use the word object in here to refer to any type of data.
14

15

00:00:55,890  -->  00:01:01,140
For example, it could be files that belong to another user, it could be images
15

16

00:01:01,240  -->  00:01:05,230
that belong to another user. Now obviously we're talking about private images.
16

17

00:01:05,239  -->  00:01:08,180
So if it's a profile picture for example in facebook that is
17

18

00:01:08,180  -->  00:01:11,870
visible to everybody and you're able to access it as another user
18

19

00:01:12,040  -->  00:01:14,680
that is not a broken access control or an ID
19

20

00:01:14,680  -->  00:01:17,420
or because that is the design of the application,
20

21

00:01:17,430  -->  00:01:19,270
it's supposed to show you that picture.
21

22

00:01:19,450  -->  00:01:21,270
But assume you're able to access
22

23

00:01:21,370  -->  00:01:25,100
private pictures that shouldn't be available to you or to other users,
23

24

00:01:25,190  -->  00:01:28,250
then that would be a valid ID or vulnerability
24

25

00:01:28,840  -->  00:01:32,830
objects could also refer to database records.
25

26

00:01:32,840  -->  00:01:37,850
So in the previous example we were able to modify this value in here.
26

27

00:01:37,850  -->  00:01:41,870
So this is user input and we were able to retrieve the
27

28

00:01:41,870  -->  00:01:46,750
api key and other information by simply modifying this value in here.
28

29

00:01:47,240  -->  00:01:51,200
So this could possibly be a blind eye door.
29

30

00:01:51,210  -->  00:01:56,200
I'm saying blind because we don't have access to the web application and
30

31

00:01:56,200  -->  00:01:59,100
we're not sure that this is an ID or we are sure that we
31

32

00:01:59,100  -->  00:02:03,450
were able to discover information and we're sure that it's a broken access control
32

33

00:02:03,460  -->  00:02:08,259
but we're not sure what is the programming mistake that is causing this.
33

34

00:02:08,539  -->  00:02:13,290
But assuming that the web application is directly communicating with the database
34

35

00:02:13,400  -->  00:02:16,520
based on the user input that were given it in here,
35

36

00:02:16,690  -->  00:02:19,730
then that means it is an eye door because it is directly
36

37

00:02:19,740  -->  00:02:25,960
accessing database records associated with this idea using our user input.
37

38

00:02:28,040  -->  00:02:31,460
So by simply modifying this idea right here,
38

39

00:02:31,840  -->  00:02:35,900
even though we're logged in as john wick. If we replace this I. D.
39

40

00:02:35,910  -->  00:02:38,700
By one which is James Bond's I. D.
40

41

00:02:38,710  -->  00:02:42,160
We will be able to retrieve James Bond's information
41

42

00:02:42,540  -->  00:02:46,230
because the web application is communicating with the database
42

43

00:02:46,330  -->  00:02:51,100
directly and retrieving the record that is associated with I. D.
43

44

00:02:51,110  -->  00:02:54,860
Number one and therefore it will get us James Bond's information.
44

45

00:02:55,540  -->  00:03:00,170
Now, as mentioned earlier I doors are not limited to database records.
45

46

00:03:00,180  -->  00:03:04,150
You could use them to directly access documents and images that
46

47

00:03:04,150  -->  00:03:06,900
are supposed to be private that do not belong to us.
47

48

00:03:06,910  -->  00:03:08,920
So an example in here
48

49

00:03:09,080  -->  00:03:13,840
assuming that the web application directly loads the profile picture from
49

50

00:03:13,840  -->  00:03:16,990
a path that is called images James dot J P G.
50

51

00:03:17,350  -->  00:03:21,850
And assuming that this picture is private, it shouldn't be visible to all users
51

52

00:03:22,240  -->  00:03:24,940
as another user or without logging in.
52

53

00:03:25,030  -->  00:03:30,600
If you're able to open up your browser and put the direct path to this image in your,
53

54

00:03:30,600  -->  00:03:31,500
your al bar.
54

55

00:03:31,510  -->  00:03:35,130
And the image is actually loading without authentication and
55

56

00:03:35,130  -->  00:03:38,480
without checking if you actually own this image,
56

57

00:03:38,490  -->  00:03:43,220
then you're able to directly access this object and therefore
57

58

00:03:43,230  -->  00:03:47,960
this is an id or vulnerability insecure direct object reference.
58

59

00:03:48,940  -->  00:03:52,010
Let me show you an example which will make it even easier
59

60

00:03:52,150  -->  00:03:52,960
to understand.
60

61

00:03:53,340  -->  00:03:57,160
I'm going to include the link of this example and the resources as usual.
61

62

00:03:57,300  -->  00:04:01,760
And we have a simple website in here similar to the ones that we've been testing and as
62

63

00:04:01,760  -->  00:04:04,860
usual you should be testing every single functionality but
63

64

00:04:04,860  -->  00:04:07,240
I know that the bug is in the live chat
64

65

00:04:07,430  -->  00:04:11,660
and as usual you want to go ahead and use this chat as a normal user.
65

66

00:04:11,740  -->  00:04:16,470
So let's just say hello, we're gonna click on send to send the message.
66

67

00:04:16,480  -->  00:04:20,649
You get a response, we're going to say, how are you
67

68

00:04:21,040  -->  00:04:22,560
send the message again?
68

69

00:04:22,720  -->  00:04:24,340
You get another response.
69

70

00:04:24,520  -->  00:04:28,760
So we know that this feature works and we know how it should work in general
70

71

00:04:28,930  -->  00:04:32,650
and we have another button that we haven't pressed which is the view transcript.
71

72

00:04:32,740  -->  00:04:36,150
And when you press it it's going to download a text file for you
72

73

00:04:36,340  -->  00:04:39,090
and if we open this text file you'll
73

74

00:04:39,090  -->  00:04:42,460
see the content of your conversation with this but
74

75

00:04:43,140  -->  00:04:46,950
Also notice the name of this text file is 2.txt.
75

76

00:04:47,640  -->  00:04:49,370
And if we continue
76

77

00:04:49,500  -->  00:04:53,010
chatting to the spot. So let's say test this time
77

78

00:04:54,640  -->  00:05:00,250
and then download the transcript again, you'll notice now the number is three.
78

79

00:05:00,370  -->  00:05:01,700
And if we click it
79

80

00:05:01,800  -->  00:05:06,760
again we're gonna get an updated version of our conversation with the spot.
80

81

00:05:07,540  -->  00:05:10,700
Now that we tested the normal functionality of the spot,
81

82

00:05:10,860  -->  00:05:13,280
let's go ahead and intercept the requests.
82

83

00:05:13,280  -->  00:05:16,930
Because as you can see when we're clicking on send and view transcript,
83

84

00:05:16,980  -->  00:05:19,090
nothing is really being upended in
84

85

00:05:19,090  -->  00:05:22,450
here because everything is actually being submitted
85

86

00:05:22,460  -->  00:05:25,250
as a post request as you're going to see in a second.
86

87

00:05:25,400  -->  00:05:27,850
Therefore there isn't really much for us to test
87

88

00:05:27,860  -->  00:05:30,630
other than the data that we input in here.
88

89

00:05:30,640  -->  00:05:33,430
So you should test this text box for all
89

90

00:05:33,430  -->  00:05:36,300
the vulnerabilities that you will learn in the future.
90

91

00:05:36,350  -->  00:05:40,930
But for now let's turn on the interceptor and let's send another message.
91

92

00:05:40,930  -->  00:05:42,360
So let's say another test.
92

93

00:05:43,040  -->  00:05:45,850
And as you can see we can see the message that is being sent.
93

94

00:05:45,850  -->  00:05:47,260
We're just going to forward it
94

95

00:05:47,740  -->  00:05:51,900
forward it again. You can see the typing when the boat is typing
95

96

00:05:52,250  -->  00:05:53,160
forward
96

97

00:05:54,040  -->  00:05:57,060
and we have the answer as well, which is pretty offensive.
97

98

00:05:57,740  -->  00:06:00,660
But anyway, now that our interceptor is on,
98

99

00:06:00,670  -->  00:06:05,170
let's also click on view transcript to download our transcript
99

100

00:06:05,460  -->  00:06:07,440
and as you can see for that to work
100

101

00:06:07,440  -->  00:06:10,540
this button is simply just sending an empty message to
101

102

00:06:10,540  -->  00:06:13,700
download the transcript and the web application is translating
102

103

00:06:13,700  -->  00:06:16,550
that as the user wants to download the transcript.
103

104

00:06:17,140  -->  00:06:18,960
So anyway, we're gonna forward this.
104

105

00:06:19,120  -->  00:06:23,040
And as you can see now a post request is being sent
105

106

00:06:23,050  -->  00:06:27,360
to this path and when a post request is sent to this path
106

107

00:06:27,740  -->  00:06:34,800
it's gonna trigger another get request but this time it is to a text file.
107

108

00:06:34,810  -->  00:06:38,170
So as you can see this time, the number is four dot txt.
108

109

00:06:38,180  -->  00:06:40,920
So the first file that we downloaded was to the
109

110

00:06:40,920  -->  00:06:45,050
second was three and the third is four dot txt.
110

111

00:06:45,640  -->  00:06:48,310
And if we forward this request again,
111

112

00:06:48,520  -->  00:06:51,850
our browser is going to download this file right here.
112

113

00:06:52,940  -->  00:06:56,270
Now this is a direct object reference
113

114

00:06:56,370  -->  00:06:59,460
to this file to the file. That is number four dot txt.
114

115

00:06:59,470  -->  00:07:03,530
But this is not a bug because this file does belong to us.
115

116

00:07:03,710  -->  00:07:08,710
But if you're able to download this file as another user or without logging in
116

117

00:07:08,940  -->  00:07:12,950
then that will add the insecure part to the direct object reference,
117

118

00:07:12,960  -->  00:07:17,020
which will make it an access control bug because the web application
118

119

00:07:17,020  -->  00:07:21,760
should check if you actually are authorized to downloading this file.
119

120

00:07:22,640  -->  00:07:24,110
Now we can also play with this.
120

121

00:07:24,110  -->  00:07:27,310
So as I mentioned earlier, the first file was number two.
121

122

00:07:27,320  -->  00:07:31,470
The second file was number three and the third file was number four
122

123

00:07:31,570  -->  00:07:35,260
but we actually never downloaded a file that was called one dot txt.
123

124

00:07:35,640  -->  00:07:39,250
So let's see if there was another user that had a chat before us
124

125

00:07:39,350  -->  00:07:42,060
and got the file that was called one dot txt.
125

126

00:07:42,840  -->  00:07:47,660
So modified that to one in here, just in the plain text. We're gonna click on forward
126

127

00:07:48,040  -->  00:07:48,790
Forward
127

128

00:07:48,960  -->  00:07:51,850
and we're going to modify it again here to number one.
128

129

00:07:52,740  -->  00:07:54,550
And let's forward it again.
129

130

00:07:54,880  -->  00:07:59,230
And perfect as you can see now we downloaded a file that is called one dot txt.
130

131

00:07:59,240  -->  00:08:04,340
So we successfully managed to download the file that belongs to another user.
131

132

00:08:04,680  -->  00:08:07,310
Now, regardless of the content of this file
132

133

00:08:07,450  -->  00:08:08,140
right now,
133

134

00:08:08,140  -->  00:08:12,660
we have an insecure direct object reference because we are able to download
134

135

00:08:12,670  -->  00:08:16,870
a file that does not belong to us that belongs to another user.
135

136

00:08:16,990  -->  00:08:19,630
And we were able to know the path of this
136

137

00:08:19,630  -->  00:08:22,600
file by simply looking at the requests in here.
137

138

00:08:22,860  -->  00:08:24,990
So in a secure web application,
138

139

00:08:25,780  -->  00:08:27,380
applications should have checked.
139

140

00:08:27,390  -->  00:08:31,670
Do we own this file and only display it to us if we actually own it.
140

141

00:08:31,920  -->  00:08:34,520
But now we're able to get someone else's file
141

142

00:08:34,750  -->  00:08:37,409
and if I open this file, you'll actually see
142

143

00:08:37,630  -->  00:08:39,840
this file actually contains the password.
143

144

00:08:39,960  -->  00:08:43,970
But even if it didn't, like I said, regardless of the content of the file,
144

145

00:08:43,980  -->  00:08:45,060
you have discovered
145

146

00:08:45,200  -->  00:08:48,960
an ID or vulnerability because you're able to access files
146

147

00:08:49,240  -->  00:08:50,160
directly
147

148

00:08:50,300  -->  00:08:52,150
using their file path
148

149

00:08:52,640  -->  00:08:55,400
and therefore you should definitely include this in your pen, test,
149

150

00:08:55,400  -->  00:08:57,010
report or submit a book.
150

151

00:08:57,020  -->  00:08:59,960
If you are participating in a bug, bounty program

0
1

00:00:00,110  -->  00:00:05,260
so far all of the broken access control vulnerabilities or bugs that we covered
1

2

00:00:05,400  -->  00:00:10,160
allowed us to access information or data beyond our limits.
2

3

00:00:10,530  -->  00:00:13,860
But as you can see in this definition we said broken
3

4

00:00:13,860  -->  00:00:18,160
access control can allow us to access or modify information.
4

5

00:00:18,640  -->  00:00:21,960
So in this lecture I'm going to show you an example of a broken
5

6

00:00:21,960  -->  00:00:24,520
access control vulnerability where we're gonna be
6

7

00:00:24,530  -->  00:00:28,100
able to modify information beyond our limits.
7

8

00:00:28,240  -->  00:00:34,470
And as we do that I'm gonna introduce you to the repeater functionality of Burp suite
8

9

00:00:34,480  -->  00:00:40,360
so that you can use it in any other situation where you need such functionality.
9

10

00:00:40,940  -->  00:00:45,310
So I have the target website in here as usually want to test everything and the
10

11

00:00:45,310  -->  00:00:47,520
link is in the resources and this website
11

12

00:00:47,520  -->  00:00:49,810
is pretty similar to everything we've been testing
12

13

00:00:49,970  -->  00:00:56,080
and usually once we log in with the user name winner and the password peter as usual
13

14

00:00:56,320  -->  00:01:01,850
we get this input box that allow us to change or update our email
14

15

00:01:02,040  -->  00:01:05,370
but I never actually tested this. So as we mentioned.
15

16

00:01:05,370  -->  00:01:09,160
And as I'm going to show you later on when I get to my hacking methodology,
16

17

00:01:09,290  -->  00:01:11,620
you're gonna have to press every button and test
17

18

00:01:11,620  -->  00:01:14,850
every single functionality offered by the target website.
18

19

00:01:15,440  -->  00:01:19,590
So let's go ahead and test it normally as a normal user assuming
19

20

00:01:19,590  -->  00:01:22,760
we want to change our email and to do so we're just gonna type
20

21

00:01:22,760  -->  00:01:26,270
test at test dot com just for example we want to change our
21

22

00:01:26,270  -->  00:01:31,250
email to that our current email is this And if I collect an update
22

23

00:01:31,940  -->  00:01:33,570
as you can see. Perfect.
23

24

00:01:33,570  -->  00:01:38,660
My user name is still the same but my email changed to test at test dot com.
24

25

00:01:39,340  -->  00:01:40,870
Now this is an input box.
25

26

00:01:40,910  -->  00:01:45,900
And as mentioned before, input boxes can be used to send data to the web application.
26

27

00:01:45,950  -->  00:01:48,710
And a great example is this one right here because
27

28

00:01:48,710  -->  00:01:51,430
once we typed our email test at test dot com,
28

29

00:01:51,570  -->  00:01:53,860
this email was sent to the web application.
29

30

00:01:54,000  -->  00:01:57,780
And based on this input, the web application executed code
30

31

00:01:57,940  -->  00:02:02,730
to change our email to the email that we inputted in the input box.
31

32

00:02:02,790  -->  00:02:06,870
And therefore we can use this box to manipulate the behavior of
32

33

00:02:06,870  -->  00:02:10,750
the web application and possibly discover box in it and exploit them.
33

34

00:02:11,840  -->  00:02:14,900
So every single book that you're gonna learn later on in the course,
34

35

00:02:14,900  -->  00:02:17,440
you should try to test for it in this input box.
35

36

00:02:17,600  -->  00:02:21,150
But right now we are talking about broken access control.
36

37

00:02:21,160  -->  00:02:23,690
So let's try to understand how this works,
37

38

00:02:23,700  -->  00:02:27,200
analyze it and see if we can discover any bugs in it.
38

39

00:02:27,320  -->  00:02:33,560
And a great way of doing this is using the repeater functionality of Burp suite.
39

40

00:02:34,040  -->  00:02:38,100
So we're familiar with the proxy and right now I'm already in the proxy window,
40

41

00:02:38,110  -->  00:02:42,160
I'm gonna turn the proxy on and I'm gonna put another email.
41

42

00:02:42,160  -->  00:02:46,550
So let's say test to at test dot com
42

43

00:02:47,040  -->  00:02:49,350
and we're going to click on update the email.
43

44

00:02:49,490  -->  00:02:51,750
We're gonna intercept the request in here.
44

45

00:02:51,900  -->  00:02:53,960
And as you can see it's a post request.
45

46

00:02:54,070  -->  00:02:56,920
It's being sent to this path right here.
46

47

00:02:57,100  -->  00:03:01,760
And if we look down in the body parameters we can see a value of an email
47

48

00:03:01,870  -->  00:03:05,360
and the value is what we inputted in this input box.
48

49

00:03:05,940  -->  00:03:09,700
So you can test for all of the vulnerabilities that you will learn later
49

50

00:03:09,700  -->  00:03:13,780
on and everything that you learned so far by inputting data in this box,
50

51

00:03:13,780  -->  00:03:18,550
click and update, intercepting it in here and modifying this value in here.
51

52

00:03:18,560  -->  00:03:25,230
Or you can actually right click this request and click on send to repeater.
52

53

00:03:26,040  -->  00:03:30,240
Once you do that you'll see now the repeater is highlighted in orange
53

54

00:03:30,360  -->  00:03:32,250
and if I click on the repeater
54

55

00:03:32,740  -->  00:03:36,060
I have the exact same request that we had before.
55

56

00:03:36,840  -->  00:03:41,160
And if we scroll down we have the same values that we inputted in here.
56

57

00:03:42,040  -->  00:03:45,900
You can click on the inspector similar to what we had in the proxy to
57

58

00:03:45,900  -->  00:03:49,860
browse and edit this data based on the categories that you have in here.
58

59

00:03:50,460  -->  00:03:53,160
And once you click on sand
59

60

00:03:53,640  -->  00:03:56,200
this request is going to be sent and you'll
60

61

00:03:56,200  -->  00:03:59,990
be able to see the response that this request triggered
61

62

00:04:00,150  -->  00:04:01,620
right here at the bottom.
62

63

00:04:02,010  -->  00:04:05,060
Therefore you can modify this request as you
63

64

00:04:05,060  -->  00:04:08,360
wish right here or you can use the inspector
64

65

00:04:08,360  -->  00:04:12,490
in here and modify it similar to what we did when we were using the proxy.
65

66

00:04:12,740  -->  00:04:16,420
But the nice thing is once you modify this request you
66

67

00:04:16,420  -->  00:04:20,490
can click consent and you'll instantly see the response in here.
67

68

00:04:20,500  -->  00:04:25,210
So you won't have to always submit the data using the target website
68

69

00:04:25,240  -->  00:04:29,000
intercepted in the proxy and then edit it and then forward it.
69

70

00:04:29,010  -->  00:04:32,710
You can use the repeater to quickly test for a large
70

71

00:04:32,710  -->  00:04:35,740
number of vulnerabilities because as we go through the course,
71

72

00:04:35,750  -->  00:04:38,100
I'm going to teach you so many different vulnerabilities.
72

73

00:04:38,130  -->  00:04:42,550
And every time you see data being sent to the target such as this
73

74

00:04:42,550  -->  00:04:47,900
data right here or if it's being sent in the request parameters in here,
74

75

00:04:47,910  -->  00:04:51,560
you want to modify this data and test for all of these vulnerabilities.
75

76

00:04:51,740  -->  00:04:56,360
So with the repeater you can do this pretty quickly and analyze the response.
76

77

00:04:57,040  -->  00:05:01,320
Another benefit of using the repeater is that sometimes when you load
77

78

00:05:01,320  -->  00:05:04,100
the website or when you click on update or submit a form,
78

79

00:05:04,160  -->  00:05:06,510
a number of requests are sent.
79

80

00:05:06,590  -->  00:05:11,610
So you'd want to test every single request and therefore you can split them,
80

81

00:05:11,620  -->  00:05:12,850
send them to the repeater.
81

82

00:05:12,850  -->  00:05:15,380
Like so analyze the results and see if
82

83

00:05:15,380  -->  00:05:18,660
you can use it to discover any vulnerabilities.
83

84

00:05:19,440  -->  00:05:22,710
Now once we send a post request to this path
84

85

00:05:22,890  -->  00:05:26,360
and the value of the request is a new email we
85

86

00:05:26,360  -->  00:05:30,490
get a response of 300 which is a redirection response.
86

87

00:05:30,500  -->  00:05:33,310
If you remember when we spoke about the responses earlier
87

88

00:05:33,470  -->  00:05:36,960
and we're being redirected to my account to the account page
88

89

00:05:37,340  -->  00:05:42,350
and the result you can see the user name which we can actually see on our user name.
89

90

You can see the new email that we requested in our request.
90

91

00:05:46,290  -->  00:05:51,190
You can see the api key but this is our api key. So this is not really
91

92

00:05:51,290  -->  00:05:54,460
an information leak or an information disclosure.
92

93

00:05:54,840  -->  00:05:57,750
And you can also see the role I. D.
93

94

00:05:58,240  -->  00:06:03,850
So this is probably my permissions on this website or my role on this website.
94

95

00:06:04,540  -->  00:06:07,670
Now again this is another example where the
95

96

00:06:07,670  -->  00:06:11,010
hacker or bug hunter mentality comes into play.
96

97

00:06:11,020  -->  00:06:16,420
It's very important to pause when you see information like this and just go back
97

98

00:06:16,420  -->  00:06:20,830
to the website and think what does this form allow the user to do?
98

99

00:06:20,880  -->  00:06:25,460
And the answer is very simple. It allows the user to update their email.
99

100

00:06:25,640  -->  00:06:30,830
Now what is the email? The email is part of the user information.
100

101

00:06:31,160  -->  00:06:32,380
So this form
101

102

00:06:32,490  -->  00:06:36,610
allows the user to update their user information and we can
102

103

00:06:36,610  -->  00:06:40,620
see once we submit the new information which is the new email
103

104

00:06:40,680  -->  00:06:45,460
we get a response with all of the user information including the
104

105

00:06:45,460  -->  00:06:50,150
email which is the specific information that we wanted to modify.
105

106

00:06:50,940  -->  00:06:54,760
So let's try to use the same form and the same request
106

107

00:06:54,930  -->  00:06:57,690
to update another piece of information.
107

108

00:06:57,720  -->  00:07:03,150
And for example let's try to change the role I. D. To a different role I. D.
108

109

00:07:03,640  -->  00:07:05,460
We're gonna copy what we have in here
109

110

00:07:05,940  -->  00:07:07,560
and we're going to use the same format.
110

111

00:07:07,560  -->  00:07:11,460
So we're gonna put a coma in our request a new line
111

112

00:07:12,240  -->  00:07:14,170
and we're gonna paste what we copied
112

113

00:07:14,510  -->  00:07:17,790
and our character all idea is one as you can see in here.
113

114

00:07:18,040  -->  00:07:20,060
Therefore we're going to set it to two
114

115

00:07:20,840  -->  00:07:22,960
we're going to click on send to send it.
115

116

00:07:23,540  -->  00:07:26,350
And as you can see in the response we're not getting
116

117

00:07:26,500  -->  00:07:30,090
a 400. We're not getting anything that is given us an error.
117

118

00:07:30,260  -->  00:07:33,030
We're actually getting a 300 meaning a redirect
118

119

00:07:33,030  -->  00:07:35,670
to the my account with the new information.
119

120

00:07:35,870  -->  00:07:39,460
So the email is still the same because we actually didn't modify the email.
120

121

00:07:39,640  -->  00:07:43,060
But now my role I. D. has changed too.
121

122

00:07:43,440  -->  00:07:44,280
So that's perfect.
122

123

00:07:44,280  -->  00:07:50,440
That means I managed to modify my user information in here and change my role I. D.
123

124

00:07:50,460  -->  00:07:54,660
Even though this form is not designed to allow me to change that
124

125

00:07:55,570  -->  00:07:58,830
now let's go ahead to my proxy and turn off the interceptor
125

126

00:07:59,030  -->  00:08:01,250
and let's refresh this page.
126

127

00:08:02,640  -->  00:08:05,860
And as you can see now I actually have access to the
127

128

00:08:05,860  -->  00:08:11,110
admin panel because I changed my privileges to a higher privileged user.
128

129

00:08:11,120  -->  00:08:13,500
So I changed my role idea to number two.
129

130

00:08:13,700  -->  00:08:16,700
And it looks like in this web application role I. D.
130

131

00:08:16,700  -->  00:08:21,220
Number two gives you admin privileges. So again this is not concrete.
131

132

00:08:21,220  -->  00:08:24,260
This is not always the way but you want to look at your
132

133

00:08:24,440  -->  00:08:29,150
target web application and manipulate its values based on what you see.
133

134

00:08:29,160  -->  00:08:30,740
So we saw that we have roll I. D.
134

135

00:08:30,740  -->  00:08:34,049
Number one and usually I would actually try to set that to
135

136

00:08:34,049  -->  00:08:37,679
zero first and if that didn't work I would try to 34.
136

137

00:08:37,710  -->  00:08:42,860
If I saw I I would call it be if I saw a normal user I will try to change my role I. D.
137

138

00:08:42,860  -->  00:08:43,580
To admin.
138

139

00:08:43,590  -->  00:08:47,750
So you want to see how the web application is working and
139

140

00:08:47,750  -->  00:08:52,360
modify it within the same constraints or within the same methodology.
140

141

00:08:53,040  -->  00:08:56,570
So let's click on the admin panel and see if this actually works.
141

142

00:08:56,570  -->  00:09:00,830
And as you can see we managed to get access to the admin page.
142

143

00:09:00,840  -->  00:09:05,160
So this is obviously an example of broken access control.
143

144

00:09:06,140  -->  00:09:10,060
Now you don't necessarily have to use the repeater to do this.
144

145

00:09:10,070  -->  00:09:14,210
But I wanted to introduce you to it because it is very useful and
145

146

00:09:14,210  -->  00:09:17,980
we will be using it in the future when we're testing for vulnerabilities.
146

147

00:09:17,980  -->  00:09:20,230
When you want to run a number of tests,
147

148

00:09:20,240  -->  00:09:25,070
you want to be able to modify the requests quickly and study the response
148

149

00:09:25,080  -->  00:09:30,560
to see if that would help you to discover other bugs and vulnerabilities.

in this class.

I'm going to show you an example of

3

4

or in my opinion broken access control which will

5

5

00:00:15,340  -->  00:00:19,340
as we do that. I'm also going to introduce you to the H T. T. P.
6

7

00:00:19,350  -->  00:00:21,810
Trace method and see how useful it
7

8

00:00:21,810  -->  00:00:25,520
is in understanding how the target application works
8

9

00:00:25,530  -->  00:00:29,950
and how it can help us with exploiting it and discovering bugs within it.
9

10

00:00:30,740  -->  00:00:33,600
So we have a normal application here. As usual.
10

11

00:00:33,600  -->  00:00:35,860
The link is in the resources and you
11

12

00:00:35,860  -->  00:00:38,380
want to test every single functionality within it.
12

13

00:00:38,390  -->  00:00:42,550
But I know the bug is actually in the admin page.
13

14

00:00:42,560  -->  00:00:45,680
So if we go to the admin page you will see you again
14

15

00:00:45,690  -->  00:00:51,650
an informative air message saying the interface is only available to local users.
15

16

00:00:51,940  -->  00:00:55,000
Even if you didn't get an informative error message like this.
16

17

00:00:55,000  -->  00:00:57,970
And the admin page just simply didn't even load didn't
17

18

00:00:57,970  -->  00:01:00,260
even ask you for a user name and a password
18

19

00:01:00,560  -->  00:01:04,800
that should trigger your curiosity or your bug hunter or
19

20

00:01:04,800  -->  00:01:08,560
hacker mentality that we spoke about because we want to understand
20

21

00:01:08,740  -->  00:01:12,360
how is it knowing that I'm not the admin and it's not letting me see anything
21

22

00:01:13,240  -->  00:01:18,750
to try to understand how this page works. We're going to turn on our interceptor
22

23

00:01:18,890  -->  00:01:21,160
and we're going to load the admin page again.
23

24

00:01:21,940  -->  00:01:24,520
And when we do that we can see we have a simple get
24

25

00:01:24,520  -->  00:01:27,960
request to the path that we are requesting which is the admin.
25

26

00:01:28,340  -->  00:01:31,180
If you look at the values being sent, everything is fine.
26

27

00:01:31,250  -->  00:01:33,930
We'll look at the cookies and there's only a session cookie.
27

28

00:01:33,930  -->  00:01:35,120
So there's nothing weird.
28

29

00:01:35,230  -->  00:01:39,040
We'll look at the body parameters in here and there is nothing as well.
29

30

00:01:39,050  -->  00:01:44,940
So there's nothing that is being sent that would allow do have application to know
30

31

00:01:44,940  -->  00:01:49,460
that I'm not admin and to know that it shouldn't even show me login boxes
31

32

00:01:50,240  -->  00:01:51,200
in such scenarios.
32

33

00:01:51,200  -->  00:01:53,980
I'd usually forward the packet to see if there is more data
33

34

00:01:53,980  -->  00:01:56,940
being sent and sure enough there is more data being sent.
34

35

00:01:56,990  -->  00:02:01,160
But again if you analyze this data you will not see anything useful.
35

36

00:02:01,740  -->  00:02:05,120
So we forward it again and that's it simply do have
36

37

00:02:05,120  -->  00:02:08,460
applications saying I'm not gonna load for you because you're not admin
37

38

00:02:09,039  -->  00:02:13,730
in that case the next thing I would do is try to send the same request.
38

39

00:02:13,740  -->  00:02:16,970
But instead of get so let me just send it now.
39

40

00:02:17,180  -->  00:02:21,000
Instead of getting here I would type trace
40

41

00:02:22,140  -->  00:02:25,070
so so far we saw post and get as
41

42

00:02:25,070  -->  00:02:28,750
http methods to request paths within the target website.
42

43

00:02:28,940  -->  00:02:32,960
And another http method that we can use is the trace method.
43

44

00:02:33,640  -->  00:02:37,630
The trace method is really useful in such scenarios because it
44

45

00:02:37,630  -->  00:02:42,250
helps us debug or understand how the target web application works
45

46

00:02:43,140  -->  00:02:45,120
because when we send a trace
46

47

00:02:45,290  -->  00:02:47,000
to the target web server,
47

48

00:02:47,200  -->  00:02:50,930
the target web server will response by simply sending
48

49

00:02:50,930  -->  00:02:54,060
us back the request that we sent to it.
49

50

00:02:54,640  -->  00:02:59,680
This is very very useful because it lets us understand whether the
50

51

00:02:59,680  -->  00:03:04,660
data that we sent to the server got modified at some stage.
51

52

00:03:05,340  -->  00:03:10,140
So in some cases you send a request like so to the target server.
52

53

00:03:10,280  -->  00:03:13,570
But that request will pass through a number of
53

54

00:03:13,570  -->  00:03:17,460
points or nodes before it reaches the target server.
54

55

00:03:17,840  -->  00:03:23,370
And these points or notes can actually modify the original request that you sent.
55

56

00:03:23,440  -->  00:03:28,320
For example you started with this request but the end result that the
56

57

00:03:28,320  -->  00:03:33,760
target web server receives could possibly contain extra headers and extra data.
57

58

00:03:34,440  -->  00:03:37,120
And the target web server might use this
58

59

00:03:37,120  -->  00:03:40,560
data to identify you or treat you differently.
59

60

00:03:41,340  -->  00:03:47,000
Therefore when we use the trace method the web server is going to send us back the
60

61

00:03:47,000  -->  00:03:50,260
request that it received after it got modified
61

62

00:03:50,270  -->  00:03:53,520
along with the modified headers and the modified data
62

63

00:03:53,700  -->  00:04:00,660
and will be able to read the request and compared to the original request that we sent
63

64

00:04:00,840  -->  00:04:05,960
and see if there is any extra headers and if they are useful in any way.
64

65

00:04:06,940  -->  00:04:10,670
So we're gonna go back to our example and we already modified the
65

66

00:04:10,670  -->  00:04:15,160
original Get request and now we're sending a trace request to the admin.
66

67

00:04:15,740  -->  00:04:17,320
We're going to forward the request
67

68

00:04:17,740  -->  00:04:21,350
And perfect as you can see we managed to download a text file
68

69

00:04:22,040  -->  00:04:23,860
and if we open this text file
69

70

00:04:24,940  -->  00:04:31,190
you will notice here at the bottom we actually have an extra header called X custom I.
70

71

00:04:31,190  -->  00:04:32,640
P authorization.
71

72

00:04:32,820  -->  00:04:37,440
So the original request that we sent contained all of this data
72

73

00:04:37,610  -->  00:04:41,970
but somehow along the way this particular header
73

74

00:04:41,980  -->  00:04:45,160
was modified and added to the target web server
74

75

00:04:45,780  -->  00:04:49,560
and the I. P. That you see in here is actually my I. P.
75

76

00:04:50,000  -->  00:04:53,750
So I can simply just go to google and type my I. P.
76

77

00:04:54,940  -->  00:04:57,290
And sure enough it is the same I. P.
77

78

00:04:57,400  -->  00:04:59,460
That is being used to the web server.
78

79

00:05:00,040  -->  00:05:02,310
So looking at this again and employing your
79

80

00:05:02,310  -->  00:05:05,120
hacker or bug hunter mentality you can see
80

81

00:05:05,130  -->  00:05:09,490
that somewhere along the line this header is being added along with your own I.
81

82

00:05:09,490  -->  00:05:09,890
P.
82

83

00:05:10,010  -->  00:05:12,410
And then the admin pages not loading.
83

84

00:05:12,940  -->  00:05:17,060
Therefore the chances are the admin pages not loading for you
84

85

00:05:17,070  -->  00:05:20,460
because the web server knows that you're not the admin.
85

86

00:05:21,140  -->  00:05:25,110
So you should either try to put the admins I. Pee in here.
86

87

00:05:25,110  -->  00:05:28,060
So you're gonna have to try somehow to find the admins I. P.
87

88

00:05:28,070  -->  00:05:33,480
Or you should simply replace this with a reference to the local I. P.
88

89

00:05:33,510  -->  00:05:42,860
So 127001 is one way of referencing the local host or the local I. P. Of a computer.
89

90

00:05:43,640  -->  00:05:46,860
So right now we're just trying we're doing a trial and error.
90

91

00:05:46,860  -->  00:05:50,020
We're not sure if this is gonna work. This is an educated guess.
91

92

00:05:50,240  -->  00:05:53,280
And what we're trying to do is we're trying to send this header
92

93

00:05:53,390  -->  00:05:57,310
and saying that we are a local user on the target computer.
93

94

00:05:57,440  -->  00:06:01,870
And hopefully based on this information the web application is going to think that
94

95

00:06:01,870  -->  00:06:06,050
we are the admin and we should be allowed to see the admin page
95

96

00:06:06,940  -->  00:06:09,600
now to send this. We're gonna copy all of this.
96

97

00:06:09,610  -->  00:06:14,100
We're gonna close the page and I'm gonna turn off the interceptor for now.
97

98

00:06:14,130  -->  00:06:15,850
We're gonna go to the options
98

99

00:06:16,340  -->  00:06:19,550
and we're gonna use the much and replaced feature.
99

100

00:06:19,940  -->  00:06:22,410
So previously you saw how we can use it too
100

101

00:06:22,410  -->  00:06:25,400
much a certain string and replace it with something else.
101

102

00:06:25,840  -->  00:06:29,710
But right now we don't want to match anything. We actually just want to add
102

103

00:06:29,880  -->  00:06:31,620
this text that we just copied
103

104

00:06:31,780  -->  00:06:33,550
to every single request.
104

105

00:06:33,940  -->  00:06:35,260
So we're going to click on add
105

106

00:06:35,640  -->  00:06:38,040
and we're going to leave the match empty
106

107

00:06:38,190  -->  00:06:40,650
and we're simply just going to fill whatever we
107

108

00:06:40,650  -->  00:06:43,760
want to add to the requests in the replace part
108

109

00:06:44,140  -->  00:06:48,160
we're gonna click on. Ok. And we're gonna make sure that it is ticked
109

110

00:06:48,740  -->  00:06:49,750
and that's it. Now.
110

111

00:06:49,750  -->  00:06:53,830
We don't even need to have the interceptor on as I showed before because
111

112

00:06:54,020  -->  00:06:58,250
is automatically going to add this rule to every packet that goes through it.
112

113

00:06:59,140  -->  00:07:03,160
So we're simply just gonna go and load the admin page again.
113

114

00:07:03,540  -->  00:07:06,410
And perfect as you can see the admin page
114

115

00:07:06,410  -->  00:07:10,110
loads for us because now the web application thinks
115

116

00:07:10,110  -->  00:07:13,090
that we are coming from the local computer from
116

117

00:07:13,090  -->  00:07:16,760
the local host because we're adding this extra header
117

118

00:07:17,140  -->  00:07:19,960
to every single request that is being sent.
118

119

00:07:20,340  -->  00:07:21,060
And
119

120

00:07:21,840  -->  00:07:24,180
In this header we're saying that we are
120

121

00:07:24,180  -->  00:07:28,100
coming from the local computer because 127001 is
121

122

00:07:28,100  -->  00:07:30,080
a reference to the local computer to the
122

123

00:07:30,080  -->  00:07:32,620
local host and therefore the web applications,
123

124

00:07:32,620  -->  00:07:35,850
thinking that we should have access to this page.
124

125

00:07:37,140  -->  00:07:39,730
Now again, as usual, once you're done with this,
125

126

00:07:39,740  -->  00:07:43,500
you want to make sure that you either intake your rule or even remove it
126

127

00:07:43,510  -->  00:07:47,660
because you don't want it to be applied to every single request that gets sent.
127

128

00:07:48,740  -->  00:07:52,550
Now this is one example of where the trace method is useful,
128

129

00:07:52,560  -->  00:07:55,690
but you can use it in any other scenario
129

130

00:07:55,700  -->  00:07:59,140
when the web application is behaving in an unknown manner
130

131

00:07:59,150  -->  00:08:01,740
and you want to debug that further and have a
131

132

00:08:01,740  -->  00:08:05,150
better understanding of how the swab application is working.

# Section 4: Path  Directory Traversal Vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:04,570
In today's class, we're gonna talk about directory or path traversal.
1

2

00:00:04,590  -->  00:00:04,920
Now,
2

3

00:00:04,920  -->  00:00:06,960
we already covered broken access control
3

4

00:00:06,960  -->  00:00:09,460
vulnerabilities previously and we said that
4

5

00:00:09,460  -->  00:00:15,020
94% of websites according to China 10 are vulnerable to these vulnerabilities.
5

6

00:00:15,030  -->  00:00:18,550
So if you're doing bug hunting our pen testing,
6

7

00:00:18,560  -->  00:00:21,950
then your chances of finding these vulnerabilities are really, really high.
7

8

00:00:22,300  -->  00:00:26,340
And we said in the previous class, when we spoke about broken access control,
8

9

00:00:26,350  -->  00:00:29,040
the definition of it is that they allow us
9

10

00:00:29,040  -->  00:00:32,570
to access or modify information beyond our limits.
10

11

00:00:32,580  -->  00:00:34,860
That's the main thing. That is the definition.
11

12

00:00:34,870  -->  00:00:39,240
So if you find any bug that allows you to modify information beyond your limits,
12

13

00:00:39,250  -->  00:00:41,880
then it falls under broken access control.
13

14

00:00:42,340  -->  00:00:43,330
As you can see,
14

15

00:00:43,340  -->  00:00:47,190
the term is very broad and a lot of other subcategories
15

16

00:00:47,200  -->  00:00:51,210
or sub bugs or vulnerabilities can fall under this category.
16

17

00:00:51,300  -->  00:00:55,870
And an example of that is directory or path traversal.
17

18

00:00:56,470  -->  00:00:59,370
So the idea of these bugs or vulnerabilities,
18

19

00:00:59,370  -->  00:01:03,450
they basically allow you to access paths or locations on
19

20

00:01:03,450  -->  00:01:06,810
the target web server that you're not allowed to access.
20

21

00:01:06,820  -->  00:01:08,950
So we said broken access control,
21

22

00:01:08,950  -->  00:01:11,960
allow you to access or modify information beyond your limits.
22

23

00:01:12,290  -->  00:01:17,690
And directory or path traversal allow you to access paths or files that
23

24

00:01:17,700  -->  00:01:21,250
are beyond your limits that you're not supposed to access or see.
24

25

00:01:21,730  -->  00:01:24,580
So zooming in on a file system in Linux,
25

26

00:01:24,810  -->  00:01:28,100
basically you have the forward slash literally just
26

27

00:01:28,100  -->  00:01:30,290
a forward slash with no text after it.
27

28

00:01:30,340  -->  00:01:34,640
That is your root or base directory in your file system
28

29

00:01:34,820  -->  00:01:38,600
and then inside that you would have a number of directories, for example,
29

30

00:01:38,600  -->  00:01:42,210
you would have via, you would have home where you have the user data,
30

31

00:01:42,500  -->  00:01:45,080
you would have E T C where you would have the
31

32

00:01:45,080  -->  00:01:49,120
configuration files for the various programs installed on the system.
32

33

00:01:49,130  -->  00:01:52,460
And you would have obviously a lot of other directories and then within them
33

34

00:01:52,460  -->  00:01:55,680
you can have other subdirectories and you can even create your own directories.
34

35

00:01:55,680  -->  00:01:57,730
You've all seen that and we've all done that.
35

36

00:01:58,370  -->  00:01:58,630
Now.
36

37

00:01:58,630  -->  00:02:02,410
When you install a web server on a server or a computer,
37

38

00:02:02,420  -->  00:02:07,660
you basically define a certain path where the website files will be stored.
38

39

00:02:07,830  -->  00:02:12,750
So that path is usually var www. And sometimes you would have html in it.
39

40

00:02:12,750  -->  00:02:15,860
Sometimes you would have the user name in here somewhere.
40

41

00:02:15,870  -->  00:02:20,460
But the main idea is you would define a path within your file system so
41

42

00:02:20,460  -->  00:02:25,920
that it contains the files of the website and when users access to web site,
42

43

00:02:25,930  -->  00:02:26,740
they get served.
43

44

00:02:26,740  -->  00:02:30,330
Files that are included in that directory.
44

45

00:02:30,860  -->  00:02:34,760
So this directory would be called a web root. It can actually be anywhere.
45

46

00:02:34,770  -->  00:02:36,420
It doesn't have to be in this path.
46

47

00:02:36,430  -->  00:02:39,790
But this is one of the default paths where the web root is.
47

48

00:02:40,580  -->  00:02:43,530
So this path will contain the website files
48

49

00:02:43,530  -->  00:02:46,310
and when someone accesses your website for example
49

50

00:02:46,450  -->  00:02:48,610
dot com or the security dot com.
50

51

00:02:48,880  -->  00:02:53,060
The files included in this directory will be served to the user
51

52

00:02:53,530  -->  00:02:53,850
now.
52

53

00:02:53,850  -->  00:02:58,870
Directory or path traversal vulnerabilities allow us to break out of
53

54

00:02:58,880  -->  00:03:02,770
this path and load files from other locations on the server
54

55

00:03:02,980  -->  00:03:08,320
or maybe even load files within this server but you're not allowed to see.
55

56

00:03:08,320  -->  00:03:11,850
So maybe load source code or sensitive files
56

57

00:03:11,860  -->  00:03:14,950
that are not directly visible through the U.
57

58

00:03:14,950  -->  00:03:18,250
R. L. Bar through basically just putting the website location.
58

59

00:03:19,370  -->  00:03:21,590
So let's go ahead with a simple example.
59

60

00:03:22,250  -->  00:03:24,770
We have a normal website as usual.
60

61

00:03:24,770  -->  00:03:28,320
And you should test every single functionality as usual as I say.
61

62

00:03:28,330  -->  00:03:31,650
But we're gonna go ahead and load a product because
62

63

00:03:31,650  -->  00:03:34,640
I know that is what's vulnerable within the website.
63

64

00:03:36,200  -->  00:03:38,130
And as you can see you get a product image,
64

65

00:03:38,130  -->  00:03:40,910
the description and a back button and as usual you want
65

66

00:03:40,910  -->  00:03:43,280
to test everything whatever parameters you have in the U.
66

67

00:03:43,280  -->  00:03:44,640
R. L. And all of that.
67

68

00:03:44,650  -->  00:03:50,270
And as usual we usually look at the normal behavior of the website before we break it.
68

69

00:03:50,280  -->  00:03:54,180
And then we would turn on our interceptor load the website
69

70

00:03:54,180  -->  00:03:57,740
again and start analyzing the data that is being sent.
70

71

00:03:58,460  -->  00:04:01,520
So we can see we have a product I. D. Parameter number three.
71

72

00:04:01,520  -->  00:04:04,180
Again that should be tested but I'm gonna skip it now
72

73

00:04:04,180  -->  00:04:06,740
because that's not the vulnerability that I want to show you.
73

74

00:04:06,740  -->  00:04:07,790
So I'm gonna forward
74

75

00:04:08,880  -->  00:04:12,040
and the next request that is being sent is the one that
75

76

00:04:12,040  -->  00:04:15,410
I want to focus on because that is the vulnerable one.
76

77

00:04:15,420  -->  00:04:18,680
As usual you can modify the data and here by modifying
77

78

00:04:18,690  -->  00:04:22,310
anything that comes after the equal science as we said before.
78

79

00:04:22,320  -->  00:04:26,600
Or you could just filter it here using the request attributes and
79

80

00:04:26,600  -->  00:04:30,390
the request parameters and double collecting here and modify the data.
80

81

00:04:31,180  -->  00:04:34,810
So I prefer to do it here directly in plain text.
81

82

00:04:34,820  -->  00:04:39,440
And if you look in here you'll notice that you have a
82

83

00:04:39,440  -->  00:04:42,670
request that is being sent to an end point called image.
