00:07:59,000  -->  00:08:01,780
in the pro version of Perp you can simply
125

126

00:08:01,960  -->  00:08:04,520
load a preloaded payload list.
126

127

00:08:04,600  -->  00:08:07,720
So in here in the fuzzing in the path reversal,
127

128

00:08:07,730  -->  00:08:12,470
if I click on that you can see it will automatically fill it with a large number.
128

129

00:08:12,470  -->  00:08:14,500
It's actually much bigger than the cheat sheet that I
129

130

00:08:14,955  -->  00:08:19,005
2200 of possible path reversal payloads.
130

131

00:08:19,015  -->  00:08:23,285
Again, all you have to do is click on start attack and it will start the attack for you.
131

132

00:08:23,335  -->  00:08:24,625
But as you said earlier,
132

133

00:08:24,625  -->  00:08:27,625
you don't really have to have the pro you can simply use your own kitchen
133

134

00:08:27,685  -->  00:08:32,115
or the kitchen that I have right now and you'll be able to use the intruder
134

135

00:08:32,304  -->  00:08:36,385
pretty much just as good as the pro it will just be a little bit slower

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

# Section 5: CSRF - Cross-Site Request Forgery

1
00:00:00,560 --> 00:00:05,480
In this lecture, we will talk about csrf vulnerabilities.

2
00:00:05,840 --> 00:00:12,830
These vulnerabilities fall under the broken access control category and happen because the application

3
00:00:12,830 --> 00:00:16,700
does not properly validate the submitted requests.

4
00:00:16,850 --> 00:00:23,960
As a result, requests can be forged to get the users to take actions they do not want to do.

5
00:00:24,560 --> 00:00:31,820
For example, we can get a user to change their email or change their password, submit a payment or

6
00:00:31,820 --> 00:00:37,340
submit any kind of a request without them knowing that they are submitting that request.

7
00:00:37,970 --> 00:00:43,550
To test for this similar to what we do with other broken access control vulnerabilities, you want to

8
00:00:43,550 --> 00:00:46,340
create two accounts on the target website.

9
00:00:46,580 --> 00:00:53,360
Log in with one of the accounts and make a list of all of the different requests that we can submit

10
00:00:53,360 --> 00:00:55,110
as this logged in user.

11
00:00:55,130 --> 00:01:00,450
For example, you usually can change your name, your email or your password from your account page,

12
00:01:00,450 --> 00:01:04,970
or you can, for example, submit payments or comments or reviews.

13
00:01:04,980 --> 00:01:10,020
So you want to make a list of all of these requests and see if you can forge them.

14
00:01:10,020 --> 00:01:12,120
And I'm going to show you how to forge requests.

15
00:01:12,120 --> 00:01:19,650
And then we're going to log in as the other user, submit the forged requests and see if they get accepted

16
00:01:19,650 --> 00:01:21,330
by the Web application.

17
00:01:22,170 --> 00:01:25,020
So let me show you all of this in an example.

18
00:01:25,020 --> 00:01:26,970
Right here we have our target.

19
00:01:26,970 --> 00:01:29,100
I'm going to include its link in the resources.

20
00:01:29,100 --> 00:01:33,600
And as you can see, this time, you have two accounts already made for you.

21
00:01:33,600 --> 00:01:36,510
So in a real life scenario, you want to create these accounts.

22
00:01:36,510 --> 00:01:40,010
But right now, we already have these two accounts created for us.

23
00:01:40,020 --> 00:01:45,480
So we're going to log in as the first account, which is Wiener Peter, We're going to go to my account,

24
00:01:45,900 --> 00:01:48,330
put the username and the password.

25
00:01:48,900 --> 00:01:54,810
And as you can see straight away, we can see from our admin page, we can change our email.

26
00:01:54,810 --> 00:01:58,770
So first of all, let's test this functionality and make sure it works.

27
00:01:58,770 --> 00:02:05,130
So let's change our email to test at test.com hit enter and perfect.

28
00:02:05,130 --> 00:02:05,910
That works.

29
00:02:06,120 --> 00:02:13,950
So now the next step is we want to see if we can forge this request and then log in as another user

30
00:02:13,980 --> 00:02:20,190
as Carlos and submit that forged request and see if the web application accepts it.

31
00:02:20,220 --> 00:02:27,180
If it does, then that means the web application is not verifying the users that are submitting the

32
00:02:27,180 --> 00:02:34,680
requests because we're creating the request as wiener and submitting it as Carlos, but the application

33
00:02:34,680 --> 00:02:37,950
is still accepting it and still applying our changes.

34
00:02:37,950 --> 00:02:43,710
And therefore, for example, if we're able to get Carlos to change their email to an email that we

35
00:02:43,740 --> 00:02:50,580
control, for example, my email, I can go ahead and use the password recovery functionality that is

36
00:02:50,580 --> 00:02:56,460
available on most websites and recover their password, and then I'll be able to gain full control over

37
00:02:56,460 --> 00:02:57,210
their account.

38
00:02:57,210 --> 00:03:00,660
So the impact of these vulnerabilities can be very, very high.

39
00:03:00,660 --> 00:03:03,180
It can actually be account takeover.

40
00:03:03,810 --> 00:03:09,810
So the form that is executing the action that I'm interested in is this form right here.

41
00:03:09,810 --> 00:03:18,600
So before we can create forged requests, let's try to simply clone this form and copy its functionality

42
00:03:18,600 --> 00:03:21,120
in a simple HTML file.

43
00:03:21,690 --> 00:03:28,770
To do that, I'm going to right click the input box, click on Inspect, and that's going to reveal

44
00:03:28,770 --> 00:03:33,960
the HTML code that is responsible for rendering this page right here.

45
00:03:33,960 --> 00:03:40,260
And like I said before, it doesn't matter what language is used to program this web application, everything

46
00:03:40,260 --> 00:03:47,190
gets translated to HTML and JavaScript at the end of the day, and as I move my mouse over the code,

47
00:03:47,190 --> 00:03:52,800
you can see the element that is relevant to the code that I'm selecting is being highlighted on the

48
00:03:52,800 --> 00:03:53,340
left.

49
00:03:54,190 --> 00:03:57,430
So like I said, we're interested in this form right here.

50
00:03:57,430 --> 00:04:02,650
This is the form that is carrying out the functionality that we are interested in.

51
00:04:02,770 --> 00:04:09,510
So I'm going to click on the code that is responsible for rendering this element or this form.

52
00:04:09,520 --> 00:04:14,200
I'm going to right click it and I'm going to select copy element.

53
00:04:15,230 --> 00:04:18,080
Then I'm going to go to an empty text file.

54
00:04:18,080 --> 00:04:20,450
You can use any text editor that you want.

55
00:04:20,480 --> 00:04:25,040
We're going to paste the code and I'm going to save this file.

56
00:04:26,340 --> 00:04:28,920
In my local storage in a test directory.

57
00:04:28,950 --> 00:04:35,640
You can save it anywhere you want and I'm just going to call it csrf dot xHTML.

58
00:04:37,940 --> 00:04:45,120
This parameter states the endpoint or the path that the data that you input in the form goes to.

59
00:04:45,140 --> 00:04:51,860
So it states where the values that you input in here go to once you click on update email.

60
00:04:52,340 --> 00:04:56,890
And as you can see right now, it's going to go to my account change email.

61
00:04:56,900 --> 00:05:03,620
But because we stored this file locally, we have to give the full location or the full path.

62
00:05:03,620 --> 00:05:06,380
So we have to put the full website in here.

63
00:05:06,650 --> 00:05:08,460
So we're just going to go back.

64
00:05:08,480 --> 00:05:14,090
We're going to copy the website, name the target name, and we're going to paste it in here.

65
00:05:16,100 --> 00:05:23,420
I'm going to save again and I'm going to go to the location where I stored this csrf dot HTML file.

66
00:05:23,420 --> 00:05:29,810
So I stored it in a directory called test and I'm going to double click it to open it in my web browser.

67
00:05:30,140 --> 00:05:33,260
And as you can see, we get a very simple form.

68
00:05:33,260 --> 00:05:39,830
It doesn't look the same as the form that we have in the original target, but it functions the same.

69
00:05:39,830 --> 00:05:44,180
So you have the label, you have the input box, and you have the button that submits the form.

70
00:05:44,480 --> 00:05:47,600
And I'm just going to inspect this element.

71
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:54,860
And as you can see, once we click on submit, the data will be sent to the target website, which we

72
00:05:54,860 --> 00:05:55,910
have right here.

73
00:05:56,610 --> 00:06:03,190
Now, the only thing is we are trying to discover a client side request, forgery, vulnerability.

74
00:06:03,210 --> 00:06:08,270
So we logged in as one user and we created a forged request.

75
00:06:08,280 --> 00:06:15,710
But to test if this request will be accepted by the web application, we need to log out from this user,

76
00:06:15,720 --> 00:06:23,370
log in as the other user, and then submit the form and see if the web application is going to accept

77
00:06:23,370 --> 00:06:27,390
this request that we created as user number one.

78
00:06:27,420 --> 00:06:32,910
If it does, then the target is vulnerable for a client side request forgery.

79
00:06:34,290 --> 00:06:37,230
So I'm going to go to the Target website.

80
00:06:37,560 --> 00:06:39,600
I'm going to go to my account.

81
00:06:39,870 --> 00:06:47,550
I'm going to log out and I'm going to log in again with the other user, which is Carlos.

82
00:06:47,940 --> 00:06:49,440
Put the password.

83
00:06:50,270 --> 00:06:51,260
Hit enter.

84
00:06:52,490 --> 00:06:59,020
And now that I'm logged in as this user and we can see this email, we're going to see if the Web application

85
00:06:59,030 --> 00:07:04,850
accepts a forged request that we created in here using a completely different user.

86
00:07:04,850 --> 00:07:07,160
So I'm just going to put another email in here.

87
00:07:07,160 --> 00:07:12,890
So let's say Test 55 at Test.com.

88
00:07:13,100 --> 00:07:16,850
Click on update, email and perfect.

89
00:07:16,850 --> 00:07:18,230
It looks like it worked.

90
00:07:18,260 --> 00:07:22,940
Let's go back to the My account page and refresh and perfect.

91
00:07:22,940 --> 00:07:29,750
As you can see, we managed to get the user Carlos to change their email to a completely different email

92
00:07:29,780 --> 00:07:36,380
using a forged request that was created by a completely different user, which is wiener.

93
00:07:37,780 --> 00:07:44,560
Now, the thing is, you might argue that a target user will never submit a form like this, and you

94
00:07:44,560 --> 00:07:46,120
are correct in saying that.

95
00:07:46,120 --> 00:07:53,830
But there are ways to have this form in a HTML page that will automatically submit the request when

96
00:07:53,830 --> 00:07:56,210
the target user clicks on the link.

97
00:07:56,230 --> 00:08:01,660
But this goes more into website hacking and I cover that more in my website hacking course.

98
00:08:01,660 --> 00:08:05,500
But what we're talking right now is bug hunting and pen testing.

99
00:08:05,500 --> 00:08:11,800
So when you're hunting for bugs and you discover something like this, it's actually better to include

100
00:08:11,800 --> 00:08:18,010
your finding like this because it is easier for the person that is reviewing your bug to be able to

101
00:08:18,010 --> 00:08:19,840
see this form and play with it.

102
00:08:21,140 --> 00:08:27,520
Another thing that you want to look for when looking for Csrf vulnerabilities is hidden inputs.

103
00:08:27,530 --> 00:08:30,260
So as you can see right here, we have this input.

104
00:08:30,260 --> 00:08:30,950
It's hidden.

105
00:08:30,950 --> 00:08:36,740
So we didn't see it on the page and the value of it seems like a bit of a random text.

106
00:08:36,770 --> 00:08:42,410
This is called a token and it's usually used to prevent csrf vulnerabilities.

107
00:08:42,410 --> 00:08:45,170
But this is not always implemented properly.

108
00:08:45,170 --> 00:08:50,570
As you can see in this example, we were still able to use this token from a different user.

109
00:08:50,570 --> 00:08:57,740
So if you see this when you're testing, you should still try to discover Csfs Just keep in mind you

110
00:08:57,740 --> 00:09:01,370
might need to use a new token with every request.

111
00:09:01,370 --> 00:09:08,030
So just refresh the target page, get the new token, paste it in here and then test it from a different

112
00:09:08,030 --> 00:09:08,660
user.

113
00:09:08,660 --> 00:09:11,840
If that doesn't work, try to remove it completely.

114
00:09:11,840 --> 00:09:17,330
And sometimes web applications still accept your request even if you don't submit a token.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 6: OAUTH 2.0 Vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,400  -->  00:00:03,980
in this class and the next few classes we're going to focus on.
1

2

00:00:03,990  -->  00:00:05,520
O off vulnerabilities.
2

3

00:00:06,180  -->  00:00:12,150
Oh it is generally used to allow users to log into websites without having
3

4

00:00:12,150  -->  00:00:16,680
to sign up to this website and without having to fill any forms on it
4

5

00:00:17,590  -->  00:00:18,620
the way this works.
5

6

00:00:18,620  -->  00:00:23,210
Usually assuming that this is the website that the target user wants to log into.
6

7

00:00:23,610  -->  00:00:25,690
It would have a social login button,
7

8

00:00:25,690  -->  00:00:28,790
so it could be a log in with facebook or google or twitter.
8

9

00:00:28,800  -->  00:00:29,900
We've all seen them.
9

10

00:00:30,070  -->  00:00:33,160
Once the user clicks on the social login button,
10

11

00:00:33,170  -->  00:00:37,070
the website is going to display a pop up for the social network or
11

12

00:00:37,070  -->  00:00:40,800
for the website that they chose to log in with assuming it's google,
12

13

00:00:40,850  -->  00:00:44,760
they'll get a pop up window that will ask them to log into google.
13

14

00:00:45,130  -->  00:00:47,230
This window is displayed to the user.
14

15

00:00:47,490  -->  00:00:50,860
The user will then have to log into this website with
15

16

00:00:50,860  -->  00:00:54,540
their own account that they already have on this website.
16

17

00:00:54,920  -->  00:00:57,730
And if they enter the correct information,
17

18

00:00:57,940  -->  00:01:03,130
the social website will return a access token to
18

19

00:01:03,140  -->  00:01:05,319
the website that they want to log into.
19

20

00:01:05,920  -->  00:01:09,070
Then the target website or the website that they want to log
20

21

00:01:09,070  -->  00:01:14,460
into will communicate with the social website using the access token and
21

22

00:01:14,460  -->  00:01:18,500
retrieve the user data and use it on this website to set
22

23

00:01:18,500  -->  00:01:23,160
up an account or to log in the user to this website.
23

24

00:01:24,130  -->  00:01:27,830
So as a result this user did not have to fill any forms.
24

25

00:01:27,900  -->  00:01:30,690
They did not have to manually sign up to this website.
25

26

00:01:30,700  -->  00:01:35,170
All of the data is being pulled from the social website that the user
26

27

00:01:35,180  -->  00:01:41,590
already has an account on and has already authenticated to using the access token.
27

28

00:01:42,450  -->  00:01:47,290
So let me show you an example of a website that implements social login just
28

29

00:01:47,290  -->  00:01:50,810
to make it clearer and then we will talk about how to exploit it.
29

30

00:01:51,620  -->  00:01:54,290
Now you've probably used this many times yourself,
30

31

00:01:54,300  -->  00:01:58,220
you would go to a website and click on the sign up or the sign in button
31

32

00:01:59,730  -->  00:02:04,100
and instead of filling up your information in the fields that the website gives you,
32

33

00:02:04,110  -->  00:02:06,600
you would see an option to log in with google,
33

34

00:02:06,600  -->  00:02:11,840
facebook twitter or any other account that you might have on a different website.
34

35

00:02:12,380  -->  00:02:16,770
The reason why websites offer this functionality because most people are lazy
35

36

00:02:16,830  -->  00:02:20,070
and they might be turned off by the sign up process.
36

37

00:02:20,340  -->  00:02:23,350
Therefore by allowing them to sign in with an
37

38

00:02:23,350  -->  00:02:26,070
account that they already have on a different website,
38

39

00:02:26,080  -->  00:02:29,710
they would make creating an account on their website much easier.
39

40

00:02:30,430  -->  00:02:31,060
So in here.
40

41

00:02:31,060  -->  00:02:31,330
Now,
41

42

00:02:31,330  -->  00:02:34,130
instead of having to fill up this whole form we can just click
42

43

00:02:34,130  -->  00:02:38,650
on sign up with google and then sign in with my google account.
43

44

00:02:38,680  -->  00:02:43,230
So as you can see I got redirected to google to sign into my google account.
44

45

00:02:43,240  -->  00:02:45,610
I'm not signing into my website yet
45

46

00:02:46,270  -->  00:02:47,710
and once you do that
46

47

00:02:48,430  -->  00:02:51,340
you'll see that you'll get redirected back to the original
47

48

00:02:51,340  -->  00:02:54,330
website that you are trying to create an account with
48

49

00:02:54,660  -->  00:02:57,680
and it will either allow you to create an account straight away
49

50

00:02:57,850  -->  00:03:01,990
or in many cases you'll simply just sign into the website
50

51

00:03:02,580  -->  00:03:05,910
so as you can see now I can go to my profile and it already
51

52

00:03:05,910  -->  00:03:10,500
has the correct information about me like my name and my location and so on.
52

53

00:03:11,030  -->  00:03:15,350
I didn't feel any of this information in the sign up field as you've seen.
53

54

00:03:15,460  -->  00:03:18,730
But this website was able to get it all from
54

55

00:03:18,740  -->  00:03:22,720
my google account because I authenticated them and I allowed
55

56

00:03:22,720  -->  00:03:26,190
them to extract all of this information from the account
56

57

00:03:26,190  -->  00:03:29,420
that I already have with the social account on google.
57

58

00:03:30,180  -->  00:03:33,580
Now, all our vulnerabilities are very, very serious
58

59

00:03:33,710  -->  00:03:34,290
actually.
59

60

00:03:34,290  -->  00:03:38,000
Before deploying the security we carried out a pen test and
60

61

00:03:38,000  -->  00:03:40,640
we discovered one in a plug in that we were using.
61

62

00:03:40,780  -->  00:03:43,620
It was called Omni orange if I remember correctly.
62

63

00:03:43,680  -->  00:03:47,650
And that plug in was used by more than 30,000 websites.
63

64

00:03:47,800  -->  00:03:49,880
And as a result of this vulnerability,
64

65

00:03:49,920  -->  00:03:52,720
we were able to log in as any account
65

66

00:03:52,730  -->  00:03:56,150
that is registered on the website on the Web application
66

67

00:03:56,370  -->  00:03:58,950
including the administrator account.
67

68

00:03:59,150  -->  00:04:03,650
So once we discovered this vulnerability we would have been able to log in as
68

69

00:04:03,650  -->  00:04:07,520
admin to over 30,000 websites all because they
69

70

00:04:07,520  -->  00:04:10,310
were vulnerable to an o off vulnerability.
70

71

00:04:10,870  -->  00:04:15,400
So this is a very serious vulnerability when discovered it could allow any user
71

72

00:04:15,400  -->  00:04:20,100
to log in like any other user or as an administrator which would compromise.
72

73

00:04:20,100  -->  00:04:21,490
Pretty much the whole website

9

00:00:33,610  -->  00:00:38,620
CSR F happens when the requests are not validated by the server side.
9

10

00:00:38,930  -->  00:00:41,780
So when the server gets a request
10

11

00:00:41,960  -->  00:00:46,750
it doesn't validate whether the user actually created the request or if it
11

12

00:00:46,750  -->  00:00:51,540
was created by someone else and the user is just executing that request.
12

13

00:00:52,340  -->  00:00:54,950
So a possible exploitation of this,
13

14

00:00:55,050  -->  00:00:58,750
the hacker would forge a specific request that is useful to them.
14

15

00:00:58,760  -->  00:01:02,220
For example changing the user's password to a password they know
15

16

00:01:02,340  -->  00:01:05,900
and then social engineer the user to submit that request.
16

17

00:01:05,900  -->  00:01:08,830
So it could be a RL or a html page.
17

18

00:01:08,840  -->  00:01:12,020
If the user submits that request to the target website,
18

19

00:01:12,200  -->  00:01:16,460
the target website will not verify that the user actually created this request.
19

20

00:01:16,680  -->  00:01:19,390
Therefore it will execute it but it's going to
20

21

00:01:19,400  -->  00:01:22,060
execute it as if this user submitted it.
21

22

22

23

24

25

Now in this example with
26

27

# Section 7: Injection Vulnerabilities

1
00:00:00,480 --> 00:00:06,390
And the next few sections, we're going to cover various types of injection vulnerabilities, such as

2
00:00:06,390 --> 00:00:12,600
OS command injection, cross-site scripting, or access and SQL injections.

3
00:00:12,900 --> 00:00:19,650
Many people think these vulnerabilities are a thing of the past, but they're simply incorrect, according

4
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:23,400
to us, which is, like we said, is a non-profit organization.

5
00:00:23,400 --> 00:00:29,430
Injection vulnerabilities are the third most common in all of the tested websites.

6
00:00:29,460 --> 00:00:36,720
Companies like TikTok, Snapchat, Twitter and PlayStation and much more have paid thousands of dollars

7
00:00:36,720 --> 00:00:43,470
to the hunters that discovered such vulnerabilities in their platforms because of the severity of these

8
00:00:43,470 --> 00:00:44,560
vulnerabilities.

9
00:00:44,580 --> 00:00:50,490
So the people that think that these vulnerabilities are not common simply lack the skills of discovering

10
00:00:50,490 --> 00:00:50,910
them.

11
00:00:51,270 --> 00:00:56,730
I agree they can be a little bit tricky, and that's why we're actually going to spend a good bit of

12
00:00:56,730 --> 00:01:02,610
time going through them, through their different types, their different categories and different scenarios

13
00:01:02,610 --> 00:01:04,110
that they can exist in.

14
00:01:04,110 --> 00:01:09,720
And I'm going to show you different ways of discovering them and different ways of bypassing security.

15
00:01:09,720 --> 00:01:15,600
So by the end of the section, you're going to be an expert at discovering these vulnerabilities and

16
00:01:15,600 --> 00:01:21,120
you'll be able to discover them in websites and features that most people will think are secure.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 8: OS Command Injection

0
1

00:00:00,640  -->  00:00:02,930
the first example of injection.
1

2

00:00:02,930  -->  00:00:07,450
Vulnerabilities that we're going to talk about is os command injection.
2

3

00:00:08,540  -->  00:00:09,910
As the name suggests,
3

4

00:00:10,050  -->  00:00:13,890
these vulnerabilities allow us to execute system
4

5

00:00:13,890  -->  00:00:16,360
commands on the target web server.
5

6

00:00:17,140  -->  00:00:23,350
So as mentioned earlier, a website is simply a computer that is hosted on the cloud.
6

7

00:00:23,940  -->  00:00:29,410
This computer just like your computer runs an operating system such as Windows,
7

8

00:00:29,420  -->  00:00:29,750
Linux
8

9

00:00:29,970  -->  00:00:30,720
or Mac.
9

10

00:00:30,970  -->  00:00:32,650
Therefore command injection.
10

11

00:00:32,650  -->  00:00:37,760
Vulnerabilities allow us to execute system commands on that computer.
11

12

00:00:37,790  -->  00:00:41,690
So if the target computer is Windows, you'll be able to execute when those commands.
12

13

00:00:41,840  -->  00:00:43,910
If it's Lennox, you'll be able to execute
13

14

00:00:44,120  -->  00:00:44,750
commands,
14

15

00:00:45,040  -->  00:00:45,760
answer one.
15

16

00:00:46,140  -->  00:00:49,270
So for example, if the target web server runs Linux,
16

17

00:00:49,380  -->  00:00:53,600
which is one of the most common operating systems to run on web servers,
17

18

00:00:54,000  -->  00:00:57,650
then you'll be able to execute any terminal command
18

19

00:00:57,650  -->  00:01:00,660
or any Linux command on the target web server.
19

20

00:01:01,240  -->  00:01:02,730
Therefore, as a result,
20

21

00:01:02,740  -->  00:01:05,450
you're going to be able to compromise the application
21

22

00:01:05,459  -->  00:01:08,650
and possibly the whole computer or the whole server.
22

23

00:01:09,140  -->  00:01:14,000
And in many cases you'll even be able to compromise the whole network
23

24

00:01:14,010  -->  00:01:17,710
that the target computer or the target web server is connected to.
24

25

00:01:17,720  -->  00:01:21,250
Because if you've done any of my courses or if you have any Linux
25

26

00:01:21,410  -->  00:01:26,450
or Windows experience, you'll know that you can do so much using the command line.
26

27

00:01:27,140  -->  00:01:32,290
But anyway, in most bug bounty scenarios, all you have to do is display impact.
27

28

00:01:32,300  -->  00:01:33,940
So all you have to do is show them
28

29

00:01:34,050  -->  00:01:37,560
that you're able to execute a system command on the target
29

30

00:01:37,670  -->  00:01:42,010
and that's enough to show them that the target is vulnerable and to get your bounty.
30

31

00:01:42,840  -->  00:01:45,670
So let's have a look on a simple example and
31

32

00:01:45,670  -->  00:01:48,020
we'll build up on that in the next lectures.
32

33

00:01:48,090  -->  00:01:50,950
As usual I'm going to include the link in the resources
33

34

00:01:50,960  -->  00:01:52,950
and I'm just gonna open it in a new tab.
34

35

00:01:55,140  -->  00:01:57,770
And as you can see a simple shop website
35

36

00:01:57,890  -->  00:02:00,880
and as usual you should test every single functionality.
36

37

00:02:00,930  -->  00:02:05,360
But I know the vulnerable feature is in the product details page.
37

38

00:02:05,370  -->  00:02:10,570
And if you go down as usual you want to check out the normal behavior of the website.
38

39

00:02:10,570  -->  00:02:14,960
So we have a stock checker in here that allow us to check the stock
39

40

00:02:15,140  -->  00:02:17,060
in whatever location you select.
40

41

00:02:17,440  -->  00:02:21,460
So for example if you select paris and click on check stock
41

42

00:02:22,140  -->  00:02:25,260
It will show you that they have 73 units of this product.
42

43

00:02:25,640  -->  00:02:26,360
Pretty cool.
43

44

00:02:27,540  -->  00:02:31,910
Now usually the next step is to turn on our interceptor in the proxy
44

45

00:02:31,920  -->  00:02:36,110
and send the request to see all of the data that is being sent,
45

46

00:02:36,130  -->  00:02:39,070
analyze it and see if we can modify user
46

47

00:02:39,070  -->  00:02:42,860
data to manipulate the execution of this application.
47

48

00:02:43,240  -->  00:02:46,480
Now if we click on the inspector in here we can see that
48

49

00:02:46,480  -->  00:02:51,150
we have two body parameters that we can modify as users and manipulate.
49

50

00:02:51,190  -->  00:02:53,950
And they are the product I. D. And the store I. D.
50

51

00:02:54,340  -->  00:02:58,080
So you can double click any of these values and modify them in here.
51

52

00:02:58,090  -->  00:03:02,860
Or you can close this and modify them in here in plain text.
52

53

00:03:03,740  -->  00:03:05,210
So as mentioned before.
53

54

00:03:05,220  -->  00:03:09,100
Everything that comes after the equal sign should be tested for all of
54

55

00:03:09,100  -->  00:03:12,560
the vulnerabilities that you learned and that you will learn in the future.
55

56

00:03:12,940  -->  00:03:16,270
So this will take a lot of tests and a lot of trial and error.
56

57

00:03:16,740  -->  00:03:19,880
Therefore instead of having to click the check stalk and
57

58

00:03:19,880  -->  00:03:22,900
intercept it in here every time and then modifying it
58

59

00:03:23,050  -->  00:03:24,550
to make the process easier,
59

60

00:03:24,560  -->  00:03:27,750

60

61

00:03:28,630  -->  00:03:31,810
I explained what the repeater is and what it does previously.

62

62

63

But basically as mentioned before it allows us to modify the requests in here,
63

64

click on send and see the response and the result in here so that we
64

65

65

66

00:03:47,350  -->  00:03:49,250
and see the response that we get.

67

00:03:50,040  -->  00:03:52,570
But before doing that again as usual we want to
67

68

send the normal request and see the normal results.
68

69

00:03:55,450  -->  00:03:58,300
So this is a normal unmodified request.
69

70

00:03:58,410  -->  00:04:02,660
And as you can see once we send a violet product I. D. And a violet store I. D.
70

71

00:04:02,790  -->  00:04:07,650
We get the amount of stock available in that store for the selected product.
71

72

00:04:08,640  -->  00:04:12,110
So as usual you want to manipulate the values after the equal
72

73

00:04:12,110  -->  00:04:15,030
sign and test for all of the vulnerabilities that you know.
73

74

00:04:15,040  -->  00:04:18,860
So the first one we have is the product I. D. Equals three.
74

75

00:04:18,870  -->  00:04:23,180
And now because we're testing for command injection vulnerabilities
75

76

00:04:23,180  -->  00:04:25,460
and because I already know that this is vulnerable.
76

77

00:04:25,540  -->  00:04:28,860
I'm not gonna waste your time tasting for stuff that doesn't exist.
77

78

00:04:29,140  -->  00:04:31,540
So the first parameter that we're gonna test is the
78

79

00:04:31,540  -->  00:04:34,670
product ID parameter in here and its value is three.
79

80

00:04:34,700  -->  00:04:37,970
So we're going to simply send a normal system command.
80

81

00:04:38,190  -->  00:04:40,420
An example of this would be the U.
81

82

00:04:40,420  -->  00:04:43,800
Name command which gives you the operating system that
82

83

00:04:43,800  -->  00:04:46,440
is running on the target computer if it was Linux
83

84

00:04:46,540  -->  00:04:47,560
or UNIX based.
84

85

00:04:48,140  -->  00:04:48,890
And to do that,
85

86

00:04:48,890  -->  00:04:52,850
I'm going to put a semicolon followed by that command which is your name.
86

87

00:04:53,430  -->  00:04:57,460
Now the reason why I put the semicolon in here because semicolons
87

88

00:04:57,460  -->  00:05:02,360
can be used in the terminal to contaminate commands or join commands.
88

89

00:05:02,840  -->  00:05:05,000
So there's a number of special characters and
89

90

00:05:05,000  -->  00:05:06,950
we'll talk about that more in the next lecture
90

91

00:05:07,160  -->  00:05:09,610
that can be used in the terminal in order
91

92

00:05:09,610  -->  00:05:13,190
to execute multiple commands in the same line.
92

93

00:05:13,490  -->  00:05:14,920
Let me show you a quick example.
93

94

00:05:14,930  -->  00:05:18,070
So I have my terminal in here and I can basically do
94

95

00:05:18,080  -->  00:05:21,950
your name to show the version of my kernel which is Darwin
95

96

00:05:22,240  -->  00:05:27,660
and I can do PWd to show my current working directory which is users zoos
96

97

00:05:28,240  -->  00:05:32,250
and I can combine both of these commands so I can do you name
97

98

00:05:32,410  -->  00:05:34,520
semicolon P W D.
98

99

00:05:34,680  -->  00:05:38,160
And because I use the semicolon in here between the two commands.
99

100

00:05:38,200  -->  00:05:40,030
It's going to execute the first command,
100

101

00:05:40,030  -->  00:05:44,160
show me the result and then execute the second command and show me its result.
101

102

00:05:44,640  -->  00:05:46,200
So let me show you in here.
102

103

00:05:46,210  -->  00:05:50,060
And as you can see we get the results of executing the two commands.
103

104

00:05:50,840  -->  00:05:52,340
So what we're doing in here,
104

105

00:05:52,580  -->  00:05:57,200
we're saying if the target application is executing a system command,
105

106

00:05:57,230  -->  00:06:02,100
we wanna add a semicolon to inject the second command that we want to run.
106

107

00:06:02,110  -->  00:06:04,460
And in this case it's the U. Name command.
107

108

00:06:05,240  -->  00:06:07,660
So we're going to hit send to send it to the target.
108

109

00:06:08,040  -->  00:06:09,820
And as you can see we get nothing.
109

110

00:06:09,820  -->  00:06:12,280
So we don't even get the stock value but we
110

111

00:06:12,280  -->  00:06:15,150
also don't get the result of executing our command.
111

112

00:06:15,940  -->  00:06:18,870
Therefore we're going to move on to the next parameter
112

113

00:06:18,870  -->  00:06:21,660
that we have which is the store I D parameter.
113

114

00:06:22,110  -->  00:06:24,590
And similarly we're still saying coma,
114

115

00:06:24,600  -->  00:06:28,250
you name the command that we want to execute and we're going to hit sand
115

116

00:06:29,390  -->  00:06:33,060
and perfect this time. As you can see we're getting the stock amount
116

117

00:06:33,260  -->  00:06:37,740
plus the result of the execution of our command. So now the result is saying
117

118

00:06:38,100  -->  00:06:42,260
because the target web server is running a Linux operating system.
118

119

00:06:42,840  -->  00:06:47,840
So to verify this also, you could do P W D for example to get your working directory
119

120

00:06:47,960  -->  00:06:51,060
and as you can see we're getting our current working directory.
120

121

00:06:51,840  -->  00:06:55,560
So from here onwards you can replace this with any system
121

122

00:06:55,560  -->  00:06:58,400
command that you want to execute on the target system.
122

123

00:06:58,480  -->  00:07:00,560
And this could be very devastating.
123

124

00:07:00,590  -->  00:07:03,870
And I show this in my website hacking course because you
124

125

00:07:03,870  -->  00:07:07,220
can basically gain full control over the target web server.

126

00:07:07,230  -->  00:07:11,400
You can gain full control over the other applications installed on it and
126

127

00:07:11,400  -->  00:07:14,500
possibly even compromise the internal network that
127

128

00:07:14,500  -->  00:07:16,730
the target web server is connected to
128

129

00:07:16,940  -->  00:07:21,630
in a pen test. You have to do this. It would be very useful to include in your report.

130

00:07:21,710  -->  00:07:25,750
But in most bug bounty programs you actually don't need to do
130

131

00:07:25,750  -->  00:07:31,010
this because by simply executing any command on the target web server,
131

132

00:07:31,020  -->  00:07:33,550
you're able to display impact and you're able
132

133

00:07:33,550  -->  00:07:36,370
to prove the existence of this vulnerability.
133

134

00:07:36,380  -->  00:07:39,660
So you will be awarded by simply displaying this impact.

0
1

00:00:00,540  -->  00:00:05,850
in this lecture we'll talk about blind or s command injection. Vulnerabilities.
1

2

00:00:06,940  -->  00:00:11,130
So in the previous lecture we were able to inject a system command in
2

3

00:00:11,130  -->  00:00:16,260
a request and see the result of the command execution in the response.
3

4

00:00:16,640  -->  00:00:19,780
So in this case it's very easy to validate the
4

5

00:00:19,780  -->  00:00:22,940
existence of the bug and even to exploit it because
5

6

00:00:22,940  -->  00:00:25,130
you can literally see the result right in front of
6

7

00:00:25,130  -->  00:00:28,250
you as if you're executing the command on the terminal.
7

8

00:00:29,640  -->  00:00:29,910
Now,
8

9

00:00:29,910  -->  00:00:32,920
in many cases the target web application might be
9

10

00:00:32,920  -->  00:00:37,140
vulnerable to an os command execution similar to what we
10

11

00:00:37,140  -->  00:00:41,900
have in here and you'd be able to exploit it exactly the same way that you see in here.
11

12

00:00:42,030  -->  00:00:48,360
But the web application is not going to show you the result of the command execution,
12

13

00:00:48,740  -->  00:00:52,040
hence the name blind or s command injection
13

14

00:00:52,190  -->  00:00:56,150
and therefore making it harder to discover or spot.
14

15

00:00:56,820  -->  00:01:02,100
In this case we can execute a command that might cause the target system
15

16

to pause or sleep for a few seconds and then monitor the time taken for the response.

P. W. D.

So what we're gonna do is execute a command

So let me show you a quick example in your terminal.

00:03:47,120  -->  00:03:50,290
You can simply type sleep followed by the amount of
63

64

00:03:50,290  -->  00:03:52,900
seconds that you want the terminal to sleep for.
64

65

00:03:53,020  -->  00:03:54,270
And when I hit enter
65

66

00:03:54,450  -->  00:03:57,610
I won't see the prompt for another five seconds.
66

67

00:03:58,340  -->  00:04:00,460
As you can see now I got the prompt again.
67

68

00:04:01,040  -->  00:04:05,290
So if I inject this command in the target application and
68

69

00:04:05,290  -->  00:04:08,810
if the target application is vulnerable to a command execution,
69

70

00:04:08,830  -->  00:04:11,100
I don't even need to see the result.
70

71

00:04:11,110  -->  00:04:14,070
If the target application sleeps for 10 seconds or
71

72

00:04:14,070  -->  00:04:17,149
five seconds or whatever amount of seconds I specify
72

73

00:04:17,310  -->  00:04:21,200
then I will know that the target application is executing my commands
73

74

00:04:21,540  -->  00:04:24,980
and therefore even though we can't see the result of the command,
74

75

00:04:24,980  -->  00:04:28,750
we know that the target is vulnerable and we can execute other more
75

76

00:04:28,750  -->  00:04:33,010
dangerous commands that will allow you to gain full control over the server.
76

77

00:04:33,020  -->  00:04:34,970
And as I mentioned in the previous lecture,

79

vulnerabilities
104

and it's usually stuff that is used in the terminal

or in the command prompt to combine two commands.

107

just in case there is another command to
112

So we don't know how do web application is programmed and we don't know the code

that is used in there.

So you shouldn't simply give up if you type a command and you don't get the result,

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,740  -->  00:00:03,770
previously we covered blind or s command
1

2

00:00:03,780  -->  00:00:07,700
injections where the command was getting executed
2

3

00:00:07,710  -->  00:00:13,520
on the target server but we were not getting the result of the command execution
3

4

00:00:13,680  -->  00:00:14,750
in the response.
4

5

00:00:15,220  -->  00:00:20,420
Therefore we use commands that will delay the execution of the application
5

6

00:00:20,540  -->  00:00:25,760
to verify that the target application is vulnerable to a command injection.
6

7

00:00:26,690  -->  00:00:31,530
Now in some cases you might encounter blind or s command injection
7

8

00:00:31,620  -->  00:00:36,260
where the command is going to be executed on the target application
8

9

00:00:36,640  -->  00:00:40,840
but it's going to be executed in a new thread.
9

10

00:00:40,880  -->  00:00:44,360
So you can imagine the yellow line in here is
10

11

00:00:44,360  -->  00:00:48,320
the main application and once you inject a command into it
11

12

00:00:48,420  -->  00:00:51,460
it's going to be executed in a new thread.
12

13

00:00:52,040  -->  00:00:56,900
Therefore if you use a command to delay the execution of the application
13

14

00:00:56,960  -->  00:01:00,560
similar to what we did in here when we use the sleep command
14

15

00:01:00,990  -->  00:01:03,880
that command is going to be executed in the new
15

16

00:01:03,880  -->  00:01:06,680
thread and it's going to sleep for 10 seconds.
16

17

00:01:06,790  -->  00:01:10,600
But the Web application is going to continue running
17

18

00:01:10,710  -->  00:01:12,460
without any delays.
18

19

00:01:12,540  -->  00:01:16,140
And therefore even though your command is getting executed
19

20

00:01:16,150  -->  00:01:19,330
you'll have no way of validating that because there
20

21

00:01:19,330  -->  00:01:22,040
is nothing for you to observe to tell you
21

22

00:01:22,140  -->  00:01:25,160
that your command got executed in the background.
22

23

00:01:26,040  -->  00:01:27,950
Let me show you a quick example of this.
23

24

00:01:27,980  -->  00:01:32,350
The vulnerable feature is the submit feedback. So I'm going to go directly to it
24

25

00:01:32,620  -->  00:01:35,360
and we're just gonna submit some dummy data
25

26

00:01:35,940  -->  00:01:38,440
and in a real life scenario you want to send it,
26

27

00:01:38,450  -->  00:01:41,930
observe the result and then intercept it but you already know
27

28

00:01:41,930  -->  00:01:44,200
the sequence now so I'm not going to keep doing it.
28

29

00:01:44,210  -->  00:01:47,760
I'm just gonna turn on my interceptor and I'm gonna send it.
29

30

00:01:48,140  -->  00:01:50,280
We have the request in here
30

31

00:01:50,500  -->  00:01:54,140
and I'm actually just going to right click and send it to the repeater.
31

32

00:01:54,150  -->  00:01:58,650
So I can continuously modify the request in here and analyze
32

33

00:01:58,650  -->  00:02:02,860
the response and try to discover vulnerabilities based on that.
33

34

00:02:04,540  -->  00:02:05,580
Also as mentioned,
34

35

00:02:05,580  -->  00:02:09,370
you want to test every single value that is being sent after the equal
35

36

00:02:09,370  -->  00:02:13,730
sign but I know already the vulnerable parameter right here is the email.
36

37

00:02:13,740  -->  00:02:18,580
So we're gonna test it similar to how we tested the previous vulnerability.
37

38

00:02:18,590  -->  00:02:26,990
So we did or or we put the command which was sleep for 10 seconds and we did or at the end.
38

39

00:02:27,540  -->  00:02:30,620
So in a normal scenario I would try with calm at first
39

40

00:02:30,630  -->  00:02:33,410
I would try normal commands and see if I get the response
40

41

00:02:33,420  -->  00:02:36,130
but I already know that I will get nothing in the response
41

42

00:02:36,140  -->  00:02:39,150
and I already know that this works from the previous example.
42

43

00:02:39,160  -->  00:02:41,020
That's why we're going straight away with this
43

44

00:02:41,030  -->  00:02:43,650
because we're covering a completely different point.
44

45

00:02:44,640  -->  00:02:45,960
So I'm going to click send
45

46

00:02:46,640  -->  00:02:49,760
and as you can see I got the result instantly.
46

47

00:02:50,540  -->  00:02:52,740
Unlike what happened in the previous example
47

48

00:02:52,750  -->  00:02:55,070
where the application slept for 10 seconds.
48

49

00:02:55,250  -->  00:02:57,760
And that is because as I mentioned earlier
49

50

00:02:57,890  -->  00:03:02,580
this application is continuing to run in the main thread and the command
50

51

00:03:02,580  -->  00:03:06,780
that I'm injecting the sleep for 10 is being executed in the background
51

52

00:03:07,240  -->  00:03:13,150
and therefore there is no clues for me in here to tell me that my command got executed.
52

53

00:03:14,240  -->  00:03:16,210
Therefore to get around this,
53

54

00:03:16,220  -->  00:03:20,480
you're going to have to use a command such as the pink or the ns.
54

55

00:03:20,480  -->  00:03:21,590
Look up command
55

56

00:03:21,970  -->  00:03:24,660
to cause the target web application
56

57

00:03:24,960  -->  00:03:29,580
to send a request to another server that you control.
57

58

00:03:29,740  -->  00:03:34,630
And then by observing the logs on the server that you control on the cloud,
58

59

00:03:34,840  -->  00:03:38,760
you'll be able to see that the target web application
59

60

has made a request to the server that you specified

61

00:03:43,000  -->  00:03:46,960
indicating that it executed the command that you injected.
61

62

You could create your own hosting on the cloud and I
62

cover how to do that in my cloud course for free.

And you'll be able to test for such vulnerabilities.

So you'll just have to inject the ping command to

So in a real life scenario,
71

72

This feature allows you to generate a domain name. So if I click on copy to clipboard

So this is the I. P. Of the server that is hosting the target application
118

119

00:07:09,740  -->  00:07:15,550
and this will serve as proof that the target web application is vulnerable to
119

120

00:07:15,550  -->  00:07:19,310
a command execution vulnerability because we managed
120

121

00:07:19,310  -->  00:07:21,420
to get it to execute the Ns look
121

122

00:07:21,420  -->  00:07:26,460
up command and we have a proof of that command being executed because the web
122

123

00:07:26,460  -->  00:07:31,270
server did actually send the request to the server that is controlled by us.
123

124

00:07:31,280  -->  00:07:34,160
And we can see that we received the request in here.
124

125

00:07:35,440  -->  00:07:37,740
So again, in the bug bounty scenario,
125

126

00:07:37,750  -->  00:07:40,280
this is more than enough to display that the
126

127

00:07:40,280  -->  00:07:43,320
target web server is vulnerable in a pen test,
127

128

00:07:43,330  -->  00:07:46,630
you can inject commands in here that will send
128

129

00:07:46,630  -->  00:07:50,170
you a reverse connection to your own computer and
129

130

00:07:50,170  -->  00:07:53,000
from there you'll be able to interactively control the
130

131

00:07:53,000  -->  00:07:56,770
target web server and possibly compromise the whole network.
131

132

00:07:56,780  -->  00:07:59,900
And I do cover all of that in my website hacking course,
132

133

00:07:59,910  -->  00:08:04,220
but it's not really relevant when it comes to bug bounties because what
133

134

00:08:04,220  -->  00:08:07,860
you have in here is enough to prove that the target is vulnerable.

1

So in the previous lecture we learned that we can use the NS look up or the ping command

2

to send a request to a server that we control such as this burp collaborator domain
2

3

00:00:12,050  -->  00:00:17,100

3

4

request we're going to be able to capture it in here.
4

5

Or if we're sending it to our own server will be able to see the request in the logs.
5

6

00:00:25,940  -->  00:00:29,100
And I said that's enough proof to say that
6

7

00:00:29,100  -->  00:00:32,670
the target server is vulnerable to a command injection.

8

00:00:33,240  -->  00:00:37,820
And I also said in a pen test you can inject a command to send you a
8

9

you might find yourself in a situation where you're not able
13

14

So in this lecture, I'm going to show you how to take this exploit right here.

18

18

19

results so that you're not simply limited by
19

20

reading the requests that you're getting in here

21

and doing that is actually very very simple.

23

24

so that we perform an NS look up to the command that we
24

25

want to execute on the server surrounded by these quotes in here.
25

26

And as a result the terminal is first going to execute the command that you
26

27

00:01:49,270  -->  00:01:55,350
have between the two quotes and replace it with the result of the command execution
27

28

00:01:55,840  -->  00:01:59,500
like so and then perform an ns look up

29

to whatever results you got from executing this command

30

dot your server dot com.

And this way I'll be able to get the result of executing my command.

Let me show you a quick example before we do this. So I'm gonna clear this

38

39

which is your name.

40

Close the quote put a dot so that this is considered as a sub domain.

41

So my system is first going to execute you name,
42

get the result of that which is going to be
43

44

Darwin and then send an ns look up to Darwin dot

my domain.

Then I'm going to read the logs of my domain and I'll be able to see that somebody made

a request to Darwin dot my domain And therefore
47

48

I'll know that the result of executing the U.
48

Name command is Darwin.

So let me show you I'm going to hit enter

51

and we're gonna go to the collaborator.

52

We're gonna click on paul now, click on the result that we got.
52

53

00:03:29,440  -->  00:03:31,260
And perfect as you can see,
53

54

00:03:31,270  -->  00:03:35,360
the first part of the request now is Darwin because we're making
54

55

00:03:35,370  -->  00:03:40,160
a request to the result of the execution of the union command,
55

56

00:03:40,850  -->  00:03:43,360
which is Darwin. As you can see in here
56

57

00:03:43,740  -->  00:03:46,950
dot whatever domain that you control.
57

58

00:03:47,840  -->  00:03:50,170
So we're going to do the exact same thing in here.
58

59

00:03:50,210  -->  00:03:54,880
We're doing the ns look up command to our burp collaborator domain.
59

60

We're just going to use to back quotes and put the command between it.
60

61

So we're going to run the U name command and we're
61

62

gonna put a dot in here to make this a sub domain
62

and that's it. We're good to go.

64

So the main thing you want to keep in mind is we're using back quotes in here.
64

65

They're not single or double quotes. These are back quotes.
65

66

00:04:18,200  -->  00:04:20,760
And as mentioned when you do it this way,
66

67

the command that you're placing in here will be

68

69

the ns look up command is going to be performed

70

to the result of the execution of this command.

71

And therefore when we analyze the logs we're going to
71

be able to see the result as a sub domain.

73

00:04:41,340  -->  00:04:43,260
We're going to collect on sand.

And let's go to the collaborator client and click on paul now and perfect.

As you can see we have a DNS request and we can see the sub domain.

The first part of it is the result of executing

the u name command that we specified in here.

So now you can replace your name with any command

# Section 9: XSS - Cross Site Scripting

0
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:02,780
cross site scripting is a type of vulnerabilities
1

2

00:00:02,900  -->  00:00:09,060
that allow us to inject javascript code in the pages of the target website.
2

3

00:00:09,640  -->  00:00:12,340
Now, javascript forget about hacking,
3

4

00:00:12,340  -->  00:00:15,930
Forget about exploiting a Javascript is a language
4

5

00:00:15,940  -->  00:00:19,090
that gets interpreted and executed by the browser.
5

6

00:00:19,100  -->  00:00:21,250
So it's a client side language.
6

7

00:00:21,260  -->  00:00:26,360
What we mean by that is the javascript code does not get executed on the server,
7

8

00:00:26,370  -->  00:00:30,160
it's only executed on the client web browser.
8

9

00:00:30,340  -->  00:00:33,430
This is very important because a lot of vulnerabilities that
9

10

00:00:33,430  -->  00:00:36,870
we cover like the code execution like SQL injections,
10

11

00:00:36,880  -->  00:00:40,800
they get interpreted and executed by the server.
11

12

00:00:40,810  -->  00:00:46,030
Therefore we can directly use them to hack into the target server. Whereas X. S.
12

13

00:00:46,030  -->  00:00:48,920
S vulnerabilities allow us to inject javascript
13

14

00:00:48,930  -->  00:00:51,550
which gets executed by the web browser.
14

15

00:00:51,840  -->  00:00:54,510
That means it gets executed on the client
15

16

00:00:54,520  -->  00:00:57,960
on the user that actually loads the vulnerable page
16

17

00:00:58,340  -->  00:01:02,340
and therefore we can't directly use it to gain access to the website.
17

18

00:01:02,350  -->  00:01:06,620
But we know that usually the admin of a website,
18

19

00:01:06,620  -->  00:01:10,850
the owners always look at their website and use it and therefore we can
19

20

00:01:10,850  -->  00:01:17,070
use access to steal their cookies or steal their information or even lure them and
20

21

00:01:17,070  -->  00:01:20,880
get them to do something that will allow us to gain access to their computer
21

22

00:01:20,890  -->  00:01:24,560
and from there gain access to their website if the website was the target.
22

23

00:01:25,340  -->  00:01:28,930
So there is three types of excesses. Vulnerabilities.
23

24

00:01:28,940  -->  00:01:33,350
There is distort ones or persistent reflected and done based.
24

25

00:01:34,040  -->  00:01:37,360
The impact of all of them is always the same.
25

26

00:01:37,740  -->  00:01:43,510
The only difference is in the reflected xs s you will have to send the exploit itself,
26

27

00:01:43,510  -->  00:01:44,730
you'll have to send this link.
27

28

00:01:44,730  -->  00:01:45,320
For example,
28

29

00:01:45,320  -->  00:01:49,080
where assuming target dot com is our target page dot PHP
29

30

00:01:49,080  -->  00:01:52,440
is the vulnerable page and this is our exploit code.
30

31

00:01:52,450  -->  00:01:56,760
So this is the code that will be injected in the loaded page and will be executed.
31

32

00:01:56,940  -->  00:02:00,420
So you will have to send all of this to the target
32

33

00:02:00,540  -->  00:02:02,440
and wait for them to execute it.
33

34

00:02:02,450  -->  00:02:05,480
That's why it's called reflected because it's reflected
34

35

00:02:05,490  -->  00:02:08,160
on the browser that executes the U R.
35

36

00:02:08,160  -->  00:02:09,060
L. The link.
36

37

00:02:09,639  -->  00:02:10,020
Now,
37

38

00:02:10,020  -->  00:02:13,620
this is still not very suspicious because we're assuming
38

39

00:02:13,620  -->  00:02:16,780
dot target dot com is a popular website,
39

40

00:02:16,780  -->  00:02:18,190
for example google dot com.
40

41

00:02:18,200  -->  00:02:21,360
So you're sending away a trusted website to the target.
41

42

00:02:21,370  -->  00:02:24,560
Therefore there are likely to load it and one day loaded you
42

43

00:02:24,560  -->  00:02:27,550
will get the code that you have in here to be executed.
43

44

00:02:28,540  -->  00:02:32,840
The other main example of excess s is distort excesses vulnerabilities.
44

45

00:02:32,970  -->  00:02:33,910
These are really,
45

46

00:02:33,910  -->  00:02:39,520
really good because the code that you inject in the page will be stored.
46

47

00:02:39,620  -->  00:02:42,250
Therefore you will not have to interact with the user.
47

48

00:02:42,260  -->  00:02:45,150
The user will go willingly and load a vulnerable
48

49

00:02:45,150  -->  00:02:47,830
page and when they load this vulnerable page,
49

50

00:02:47,840  -->  00:02:50,260
the code will be executed on their browser.
50

51

00:02:50,440  -->  00:02:52,140
But the idea is with the stored
51

52

00:02:52,240  -->  00:02:57,480
the code is stored in the page so you don't need to communicate with the target.
52

53

00:02:57,490  -->  00:03:01,140
So for example, if you discover a stored excess s in google,
53

54

00:03:01,220  -->  00:03:05,170
the code will be executed on every browser that visits google.
54

55

00:03:05,180  -->  00:03:06,660
Whereas if it's reflected,
55

56

00:03:06,670  -->  00:03:08,750
you will still need to get your target
56

57

00:03:08,750  -->  00:03:12,560
to actually click the link that contains the code
57

58

00:03:13,140  -->  00:03:16,260
so we have reflected stored and dom is
58

59

00:03:16,260  -->  00:03:19,450
the third variant of excess s vulnerabilities.
59

60

00:03:19,470  -->  00:03:24,210
The only difference is there is no communication between the
60

61

00:03:24,210  -->  00:03:28,430
client and the web server when we exploit such vulnerabilities.
61

62

00:03:28,480  -->  00:03:30,990
Therefore nothing is going to be started on the server side.
62

63

00:03:30,990  -->  00:03:34,160
There is no logs to show that this has been exploited.
63

64

00:03:34,340  -->  00:03:38,350
If you have server side filters, they're not really gonna work because again,
64

65

00:03:38,360  -->  00:03:41,580
nothing is sent to the server and no server side protection,
65

66

00:03:41,580  -->  00:03:43,950
whether any kind of protection you have on the server side
66

67

00:03:43,960  -->  00:03:47,960
is useless because nothing is being sent to the server again.
67

68

00:03:48,640  -->  00:03:52,410
Now in the next lectures will cover a number of examples and
68

69

00:03:52,410  -->  00:03:55,420
you'll notice that discovering and exploiting
69

70

00:03:55,420  -->  00:03:57,790
these vulnerabilities is very similar.
70

71

00:03:57,890  -->  00:04:00,960
The main difference is the way they occur in the background

0
1

00:00:00,340  -->  00:00:02,210
now before trying an ex.
1

2

00:00:02,210  -->  00:00:08,180
Ss I usually try to see if I can inject normal html code because
2

3

00:00:08,180  -->  00:00:13,070
html injection is still considered as a vulnerability in many bug bounty programs.
3

4

00:00:13,080  -->  00:00:18,060
And if you're doing a pen test then you should certainly include it as a discovery.
4

5

00:00:19,140  -->  00:00:23,530
So a html injection is actually just pretty much very similar to X. S. S.
5

6

00:00:23,550  -->  00:00:27,960
It just means that we can inject html code instead of javascript code.
6

7

00:00:28,540  -->  00:00:32,140
Usually if I managed to discover a html injection,
7

8

00:00:32,150  -->  00:00:35,190
in most cases I'm able to convert it into an X.
8

9

00:00:35,190  -->  00:00:38,860
S. S. And therefore I would have to vulnerabilities discovered instead of one.
9

10

00:00:39,340  -->  00:00:41,510
So let's start with a simple example
10

11

00:00:41,670  -->  00:00:43,860
and then we're gonna move on to more complex ones.
11

12

00:00:44,240  -->  00:00:47,410
As you can see, Target is a normal website looks like a blog.
12

13

00:00:47,660  -->  00:00:51,160
And as usual, if you're targeting this website for the first time,
13

14

00:00:51,160  -->  00:00:55,200
whether it's a bug bounty or if it is a normal pen test,
14

15

00:00:55,210  -->  00:00:58,670
you just want to first of all visit the website itself, see what it is.
15

16

00:00:58,680  -->  00:00:59,440
It's a blog.
16

17

00:00:59,440  -->  00:01:05,120
Pretty cool load the blog post, for example, view post, look at it, see what we can do.
17

18

00:01:05,129  -->  00:01:10,030
Okay. We can add a comment. Maybe just even add a normal comment and see what happens.
18

19

See if you can exploit these comments, go back, all that kind of stuff.

Not really going to do it now because we covered it before.
20

21

00:01:16,340  -->  00:01:20,560
Just want to talk about it and then you see a search functionality. Cool.
21

22

00:01:20,560  -->  00:01:23,750
Let's try to search for something. So let's search for test,
22

23

00:01:23,940  -->  00:01:26,920
See if it works actually works. We've got zero results for tests.
23

24

00:01:27,240  -->  00:01:29,680
That's actually search for something that is in the website.
24

25

00:01:29,680  -->  00:01:34,350
So digital, there's this word digital here. So I'm just gonna tap digital
25

26

00:01:35,240  -->  00:01:38,960
just to see if the functionality works. It works. Cool. We got one result.
26

27

00:01:39,640  -->  00:01:46,450
See as I mentioned earlier, javascript is interpreted or executed by the browser.
27

28

00:01:46,460  -->  00:01:52,120
So if you have an input, whether it's an input like this in like a post box,
28

29

00:01:52,120  -->  00:01:57,410
like this one in here or if it is in here in the RL if you have a value here in the U.
29

30

00:01:57,410  -->  00:02:01,060
R. L. That is being displayed on the current page,
30

31

00:02:01,240  -->  00:02:06,070
that means that we as a user can actually inject normal text.

32

We're not saying it's a vulnerability, it just means that whatever I type,

So the reason why I always test for html injections

S. S. And therefore I would have to vulnerabilities discovered instead of one.

Ss vulnerability, why it's reflected because as you can see in here,

31

assuming this is the target again they will get the code executed.

As you can see the code that we injected is getting automatically loaded on the page.

00:04:41,710  -->  00:04:45,760
That's why it is a stored excess s vulnerability because whatever
81

00:04:45,760  -->  00:04:49,430

your code will be executed.

# Section 10: DOM XSS Vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,040  -->  00:00:04,730
previously we mentioned there are three different types of ex ss vulnerabilities.
1

2

00:00:04,790  -->  00:00:11,160
We have the reflected X. Ss where we actually put the Xs s payload or the evil code
2

3

00:00:11,270  -->  00:00:14,530
in the U. R. L. And then we'll have to send this U. R. L.
3

4

00:00:14,530  -->  00:00:17,500
To the target person to load in their browser.
4

5

00:00:17,510  -->  00:00:21,160
And once they do our evil code in here gets executed.
5

6

00:00:21,740  -->  00:00:26,980
Now this is good and not very suspicious because remember Target dot com would be
6

7

00:00:26,990  -->  00:00:31,190
a popular website that the target uses and therefore they will trust that website.
7

8

00:00:31,200  -->  00:00:32,659
It's not gonna seem malicious.
8

9

00:00:33,540  -->  00:00:35,690
We also spoke about stored excesses which
9

10

00:00:35,690  -->  00:00:38,330
is basically exactly the same as reflected.
10

11

00:00:38,350  -->  00:00:43,270
It gets you to compromise users as well. It injects javascript code in the target.
11

12

00:00:43,440  -->  00:00:47,210
The only difference is in this case instead of having to send the U. R. L.
12

13

00:00:47,230  -->  00:00:51,670
The evil code is stored somewhere possibly in the database and therefore the
13

14

00:00:51,670  -->  00:00:55,460
code will be executed to any user that loads the vulnerable page.
14

15

00:00:55,540  -->  00:00:58,120
You won't have to send them the payload within the U. R. L.
15

16

00:00:58,130  -->  00:01:00,230
Which makes it much more convincing.
16

17

00:01:00,230  -->  00:01:02,620
You won't even need to communicate directly with users.
17

18

00:01:02,620  -->  00:01:06,590
You can simply inject your evil code and just wait for people to
18

19

00:01:06,600  -->  00:01:10,150
load that page and then get the evil code loaded on their website.
19

20

00:01:11,040  -->  00:01:13,310
In this class we're going to talk about dumb
20

21

00:01:13,310  -->  00:01:16,340
based xs s vulnerabilities so we have reflected,
21

22

00:01:16,340  -->  00:01:16,960
stored
22

23

00:01:17,060  -->  00:01:21,290
and dom is the third variant of excess S vulnerabilities
23

24

00:01:21,480  -->  00:01:24,960
similar to reflected and stored. It's an excess vulnerability.
24

25

00:01:24,960  -->  00:01:28,860
So allows us to run javascript code in the vulnerable web pages.
25

26

00:01:29,540  -->  00:01:33,550
We can discover it exactly the same as the start and reflected.
26

27

00:01:33,650  -->  00:01:36,450
The only difference is there is no
27

28

00:01:36,450  -->  00:01:40,480
communication between the client and the web server
28

29

00:01:40,490  -->  00:01:43,600
when we exploit such vulnerabilities so they
29

30

00:01:43,600  -->  00:01:46,850
happen or occur completely within the dome.
30

31

00:01:47,440  -->  00:01:51,580
Adam is basically all the objects that you see on a web page.
31

32

00:01:51,580  -->  00:01:54,210
So when you load the webpage and you see all the elements on it.
32

33

00:01:54,210  -->  00:01:59,070
So let's have an example here you have a webpage here we have all these elements on it.
33

34

00:01:59,080  -->  00:02:03,070
All of this is called the dumb and each one of them is an object within the dome.
34

35

00:02:03,080  -->  00:02:06,460
So this is an object, this is an object. This is an object.
35

36

00:02:06,840  -->  00:02:10,460
The text in here is an object. Everything is an object within the loaded page.
36

37

00:02:10,940  -->  00:02:13,780
But it's all has already been loaded within your browser
37

38

00:02:14,110  -->  00:02:14,600
and
38

39

00:02:14,900  -->  00:02:15,460
says
39

40

00:02:15,840  -->  00:02:19,460
happens within this document. So when you exploit it,
40

41

00:02:19,840  -->  00:02:23,460
no communication happened between this object and the web server.
41

42

00:02:24,140  -->  00:02:29,320
The main idea is because there is no communication between the current loaded page,
42

43

00:02:29,320  -->  00:02:31,570
the document and the server.
43

44

00:02:31,610  -->  00:02:33,580
Nothing is going to be started on the server side.
44

45

00:02:33,580  -->  00:02:36,790
There's no logs to show that this has been exploited.
45

46

00:02:36,860  -->  00:02:38,780
If you have server side filters,
46

47

00:02:38,780  -->  00:02:41,390
they're not really gonna work because again nothing is
47

48

00:02:41,390  -->  00:02:44,190
sent to the server and no server side protection.
48

49

00:02:44,190  -->  00:02:46,510
Whether any kind of protection you have on the server side
49

50

00:02:46,740  -->  00:02:50,550
is useless because nothing is being sent to the server again.
50

51

00:02:51,240  -->  00:02:56,320
So usually you have the client in here. You send a request. So we send the client.
51

52

00:02:56,320  -->  00:03:01,250
If we discover an excess s vulnerable webpage we send this link to the target.
52

53

00:03:01,250  -->  00:03:04,900
This is an example of a reflected Xs s as we've seen before.
53

54

00:03:04,910  -->  00:03:08,550
So you have target dot com and then our payload here in yellow.
54

that this single quote is still part of the string.

to check if your input contains a single quote.

52

52

53

string and therefore you want to be able to run javascript code.

54

63

64

65

71

72

00:03:48,100  -->  00:03:52,050

72

73

74

00:03:55,540  -->  00:03:59,710

74

75

75

76

78

79

79

80

82

82

83

00:04:24,660  -->  00:04:28,120

84

00:04:28,130  -->  00:04:31,560

85

86

00:04:36,160  -->  00:04:39,660

87

00:04:40,040  -->  00:04:44,760
So basically it's telling the programming language to ignore this
87

88

00:04:44,760  -->  00:04:47,420
single quote every time you have a backward slash,
88

89

00:04:47,430  -->  00:04:51,560

89

90

00:04:53,240  -->  00:04:54,540

91

114

00:06:15,030  -->  00:06:18,080

115

00:00:51,460  -->  00:00:53,900
And then anybody connected to the internet
16

17

00:00:53,910  -->  00:00:56,450
is able to communicate with this computer
17

18

00:00:56,640  -->  00:01:01,310
because this computer has a specific application that allow it to act as a server.
18

19

00:01:01,320  -->  00:01:02,550
We call it a server.
19

20

00:01:02,740  -->  00:01:05,760
But if you strip everything down it's basically just
20

21

00:01:05,760  -->  00:01:08,490
a computer with special programs installed on it.
21

22

00:01:08,500  -->  00:01:12,340
Then we make that computer accessible over the cloud over the internet.
22

23

00:01:12,350  -->  00:01:14,410
Which makes it very appropriate.
23

24

00:01:14,410  -->  00:01:16,860
Use as a web server or a server that
24

25

00:01:16,860  -->  00:01:19,860
can be accessed from other computers or other locations.
25

26

00:01:20,440  -->  00:01:25,450
A very common application that could make it act as a server is a web server.
26

27

00:01:25,840  -->  00:01:26,860
What is the web server?
27

28

00:01:26,860  -->  00:01:30,110
It's basically an application that you can install on any computer.
28

29

00:01:30,110  -->  00:01:33,480
So the computer doesn't even have to be exposed to the internet.
29

30

00:01:33,480  -->  00:01:36,330
You can install it on your own local computer even
30

31

00:01:36,330  -->  00:01:39,440
if it's not connected to the internet is just an application
31

32

00:01:39,670  -->  00:01:41,540
and what that application does,
32

33

00:01:41,550  -->  00:01:45,500
it specifies a certain location within your file system
33

34

00:01:45,560  -->  00:01:48,060
and in that location you can host files.
34

35

00:01:48,440  -->  00:01:51,160
So anybody connected to the same network as you.
35

36

00:01:51,170  -->  00:01:54,410
Or if this computer is exposed to the Internet and
36

37

00:01:54,420  -->  00:01:57,770
anybody that is on the internet will be able to access
37

38

00:01:57,770  -->  00:02:01,730
the files stored within the web route or the file that
38

39

00:02:01,730  -->  00:02:04,550
the web server uses to serve files to the users.
39

40

00:02:05,140  -->  00:02:09,060
Web servers usually also contained the Web application files.
40

41

00:02:09,070  -->  00:02:12,490
So a web application is similar to any application.
41

42

00:02:12,500  -->  00:02:16,130
The only difference is is designed to run on a web server
42

43

00:02:16,140  -->  00:02:20,860
and designed to be served over the internet to another computer.
43

44

00:02:21,240  -->  00:02:24,430
So the web server can execute Web application
44

45

00:02:24,430  -->  00:02:27,330
languages similar to PHP python and ruby.
45

46

00:02:27,340  -->  00:02:32,000
So you would have the web application files stored within the web server.
46

47

00:02:32,010  -->  00:02:32,820
Because like I said,
47

48

00:02:32,830  -->  00:02:36,150
the initial goal of the web server is to be able to serve
48

49

00:02:36,160  -->  00:02:40,950
files to remote computers using a web browser using the http protocol.
49

50

00:02:41,940  -->  00:02:46,710
The web application files contain code. So the website files could be images.
50

51

00:02:46,710  -->  00:02:50,050
That could be normal html files so that they don't really contain code.
51

52

00:02:50,080  -->  00:02:52,100
But the web application files,
52

53

00:02:52,110  -->  00:02:55,920
they actually contain code written in a programming language that
53

54

00:02:55,930  -->  00:03:00,750
can process information and manipulate it and do useful tasks.
54

55

00:03:00,840  -->  00:03:04,860
So it would be written in languages like PHP python, ruby and so on.
55

56

00:03:05,140  -->  00:03:09,140
And most websites that actually do something useful other than websites.
56

57

00:03:09,140  -->  00:03:12,290
Just that just simply display information like a shop website.
57

58

00:03:12,300  -->  00:03:15,960
They would actually be written in a programming language such as these.
58

59

00:03:17,240  -->  00:03:21,950
Now another very common component of a website is the database.
59

60

00:03:22,440  -->  00:03:28,390
So the web server contains the files like images, styling files, html files.
60

61

00:03:28,390  -->  00:03:32,120
These are all just used to display the website to render the website.
61

62

00:03:32,130  -->  00:03:34,290
And then you have the web application files which is
62

63

00:03:34,290  -->  00:03:37,650
the ones run on the server site to process data.
63

64

00:03:37,840  -->  00:03:40,640
But the data is usually stored in a
64

65

00:03:40,640  -->  00:03:43,060
different application which is called a database.
65

66

00:03:43,340  -->  00:03:48,460
Now the database contains a number of tables and we have in here a sample of a table.
66

67

00:03:48,840  -->  00:03:51,240
We're assuming that this table contains products that
67

68

00:03:51,240  -->  00:03:53,450
are being sold on a shop website.
68

69

00:03:53,470  -->  00:03:58,030
So you can see, we have the idea of the products, we have the title.
69

70

00:03:58,030  -->  00:04:01,260
So this is an iphone and ipad a mouse and the price,
70

71

00:04:01,260  -->  00:04:04,050
we have the price in here for each of these products.
71

72

00:04:04,940  -->  00:04:08,230
So the web server contains the html pages.
72

73

00:04:08,230  -->  00:04:11,270
The beautiful pages that you actually see, the ones with.
73

74

00:04:11,270  -->  00:04:12,900
The it contains the images.
74

75

00:04:12,910  -->  00:04:17,230
So the images for the iphones, the ipad's the mouse is that this website is selling.
75

76

00:04:17,240  -->  00:04:22,250
It also contains a web application that interacts with
76

77

00:04:22,250  -->  00:04:25,760
the database to display all of these products.
77

78

00:04:25,840  -->  00:04:28,790
So the web application itself doesn't contain the products.
78

79

00:04:28,790  -->  00:04:31,660
The product information is stored in the database.
79

80

00:04:32,340  -->  00:04:37,410
Now the database contains the number of tables. This is just one example of a table.
80

81

00:04:37,420  -->  00:04:38,650
It will contain tables,
81

82

00:04:38,650  -->  00:04:40,690
for example for the users and it will contain the
82

83

00:04:40,690  -->  00:04:43,880
user names and passwords and then users when they log again
83

84

00:04:43,890  -->  00:04:47,890
the Web application with communicate with the database to check
84

85

00:04:47,900  -->  00:04:51,310
if the user that you're trying to log in with exists
85

86

00:04:51,440  -->  00:04:53,900
in the database and checks if the user name
86

87

00:04:53,900  -->  00:04:56,160
you supplied is the same one as the password.
87

88

00:04:56,170  -->  00:04:59,460
So it's very important to understand the role of the database
88

89

00:04:59,840  -->  00:05:02,130
Web application stored on the web server.
89

90

00:05:02,140  -->  00:05:06,140
It just communicates with the database gets information and
90

91

00:05:06,140  -->  00:05:09,120
then displays it or does something useful with it.
91

92

00:05:09,130  -->  00:05:11,440
But the data itself is not stored on the
92

93

00:05:11,440  -->  00:05:14,360
web server is not stored within the Web application.
93

94

00:05:14,370  -->  00:05:16,660
It's actually stored on the database.
94

95

00:05:17,540  -->  00:05:20,710
The Web application communicates with the database
95

96

00:05:20,720  -->  00:05:23,690
using a markup language called SQL.
96

97

00:05:23,700  -->  00:05:28,080
So whenever you load the products page, for example in a shop website,
97

98

00:05:28,080  -->  00:05:31,110
for example at the security, if you go to the shop page,
98

99

00:05:31,120  -->  00:05:34,660
it's going to communicate with our database and it's going to
99

100

00:05:34,670  -->  00:05:39,650
retrieve all of the products that we have on our store
100

101

00:05:39,940  -->  00:05:43,260
and it does that using a language called SQL
101

102

00:05:43,640  -->  00:05:47,490
If the web application itself is vulnerable as a hacker,
102

103

00:05:47,500  -->  00:05:50,180
you're going to be able to inject your
103

104

00:05:50,190  -->  00:05:54,910
own SQL statements and retrieve data from the database
104

105

00:05:55,020  -->  00:05:58,530
that you're not supposed to see. So you're supposed to see the products.
105

106

00:05:58,530  -->  00:06:00,720
That's fine because the shop website shows it
106

107

00:06:00,720  -->  00:06:03,270
to you but you're not supposed to see the
107

108

00:06:03,270  -->  00:06:05,750
data and the users table for example because
108

109

00:06:05,750  -->  00:06:08,540
that will expose the users the other emails,
109

110

00:06:08,550  -->  00:06:10,690
their phone numbers and possibly their passwords.
110

111

00:06:10,700  -->  00:06:14,590
But if you're able to inject your own SQL queries,
111

112

00:06:14,600  -->  00:06:18,150
you'll be able to manipulate this web application and
112

113

00:06:18,150  -->  00:06:21,900
communicate with the database and access the users table,
113

114

00:06:21,910  -->  00:06:26,350
possibly read the admin and log into the website as the administrator.
114

115

00:06:26,360  -->  00:06:30,050
And then you'll be able to do whatever that the admin can do on the website.
115

116

00:06:30,840  -->  00:06:33,550
But anyway usually when you're at that stage when
116

117

00:06:33,550  -->  00:06:36,860
you're able to inject whatever SQL statement you want,
117

118

00:06:37,240  -->  00:06:39,660
you don't even need to log in as admin in
118

119

00:06:39,660  -->  00:06:42,990
many cases because you already have access to the database.
119

120

00:06:43,000  -->  00:06:46,570
And the database usually contains all the information you need.
120

121

00:06:46,620  -->  00:06:48,100
It contains all the users,
121

122

00:06:48,100  -->  00:06:50,900
it contains all of their data and it contains all
122

123

00:06:50,900  -->  00:06:53,850
of the data that is stored on that website.
123

124

00:06:53,850  -->  00:06:57,890
Whatever you need from that website, it's usually stored in the database.
124

125

00:06:57,900  -->  00:06:59,950
It's not really stored in the web server.
125

126

00:07:00,340  -->  00:07:03,760
And even if it is stored on the web server you'll be able to log
126

127

00:07:03,760  -->  00:07:08,030
in as admin and then access the web server and the files on it.
127

128

00:07:08,110  -->  00:07:11,340
In many cases you'll be able to execute code,
128

129

00:07:11,350  -->  00:07:15,440
you'll be able to upload your own files and again upload the shell and gain access.
129

130

00:07:15,450  -->  00:07:20,260
You can do so much with SQL injections and we'll see that as we go through the series.
