0
1

00:00:00,140  -->  00:00:04,660
so now that we understand what SQL is, how can we discover it.
1

2

00:00:05,040  -->  00:00:06,050
As explained.
2

3

00:00:06,060  -->  00:00:11,110
And as we've seen in the excess s examples, the bottom concept is the same.
3

4

00:00:11,210  -->  00:00:17,460
We always look for places in the web applications where we can manipulate the input.
4

5

00:00:17,940  -->  00:00:21,850
So again all the arguments that you see all of the parameters,
5

6

00:00:21,860  -->  00:00:26,850
all of the post and get requests anywhere that you could manipulate as a user.
6

7

00:00:26,860  -->  00:00:29,460
You should test it for SQL injections.
7

8

00:00:30,040  -->  00:00:32,130
How do we perform the test? Very simple.
8

9

00:00:32,140  -->  00:00:36,210
We try to inject a statement that returns a false
9

10

00:00:36,410  -->  00:00:40,760
and analyze what happens on the website if the website breaks and then we try
10

11

00:00:40,760  -->  00:00:45,090
to inject a statement that returns a true and that shouldn't break the website.
11

12

00:00:45,190  -->  00:00:49,150
So if the true statement does not break the website and the false statement
12

13

00:00:49,240  -->  00:00:51,510
does break the website then the website
13

14

00:00:51,520  -->  00:00:53,980
is actually accepting whatever we're giving it.
14

15

00:00:53,980  -->  00:00:56,320
So it's executing the statements we give it
15

16

00:00:56,490  -->  00:00:59,390
and therefore from there we conclude that it's vulnerable
16

17

00:00:59,390  -->  00:01:02,120
to SQL injections and then we can inject other
17

18

00:01:02,120  -->  00:01:07,560
queries that allow us to access the database or upload files or read files and so on.
18

19

00:01:08,040  -->  00:01:10,900
So let's go ahead to the first example and have a
19

20

00:01:10,900  -->  00:01:14,850
look at how we would go about discovering and SQL injection
20

21

00:01:15,440  -->  00:01:20,420
as you can see in here we have a normal shop website as mentioned before.
21

22

00:01:20,430  -->  00:01:23,960
You should go ahead and test every single aspect of the website.
22

23

00:01:23,960  -->  00:01:25,780
Use it as a normal user.
23

24

00:01:26,020  -->  00:01:29,470
See how the website works normally without exploiting it.
24

25

00:01:29,470  -->  00:01:34,950
So you get an idea of what the pages look like first so that when you break the pages,
25

26

00:01:34,950  -->  00:01:39,150
you know what a good page or a normal page looks like and what a broken page looks like.
26

27

00:01:39,540  -->  00:01:42,120
So anyway, you can see here from the top,
27

28

00:01:42,120  -->  00:01:45,190
you can actually filter products based on the
28

29

00:01:45,190  -->  00:01:47,370
tags we have in here are the categories.
29

30

00:01:47,380  -->  00:01:50,250
So for example if we click on accessories,
30

31

00:01:50,640  -->  00:01:52,980
you will see that it's gonna filter the products that we
31

32

00:01:52,980  -->  00:01:55,650
have in here and you're only gonna see the accessories.
32

33

00:01:56,540  -->  00:01:58,800
Also if you look here on the top you
33

34

00:01:58,800  -->  00:02:02,060
will notice that the category is equal to accessories.
34

35

00:02:02,070  -->  00:02:04,780
So we have a parameter called category and
35

36

00:02:04,780  -->  00:02:07,550
the value that is being sent is accessories.
36

37

00:02:08,340  -->  00:02:09,289
Now straight away.
37

38

00:02:09,300  -->  00:02:13,450
If you look in here you'll see that the title in here says accessories.
38

39

00:02:13,460  -->  00:02:17,410
So if you apply what we learned before you're
39

40

00:02:17,410  -->  00:02:19,580
gonna try to manipulate this and play with it.
40

41

00:02:19,590  -->  00:02:22,900
So if we just type anything after the accessories,
41

42

00:02:22,910  -->  00:02:25,560
you can see that it's being reflected in here
42

43

00:02:25,740  -->  00:02:29,170
and therefore before you even try and SQL injections,
43

44

00:02:29,180  -->  00:02:34,180
you should actually try html injection as shown before and then try an Xs.
44

45

00:02:34,180  -->  00:02:39,450
S. Because you have user input that is being directly reflected on the page.
45

46

00:02:39,460  -->  00:02:44,610
And that's usually a really good cue to test for X. S. S. And html injections.
46

47

00:02:44,620  -->  00:02:48,260
Now this is not our topic. So that's why we're not gonna do that.
47

48

00:02:48,340  -->  00:02:48,590
Well,
48

49

00:02:48,590  -->  00:02:50,840
I'm just trying to tell you how I would go about
49

50

00:02:50,840  -->  00:02:53,300
a pen test if this was a real world scenario.
50

51

00:02:53,740  -->  00:02:56,610
What we're trying to test for is SQL injections.
51

52

00:02:56,620  -->  00:02:58,610
So we're just gonna go back to what it was.
52

53

00:02:58,620  -->  00:03:02,080
We just said accessories and to test for SQL injections.
53

54

00:03:02,080  -->  00:03:06,550
We want to put a true and false statement as mentioned in here.
54

55

00:03:07,010  -->  00:03:11,530
Now before we do that let's try to understand what's happening when
55

56

00:03:11,530  -->  00:03:14,860
you actually go to this website when we're sending this U.
56

57

00:03:14,860  -->  00:03:16,060
R. L. To the server.
57

58

00:03:16,440  -->  00:03:19,710
What happens is we have an SQL query this is the
58

59

00:03:19,710  -->  00:03:23,560
original query that is being executed by the web application.
59

60

00:03:23,730  -->  00:03:27,380
It's doing something like this. It's using a select query
60

61

00:03:27,540  -->  00:03:34,160
from a table called shop where the category is equal to the user input.
61

62

00:03:34,440  -->  00:03:37,550
So in our case it's called accessories in here not food and
62

63

00:03:37,550  -->  00:03:41,660
drink but in this example the category is food and drink.
63

64

00:03:42,140  -->  00:03:45,300
So what you see in here in yellow is the user input.
64

65

00:03:45,310  -->  00:03:48,840
Everything in white is being written by the web application itself.
65

66

00:03:48,850  -->  00:03:50,380
We can't really play with this.
66

67

00:03:50,390  -->  00:03:54,970
We can only change what's written in yellow because whatever we put in here after
67

68

00:03:54,970  -->  00:03:59,960
the equal sign is being placed in yellow between the two quotation marks in here.
68

69

00:04:00,740  -->  00:04:06,170
So what we're essentially trying to do is we're trying to inject our own code in here.
69

70

00:04:06,190  -->  00:04:10,610
If we manage to do that this will be sent to the database engine and it will be
70

71

00:04:10,620  -->  00:04:14,730
executed by the database engine which will retrieve whatever
71

72

00:04:14,730  -->  00:04:17,360
data we want to retrieve from the database.
72

73

00:04:19,040  -->  00:04:19,970
So
73

74

00:04:20,250  -->  00:04:24,480
the injection is gonna look like this. You have the name of the category.
74

75

00:04:24,490  -->  00:04:29,420
Food and drink. We're gonna write our own single quote to close this.
75

76

00:04:29,430  -->  00:04:32,130
So as you can see the web application itself closes it
76

77

00:04:32,140  -->  00:04:35,520
in here but we're gonna add that manually in the input
77

78

00:04:35,760  -->  00:04:38,750
and then we're going to say and one is equal to one
78

79

00:04:39,640  -->  00:04:41,460
and we're adding to
79

80

00:04:41,840  -->  00:04:46,780
minuses after it. Because this can be used as a comment to comment.
80

81

00:04:46,790  -->  00:04:50,140
The extra quote that the web application adds
81

82

00:04:50,140  -->  00:04:53,360
by itself because we're adding our own manually.
82

83

00:04:53,740  -->  00:04:58,120
So the resulting statement is going to be select star from shop where the
83

84

00:04:58,120  -->  00:05:02,890
category is equal to food and drink and one is equal to one.
84

85

00:05:03,050  -->  00:05:05,310
Now we know one is equal to one is true
85

86

00:05:05,420  -->  00:05:09,090
and we know that there is a category called food and drink
86

87

00:05:09,100  -->  00:05:13,750
and therefore this statement should not affect the execution of the page.
87

88

00:05:14,440  -->  00:05:18,110
The main thing you want to pay attention in here is we're manually adding
88

89

00:05:18,110  -->  00:05:23,590
a single quote to close the quote that the web application opens itself.
89

90

00:05:23,600  -->  00:05:27,690
And there were using a comment at the end to basically tell the
90

91

00:05:27,690  -->  00:05:31,910
web application to ignore this last quote that is added by the web application
91

92

00:05:31,910  -->  00:05:36,120
itself because then we will have an extra open quote and that would
92

93

00:05:36,120  -->  00:05:40,460
break the whole query in here because every open quote should be closed.
93

94

00:05:41,860  -->  00:05:45,180
Then we're going to run a false statement and this time we're saying
94

95

00:05:45,180  -->  00:05:49,760
select star from shop where the category is food and drink which is true

# Section 14: Blind SQL Injections

0
1

00:00:00,140  -->  00:00:03,700
So previously we covered SQL injections and in today's
1

2

00:00:03,700  -->  00:00:06,560
class we're gonna talk about blind SQL injections.
2

3

00:00:07,340  -->  00:00:09,980
Blind SQL injections are very similar to
3

4

00:00:09,980  -->  00:00:13,020
classic SQL injections that we covered previously.
4

5

00:00:13,030  -->  00:00:19,060
So they basically allow an attacker to exploit the web application and run SQL
5

6

00:00:19,060  -->  00:00:23,960
queries and then the SQL queries will actually be executed by the database engine.
6

7

00:00:24,440  -->  00:00:25,350
So for example,
7

8

00:00:25,350  -->  00:00:29,860
if you run a select statement to select the passwords from the user's database,
8

9

00:00:30,240  -->  00:00:32,950
the web application will send it to the engine to
9

10

00:00:32,950  -->  00:00:36,130
the database engine and the database engine will execute it.
10

11

00:00:36,300  -->  00:00:41,060
The only problem is with blind SQL injections is that you will not see the
11

12

00:00:41,060  -->  00:00:45,020
result being displayed on screen because the result
12

13

00:00:45,030  -->  00:00:47,660
is not returned to the web application.
13

14

00:00:47,740  -->  00:00:51,760
That is the only difference between blind and normal scale injections.
14

15

00:00:52,440  -->  00:00:54,320
So let me show you a quick example.
15

16

00:00:54,330  -->  00:00:58,290
Previously we had this example right here and I
16

17

00:00:58,290  -->  00:01:00,800
already put the payload because we covered it before.
17

18

00:01:00,810  -->  00:01:05,370
We're selecting the user name and you can see on screen the user name and
18

19

00:01:05,370  -->  00:01:08,960
the password being returned in here and we have the admin and its password.
19

20

00:01:08,970  -->  00:01:11,850
So that's an example of a normal SQL injection
20

21

00:01:12,110  -->  00:01:13,750
in a blind SQL injection.
21

22

00:01:14,240  -->  00:01:19,630
The query that we executed in here will actually be executed on the target.
22

23

00:01:19,630  -->  00:01:22,800
So this query right here would be executed on
23

24

00:01:22,800  -->  00:01:25,810
the target and will be executed by the database engine
24

25

00:01:25,920  -->  00:01:29,470
but we will not see the result on screen.
25

26

00:01:29,490  -->  00:01:32,220
Therefore exploiting it is a little bit trickier.
26

27

00:01:32,230  -->  00:01:35,150

00:01:36,040  -->  00:01:39,620
as usual you'd want to go ahead and test everything like every button.

70

# Section 16: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)

1
00:00:00,360 --> 00:00:06,180
And this section, I'm going to show you how to discover and exploit server side requests, forgery

2
00:00:06,180 --> 00:00:08,790
vulnerabilities, or SRF.

3
00:00:09,480 --> 00:00:16,890
According to OWASP top ten most common threads, XREF is actually number ten, so it is the least common

4
00:00:16,890 --> 00:00:20,850
vulnerability of everything that we're going to cover in this course.

5
00:00:20,850 --> 00:00:24,120
But it can be very, very dangerous.

6
00:00:24,120 --> 00:00:30,390
And huge companies like Google were vulnerable too, and previously, and because of its really high

7
00:00:30,390 --> 00:00:39,180
impact and because it was very dangerous, Google actually paid over 130,000 USD to the country that

8
00:00:39,180 --> 00:00:40,920
discovered this vulnerability.

9
00:00:40,920 --> 00:00:46,800
So if you want to be cashing big bounties like this one, make sure you keep your eyes open and practice

10
00:00:46,800 --> 00:00:47,910
what I'm going to show you.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:04,330
in today's class. We're gonna talk about server side request forgery.
1

2

00:00:04,660  -->  00:00:09,470
A lot of people find these vulnerabilities confusing or hard to understand.
2

3

00:00:09,560  -->  00:00:13,610
So to keep it simple, let's forget about the forgery part.
3

4

00:00:13,620  -->  00:00:17,230
And let's just talk about server side requests.
4

5

00:00:17,880  -->  00:00:21,410
Previously we learned when you try to access a website,
5

6

00:00:21,410  -->  00:00:24,030
you basically communicate with a computer
6

7

00:00:24,210  -->  00:00:28,510
that has a certain program installed on it that we call a web server.
7

8

00:00:28,530  -->  00:00:32,710
And the web server can serve you files, images and so on.
8

9

00:00:32,759  -->  00:00:39,790
And it can also interpret or execute server side languages like PHP python and so on.
9

10

00:00:40,200  -->  00:00:46,990
Now a lot of web servers do much more than simply serve you files and execute code a
10

11

00:00:46,990  -->  00:00:50,130
lot of the time they actually communicate with other
11

12

00:00:50,130  -->  00:00:52,930
servers for example and here we have an example
12

13

00:00:52,940  -->  00:00:56,185
of a web server that is communicating to another
13

14

00:00:56,185  -->  00:00:59,055
server in here that we're calling server one it's
14

15

00:00:59,055  -->  00:01:02,085
communicating to it by sending a request to this
15

16

00:01:02,085  -->  00:01:05,165
server's address which is server one dot com.
16

17

00:01:05,175  -->  00:01:07,645
Or it could be the I. P. Of this server.
17

18

00:01:07,655  -->  00:01:10,805
And then as a result it's getting user information.
18

19

00:01:10,950  -->  00:01:13,010
Now it's probably doing this because this is
19

20

00:01:13,010  -->  00:01:15,880
a protected server that contains private information.
20

21

00:01:15,920  -->  00:01:17,550
But we'll talk about that later.
21

22

00:01:17,600  -->  00:01:23,040
Right now, basically what's happening is you as a user is using a web server.
22

23

00:01:23,050  -->  00:01:27,350
The web server can serve your files, images and execute server site code.
23

24

00:01:27,360  -->  00:01:31,860
But to get the user information it actually makes a request to
24

25

00:01:31,870  -->  00:01:35,630
another server in here and then serve you the user information.
25

26

00:01:36,450  -->  00:01:41,040
Now. Again in many cases websites these days do so much more than that.
26

27

00:01:41,050  -->  00:01:43,620
So they might even have a shop and to do that.
27

28

00:01:43,620  -->  00:01:47,220
They communicate with another server to get your products or
28

29

00:01:47,220  -->  00:01:50,950
to get you your Visa card information so you can pay
29

30

00:01:50,960  -->  00:01:53,555
or to get you the stock of the amount of
30

31

00:01:53,555  -->  00:01:56,775
products that they have in their stock and so on.
31

32

00:01:56,785  -->  00:01:57,765
And to do that,
32

33

00:01:57,775  -->  00:02:00,925
it's making another request to another server in here
33

34

00:02:00,935  -->  00:02:04,355
and it's communicating to it using its domain name.
34

35

00:02:04,365  -->  00:02:08,324
But like I said, it can also communicate to it using its address.
35

36

00:02:08,759  -->  00:02:11,730
I should also mention that these servers could
36

37

00:02:11,730  -->  00:02:14,260
exist on the cloud in a completely different
37

38

00:02:14,260  -->  00:02:19,410
location or they might be connected to the same network as this computer in here.
38

39

00:02:19,420  -->  00:02:22,230
And as we know, a server is simply just a computer.
39

40

00:02:22,240  -->  00:02:25,790
So this right here could be just another computer with a certain application
40

41

00:02:25,790  -->  00:02:29,880
installed on its same goes to this and this device right here.
41

42

00:02:31,130  -->  00:02:35,800
So what we have right now is a client making a client request to the
42

43

00:02:35,800  -->  00:02:41,260
web server and then the web server is making server requests to other servers.
43

44

00:02:41,270  -->  00:02:46,290
So this is an example of a server request and this is an example of a client request.
44

45

00:02:46,560  -->  00:02:49,100
And if you remember previously we actually
45

46

00:02:49,100  -->  00:02:53,070
covered CSR f vulnerabilities where we manipulate the
46

47

00:02:53,070  -->  00:02:57,410
client request and get the client to submit something that they don't want to do
47

48

00:02:57,820  -->  00:03:00,870
and in server side request, forgery.
48

49

00:03:00,880  -->  00:03:04,310
We manipulate the server and make the server submit
49

50

00:03:04,310  -->  00:03:07,260
requests that it is not programmed to send.
50

51

00:03:07,270  -->  00:03:12,130
And as a result give us useful information as hackers or pen testers.
51

52

00:03:13,350  -->  00:03:16,070
So going back to this example right here,
52

53

00:03:16,080  -->  00:03:18,190
we have the client request in here to the web
53

54

00:03:18,190  -->  00:03:20,820
server and then the server side request in here,
54

55

00:03:20,820  -->  00:03:22,560
going to another server
55

56

00:03:22,740  -->  00:03:26,170
and a server side request forgery scenario.
56

57

00:03:26,240  -->  00:03:31,100
As a hacker, you would send a forged request to the web server.
57

58

00:03:31,110  -->  00:03:34,440
And as a result you could get the web server
58

59

00:03:34,450  -->  00:03:39,110
to go and submit requests to a completely different server.
59

60

00:03:39,170  -->  00:03:40,290
And as a result,
60

61

00:03:40,300  -->  00:03:42,280
if you are hacking the server or if
61

62

00:03:42,280  -->  00:03:44,940
you are submitting anything malicious to the server
62

63

00:03:45,120  -->  00:03:47,390
when this server is analyzed,
63

64

00:03:47,400  -->  00:03:52,380
it will seem like this server is the one that is trying to hack into it and not you.
64

65

00:03:52,720  -->  00:03:54,590
That's just one simple example.
65

66

00:03:54,600  -->  00:03:57,980
You can also submit requests to one of the
66

67

00:03:57,980  -->  00:04:01,230
servers that this server is usually communicate to.
67

68

00:04:01,240  -->  00:04:05,550
So if you remember we said this server might be communicating with
68

69

00:04:05,560  -->  00:04:09,320
this server to get user information as you can see in here.
69

70

00:04:09,490  -->  00:04:12,540
And this server is actually a protected server.
70

71

00:04:12,550  -->  00:04:15,270
So if you try to communicate with it directly,
71

72

00:04:15,280  -->  00:04:19,680
your requests will be denied because this server only communicates with
72

73

00:04:19,680  -->  00:04:23,460
this server because it probably has a whitelist or a blacklist.
73

74

00:04:23,470  -->  00:04:26,450
So it wouldn't communicate with you but it would
74

75

00:04:26,450  -->  00:04:29,720
communicate with this web server because it trusts it.
75

76

00:04:30,190  -->  00:04:34,780
So assuming this web server is vulnerable to a server side request forgery,
76

77

00:04:34,790  -->  00:04:38,610
you would be able to send a forged request to this web server.
77

78

00:04:38,620  -->  00:04:43,050
And then instead of simply asking server one dot com,
78

79

00:04:43,060  -->  00:04:46,110
you're gonna ask for forward slash private.
79

80

00:04:46,120  -->  00:04:48,180
Which in this scenario for example would
80

81

00:04:48,180  -->  00:04:51,310
get you private information about the user information
81

82

00:04:51,310  -->  00:04:55,570
that this server has like their passwords or address or phone and so on.
82

83

00:04:55,740  -->  00:04:58,820
Now this is only possible and this will only work
83

84

00:04:58,830  -->  00:05:02,170
because this server right here trusts the web server.
84

85

00:05:02,180  -->  00:05:05,300
So if you send that request directly from the client,
85

86

00:05:05,310  -->  00:05:10,460
it will be denied because this server does not know who you are and does not trust you.
86

87

00:05:11,450  -->  00:05:16,300
Another scenario of exploiting server side request forgery. You can simply send a
87

88

00:05:16,580  -->  00:05:20,640
request to the server and cause it to send requests to itself.
88

89

00:05:20,650  -->  00:05:21,200
Again,
89

90

00:05:21,200  -->  00:05:24,540
probably bypassing security measurements and firewalls that this
90

91

00:05:24,540  -->  00:05:27,700
server has because you're simply sending these requests
91

92

00:05:27,700  -->  00:05:30,440
from the server itself and overloading it by
92

93

00:05:30,440  -->  00:05:34,100
requests that are originating from the same server.
93

94

00:05:35,730  -->  00:05:38,400
You could even send forest requests to
94

95

00:05:38,400  -->  00:05:42,560
this web server to discover the internal network
95

96

00:05:42,570  -->  00:05:47,840
and possibly even discover the open ports or the running services on these ports.
96

97

00:05:47,970  -->  00:05:51,950
And then from there discover vulnerabilities that these services
97

98

00:05:51,950  -->  00:05:55,190
might have and maybe even gain full control or
98

99

00:05:55,190  -->  00:05:58,420
code execution on the server or one of the
99

100

00:05:58,420  -->  00:06:01,020
clients that are connected to the same network.
100

101

00:06:02,110  -->  00:06:04,330
So the impact of server side request
101

102

00:06:04,330  -->  00:06:08,690
forgery could be minor or very devastating depending
102

103

00:06:08,700  -->  00:06:14,430
on the web application in here and what you can do with the exact vulnerability.
103

104

00:06:14,440  -->  00:06:19,350
So as we've seen, you can simply just send requests to other servers,
104

105

00:06:19,360  -->  00:06:21,510
you might be able to use it to access
105

106

00:06:21,510  -->  00:06:24,880
private data that is only available to the server.
106

107

00:06:24,890  -->  00:06:28,860
Or you could get it to disk, cover other clients in the same network,
107

108

00:06:28,860  -->  00:06:31,480
you could get it to discover other services
108

109

00:06:31,480  -->  00:06:33,700
running on these clients on the same network.
109

110

00:06:33,710  -->  00:06:38,910
And you can even gain a remote code execution from these vulnerabilities
110

111

00:06:39,090  -->  00:06:40,720
and don't worry about all of that.
111

112

00:06:40,720  -->  00:06:43,560
We're going to spend a lot of time on these vulnerabilities.
112

113

00:06:43,570  -->  00:06:46,310
You'll understand the impact of them better and you'll
113

114

00:06:46,310  -->  00:06:49,190
have a better understanding on how they work,
114

115

00:06:49,190  -->  00:06:51,650
how to discover them and how to exploit them.

So now that we understand the theory
1

2

of server side request forgery vulnerabilities.

3

00:00:05,620  -->  00:00:10,180
Let's have a basic example on how to discover it and exploit it and then
3

5

So right here we have a shop website and as usual you don't want to click on every link,

analyze the normal behavior of the website, make a list of it,
6

7

map the website and then test every single possible
7

8

00:00:26,620  -->  00:00:28,660
value that is being sent to the website.

9

00:00:28,800  -->  00:00:30,740
But obviously I don't want to waste your time.

10

I already know where the vulnerability is.

11

00:00:33,100  -->  00:00:37,080
So I'm simply just going to click on view details on a product again,
11

12

00:00:37,080  -->  00:00:40,490
you should be testing that, testing whatever values that are being sent.

13

00:00:40,710  -->  00:00:43,370
But I already know that the check stock in
13

14

here is vulnerable to a server side request forgery.
14

15

00:00:46,300  -->  00:00:50,640
So first of all we just want to check how this functionality works.
15

16

So for example, let's click on paris. This basically checks if this item is in stock.
16

17

17

18

19

19

20

21

So as you can see in here we have a few values being sent as opposed request,

22

same goes for cookies and all that.

But what I'm really interested in for this example is

the value that is being sent to the stock A.

26

00:01:31,750  -->  00:01:33,170
P. I. In here.

Now this value looks a little bit strange but
27

if you highlight it right click click on,
28

29

00:01:39,220  -->  00:01:41,570
convert selection and U.
29

30

R. L. Decoded. You will notice that this is simply A U. R. L.
30

31

00:01:47,550  -->  00:01:52,100
So what's happening right now when you click on check stock in this website,
31

32

00:01:52,110  -->  00:01:57,220
this web server that runs this website is sending another request
32

33

00:01:57,230  -->  00:02:01,580
to the web server that is running on the following address.
33

34

00:02:01,620  -->  00:02:06,170
Because if you compare this address to the web address in here,
34

35

00:02:06,180  -->  00:02:07,980
you will notice that they are different.

36

00:02:08,389  -->  00:02:14,180
Therefore this web server is making a server side request to the server that
36

37

is running on this address and it's asking for the product for the stock.
37

38

00:02:19,030  -->  00:02:22,180
It's given the product I. D. And the store I. D.
38

39

00:02:22,620  -->  00:02:25,600
Therefore simply seeing this and noticing the fact
39

40

00:02:25,600  -->  00:02:28,230
that this web server is sending a server
40

41

00:02:28,230  -->  00:02:33,270
side request to another server and the fact that we can actually see that in here.
41

42

00:02:33,270  -->  00:02:36,810
So as a client we are able to modify this
42

43

00:02:36,960  -->  00:02:38,870
to whatever server we want.
43

44

00:02:38,880  -->  00:02:41,500
Should give you a hint that I should test this and
44

45

I should try to modify this address to a different server and
45

46

00:02:46,080  -->  00:02:48,150
analyze the behavior of the website.
46

00:02:48,260  -->  00:02:52,980
If we are able to manipulate this and get it to submit requests
47

00:02:53,150  -->  00:02:56,660
to any other server other than what's specified in here,
48

49

00:02:56,670  -->  00:03:00,500
then you have discovered a server side request forgery because you're able
49

50

00:03:00,650  -->  00:03:05,220
to tamper with the server side request that this web server is trying to make
50

51

00:03:05,230  -->  00:03:09,540
and you're making it do something else that it is not designed to do.
51

52

00:03:10,500  -->  00:03:14,790
So to test, let's simply try to load local host.
52

53

00:03:15,660  -->  00:03:17,940
Now, if you don't know what we mean by this,
53

54

00:03:17,950  -->  00:03:21,610
basically local host is a reserved address
54

55

00:03:21,810  -->  00:03:25,410
that will load this machine to itself.
55

56

00:03:25,560  -->  00:03:27,700
So essentially what we're doing
56

57

00:03:27,900  -->  00:03:31,760
right now is we're forging a request to basically load
57

58

whatever service that is loading on our own machine.

59

00:03:35,760  -->  00:03:38,690
And because we did not specify a port.

60

00:03:38,700  -->  00:03:42,270
So usually if you want to specify a certain part you
60

61

would put a call on followed by the port number.
61

62

00:03:45,320  -->  00:03:47,730
But because we're not specifying a port,
62

63

00:03:47,740  -->  00:03:50,500
we're basically loading whatever service that is
63

64

running on Port 80 in this machine.
64

65

00:03:54,010  -->  00:03:59,100
The server machine because the server is what submitting this request, not us,
65

66

00:03:59,110  -->  00:04:01,730
we're simply sending a forged request and then the
66

67

00:04:01,730  -->  00:04:04,720
server is going to submit it on our behalf.
67

68

00:04:05,690  -->  00:04:09,460
And before sending this, if you remember this was U. R. L. Encoded.

69

So we're actually gonna highlight it right click
69

70

convert selection and this time we're going to select U. R. L. N.

71

00:04:17,100  -->  00:04:18,940
Code instead of decode
71

72

00:04:19,769  -->  00:04:24,290
and we're gonna click on forward forward forward.

73

And now if you scroll down you will notice
73

74

the behavior is different.

So the default behavior we actually saw the number of units in here.
75

76

00:04:33,210  -->  00:04:36,380
But now after we sent the first request to the
76

77

00:04:36,380  -->  00:04:39,910
server and got to send that request on our behalf,
77

78

00:04:44,610  -->  00:04:45,570
which is perfect.

80

So basically we managed to discover a server side request forgery

because we sent a forged request to this web server.
81

And as a result, we're actually able to see the website being loaded within itself.

00:02:37,600  -->  00:02:42,240

53

00:02:42,240  -->  00:02:46,210

53

54

00:02:46,570  -->  00:02:49,260
And the way blacklists work is very simple.
54

55

00:02:49,270  -->  00:02:52,810
You send your request to the web server and the web server will have a
55

56

00:02:52,810  -->  00:02:57,300
very simple if statement that will check if the request is in the black list.
56

57

00:02:57,450  -->  00:03:00,380
If it is, it won't send it to the other server.
57

58

00:03:00,450  -->  00:03:03,580
If it's not in the black list, it will send it to the server.
58

59

00:03:03,900  -->  00:03:08,020
Now the blacklist, it's a simple file that contains a number of words basically.
59

60

00:03:08,030  -->  00:03:11,570
And then your if statement checks if your request contains
60

61

00:03:11,570  -->  00:03:14,070
any of these words then it will block it.
61

62

00:03:14,080  -->  00:03:16,720
So if you're trying to go to admin or local host
62

63

00:03:16,720  -->  00:03:19,770
or administrator or control panel and this can be big.
63

64

00:03:19,770  -->  00:03:24,490
So they can include as many words as they want in here. The developer can do that.
64

65

00:03:24,570  -->  00:03:27,880
So if the request that your sentence similar to what we're doing here,
65

66

00:03:27,890  -->  00:03:29,260
we're asking for local host,
66

67

00:03:29,490  -->  00:03:31,930
if it contains any of the words in the black list,
67

68

00:03:31,940  -->  00:03:35,040
then your request will be blocked if it doesn't.
68

69

00:03:35,040  -->  00:03:38,310
Which is a normal scenario when it's just checking for the stock,
69

70

00:03:38,320  -->  00:03:40,280
it will be sent to the server and the response

So let's try to even remove all of this because
70

71

00:04:25,180  -->  00:04:28,400
again remember the original way that this was formatted.
71

72

00:04:28,410  -->  00:04:33,450
It didn't even specify a domain. It simply specified a product.
72

73

00:04:33,880  -->  00:04:37,070
So again we're now we're even getting closer to its
73

74

00:04:37,070  -->  00:04:40,800
original format and all that we're modifying is the path.
74

75

00:04:40,810  -->  00:04:43,700
And we're not really guessing with the path because as we saw
75

76

00:04:43,700  -->  00:04:48,740
earlier we have an open redirect vulnerability discovered in the path and we
76

77

00:04:48,740  -->  00:04:52,730
know whatever we put in the path will be used and we will
77

78

00:04:52,730  -->  00:04:56,380
be redirected to whatever you are l that we put in here.
78

79

00:04:57,040  -->  00:04:59,200
So let's send this one
79

80

00:04:59,940  -->  00:05:01,660
and again we're still having problems.
80

81

00:05:01,660  -->  00:05:03,680
It's saying and telling us missing parameter.
81

82

00:05:03,740  -->  00:05:06,690
Now again even if you're not getting these informative messages
82

83

00:05:06,690  -->  00:05:09,610
you should still be manipulating it this way it made sense
83

84

00:05:09,610  -->  00:05:13,290
to actually submitted this way because this was closer to
84

85

00:05:13,290  -->  00:05:16,640
the original format that the original request was sent in.
85

86

00:05:17,270  -->  00:05:19,360
Now it's complaining about the path.
86

87

00:05:19,370  -->  00:05:22,350
So what I'm gonna do is I'm actually just gonna remove this because
87

88

00:05:22,350  -->  00:05:26,680
as you can see this parameter just specifies the current product I.
88

89

00:05:26,680  -->  00:05:31,690
D. And I don't need this. All I need is the path and the location that I want to go to.
89

90

00:05:31,700  -->  00:05:36,800
So again we're just making this as simple as possible and let's send it
90

91

00:05:37,640  -->  00:05:39,690
looks like we got something we ran there
91

92

00:05:41,140  -->  00:05:45,100
and as you can see now we managed to submit that
92

93

00:05:45,100  -->  00:05:48,400
request to the local host and we are able to see
93

94

00:05:48,410  -->  00:05:51,380
the admin panel and we will be able to interact with
94

95

00:05:51,380  -->  00:05:56,740
it because the server is submitting this request on behalf of us
95

96

00:05:57,060  -->  00:05:58,890
and all of this would not be
96

97

00:05:58,890  -->  00:06:02,840
possible without discovering this open redirect vulnerability.
97

98

00:06:02,850  -->  00:06:05,520
So we're only able to do this because this
98

99

00:06:05,520  -->  00:06:09,400
website already have an open redirect vulnerability in here
99

100

00:06:09,440  -->  00:06:15,550
in which we can force the website to go to the RL that we specify in here.
100

101

00:06:15,560  -->  00:06:21,250
And then we exploited that fact by feeding this path to it in
101

102

00:06:21,260  -->  00:06:26,970
the stock api parameter which is vulnerable to a server side request forgery.
102

103

00:06:27,700  -->  00:06:29,730
This is more of just chaining of two
103

104

00:06:29,730  -->  00:06:32,940
vulnerabilities and this actually happens a lot a lot
104

105

00:06:32,940  -->  00:06:35,300
of the time people think open redirect are not
105

106

00:06:35,310  -->  00:06:39,520
that dangerous or sometimes even other smaller vulnerabilities,
106

107

00:06:39,520  -->  00:06:40,680
but yes they are.
107

108

00:06:40,680  -->  00:06:44,620
But then when they're changed with other vulnerabilities it could be much worse


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 19: Blind SSRF Vulnerabilities

0
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:04,950
in today's class, we're gonna be talking about blind S. S. R. F vulnerabilities.
1

2

00:00:05,360  -->  00:00:08,340
Now we covered normal ss ri vulnerabilities.
2

3

00:00:08,390  -->  00:00:14,260
And we said earlier that in a normal scenario you would forge a request as the user.
3

4

00:00:14,420  -->  00:00:17,760
You send it to a vulnerable web server and
4

5

00:00:17,760  -->  00:00:20,520
then that web server is gonna send that request
5

6

00:00:20,520  -->  00:00:24,370
that forged the request to another protected server and
6

7

00:00:24,370  -->  00:00:27,360
send the information back to the web server.
7

8

00:00:27,370  -->  00:00:30,110
And therefore you'll be able to see it as the user.
8

9

00:00:30,560  -->  00:00:34,090
So you're gonna request for something private that is only available
9

10

00:00:34,090  -->  00:00:37,460
and only accessible to the web server because it's trusted.
10

11

00:00:37,700  -->  00:00:41,100
The response is going to be sent back because this place is trusted the web
11

12

00:00:41,100  -->  00:00:44,470
server but you as a user you're going to be able to read it.
12

13

00:00:44,800  -->  00:00:47,940
And all of that is possible because this web server has
13

14

00:00:47,950  -->  00:00:52,730
a web application that is vulnerable to an srf vulnerability.
14

15

00:00:53,550  -->  00:00:57,960
Now we're actually doing this and we're communicating to the server through
15

16

00:00:57,960  -->  00:01:02,320
the web server because if we communicate with it directly it will
16

17

00:01:02,320  -->  00:01:06,760
refuse our connections and it won't even speak to us because this
17

18

00:01:06,760  -->  00:01:10,540
is an internal server that only speaks to the web server.
18

19

00:01:10,870  -->  00:01:14,170
That's why we have to exploit an srf vulnerability
19

20

00:01:14,180  -->  00:01:17,160
to be able to communicate with it and get the
20

21

00:01:17,160  -->  00:01:19,940
response back to a place that it trusts and
21

22

00:01:19,940  -->  00:01:22,230
therefore will be able to read it as the user
22

23

00:01:22,870  -->  00:01:26,860
in the blind scenario. And blind SSR vulnerabilities.
23

24

00:01:26,870  -->  00:01:29,200
We still have a web server that has a
24

25

00:01:29,200  -->  00:01:32,210
vulnerable web application and you're still able to send
25

26

00:01:32,210  -->  00:01:35,530
your Forest request exactly the same way the forest
26

27

00:01:35,530  -->  00:01:38,930
request will be executed exactly the same way.
27

28

00:01:38,930  -->  00:01:41,590
So it is still going to go to the private or to
28

29

00:01:41,590  -->  00:01:46,200
the protected server and it's going to execute whatever you're asking for.
29

30

00:01:46,300  -->  00:01:49,990
The only problem is there will be no response.
30

31

00:01:50,740  -->  00:01:52,660
Therefore for you as the hacker,
31

32

00:01:52,670  -->  00:01:57,280
it will be difficult for you to know if your attack was actually executed.
32

33

00:01:57,700  -->  00:02:00,880
Also in many of the scenarios that I covered previously,
33

34

00:02:00,940  -->  00:02:06,250
we were requesting private or protected information from the server
34

35

00:02:06,410  -->  00:02:10,990
and we were only able to see it because a response was sent back to the
35

36

00:02:10,990  -->  00:02:15,310
web server and we were able to see that information or these pages for example,
36

37

00:02:15,320  -->  00:02:18,990
we requested to access the admin page that is only available here.
37

38

00:02:19,030  -->  00:02:24,220
And we were only able to see it because that admin page was sent back to the web server.
38

39

00:02:24,920  -->  00:02:27,900
So the problem with the S S R F, you're gonna request the admin,
39

40

00:02:28,080  -->  00:02:31,620
this website for will actually go to the admin but nothing is sent
40

41

00:02:31,620  -->  00:02:34,990
back to it and therefore you will never be able to see it.
41

42

00:02:36,270  -->  00:02:41,750
Therefore, blind. SSR vulnerabilities can be a little bit less dangerous.
42

43

00:02:41,760  -->  00:02:43,820
So the impact is a little bit lower.
43

44

00:02:43,830  -->  00:02:49,300
But in some scenarios you'll actually be able to gain code execution or
44

45

00:02:49,300  -->  00:02:52,850
exploit vulnerabilities within the devices that
45

46

00:02:52,860  -->  00:02:54,800
are connected to the same network.
46

47

00:02:54,810  -->  00:02:59,760
So if you remember another thing that we could do with Srf is that we
47

48

00:02:59,760  -->  00:03:04,980
were able to map the internal network that this web server is connected to,
48

49

00:03:05,200  -->  00:03:08,950
and we can actually blindly send exploit payloads
49

50

00:03:08,950  -->  00:03:11,760
to these devices on this network and hope
50

51

00:03:11,760  -->  00:03:15,930
for one of them to be vulnerable so that we can get access to it.
51

52

00:03:16,860  -->  00:03:17,250
Now,
52

53

00:03:17,250  -->  00:03:21,110
there is a good chance of discovering exploits in these internal
53

54

00:03:21,110  -->  00:03:26,560
services because these services remember are not exposed to the internet,
54

55

00:03:26,570  -->  00:03:29,750
they're only internal and therefore in many cases
55

56

00:03:29,890  -->  00:03:33,060
server admins do not pay a lot of attention
56

57

00:03:33,100  -->  00:03:36,370
in keeping these services fully patched and fully secure.
57

58

00:03:36,400  -->  00:03:38,430
They only pay a lot of attention on trying to
58

59

00:03:38,430  -->  00:03:40,740
secure this and have the best firewall on it.
59

60

00:03:41,090  -->  00:03:44,710
Therefore there is a good chance of you being able to exploit a
60

61

00:03:44,710  -->  00:03:50,020
non vulnerability in internal services and then gain access to that server.
61

62

00:03:50,670  -->  00:03:52,750
Now we will talk about exploitation.
62

63

00:03:52,760  -->  00:03:58,230
But for now, let's talk about discovering blind SRF vulnerabilities.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

0
1

00:00:00,000  -->  00:00:04,260
let's talk about discovering blind srf vulnerabilities.
1

2

00:00:05,140  -->  00:00:09,530
So as we mentioned earlier, the main problem with blind SSR vulnerabilities
2

3

00:00:09,640  -->  00:00:12,020
is that it does not send a response.
3

4

00:00:12,190  -->  00:00:16,530
So the first request will be executed by the vulnerable web application.
4

5

00:00:16,540  -->  00:00:19,750
It will be sent to whatever destination we're asking it to go to.
5

6

00:00:19,850  -->  00:00:23,560
But there is no response sent back in here and therefore there
6

7

00:00:23,560  -->  00:00:27,200
is no way for us to know that our payload got executed
7

8

00:00:27,430  -->  00:00:28,660
or maybe there is
8

9

00:00:28,930  -->  00:00:32,900
what we can do. We could send a forest request
9

10

00:00:33,030  -->  00:00:37,170
to get the vulnerable web server hosting the vulnerable web application
10

11

00:00:37,500  -->  00:00:41,660
to send a request to a server that I control as the hacker.
11

12

00:00:41,970  -->  00:00:46,310
And then I can look at the logs of my server
12

13

00:00:46,320  -->  00:00:49,740
and if I see a request coming from this web server,
13

14

00:00:49,750  -->  00:00:53,300
then I will know that I managed to hijack this web server and get it
14

15

00:00:53,300  -->  00:00:58,630
to send requests to another web server that is not supposed to send requests to.
15

16

00:00:59,370  -->  00:01:01,730
And then from there we'll talk about exploitation.
16

17

00:01:01,730  -->  00:01:04,090
But like I said, we'll cover that in the next lecture.
17

18

00:01:05,090  -->  00:01:07,380
So let's go ahead and have an example of this.
18

19

00:01:08,250  -->  00:01:12,180
So we have a lab right here and as you can see this lab is rated expert.
19

20

00:01:12,190  -->  00:01:15,750
So we're just gonna run it in a new tab.
20

21

00:01:17,990  -->  00:01:21,290
And as usual, we always say that you want to test this website,
21

22

00:01:21,290  -->  00:01:26,020
test every single functionality of it, use it as a normal user and see how it works.
22

23

00:01:26,150  -->  00:01:29,510
And once you're done with this we're gonna turn on our interceptor
23

24

00:01:29,550  -->  00:01:32,210
and intercept the request and discover
24

25

00:01:32,210  -->  00:01:34,450
vulnerabilities and possibly exploit them.
25

26

00:01:34,470  -->  00:01:39,060
So let's click on a product and we have the request that is sent in here.
26

27

00:01:39,110  -->  00:01:44,050
And as usual you want to go again and test all the parameters that you have.
27

28

00:01:44,170  -->  00:01:47,420
But we're not going to be doing that because I've already tested that.
28

29

00:01:47,630  -->  00:01:53,270
And what we wanna do is we wanna play with the referral value that we have in here.
29

30

00:01:53,280  -->  00:01:54,370
So as you can see,
30

31

00:01:54,380  -->  00:01:58,890
we have a website that is being sent in here as the value for the referral.
31

32

00:01:59,240  -->  00:02:04,750
And as shown previously when you see full links like this being sent as a request,
32

33

00:02:04,760  -->  00:02:06,520
you want to play with them and test for
33

34

00:02:06,520  -->  00:02:10,860
open redirect vulnerabilities and for server side request forgeries.
34

35

00:02:10,990  -->  00:02:14,880
And we covered a number of ways of discovering server side request forgeries.
35

36

00:02:14,890  -->  00:02:17,770
I'm not going to show it right now because I don't want to waste your time,
36

37

00:02:17,780  -->  00:02:20,500
but none of them will appear to work.
37

38

00:02:20,510  -->  00:02:21,890
But they're actually working.
38

39

00:02:22,230  -->  00:02:25,020
They're not going to appear like it's working because
39

40

00:02:25,020  -->  00:02:28,540
this is an example of a blind SSR vulnerability.
40

41

00:02:28,570  -->  00:02:33,270
Therefore when you're gonna send it a request like this, it's actually gonna work.
41

42

00:02:33,280  -->  00:02:35,280
It's gonna send it to whatever you want to send it,
42

43

00:02:35,290  -->  00:02:37,990
but it's not going to send you a response.
43

44

00:02:38,230  -->  00:02:44,060
Therefore we're going to have to modify that to a location of a server that we own.
44

45

00:02:44,460  -->  00:02:47,760
So you could start any cloud server on amazon or
45

46

00:02:47,760  -->  00:02:50,880
node and put the address for that server in here.
46

47

00:02:50,890  -->  00:02:55,760
Or you could use the collaborator feature of the burp suite.
47

48

00:02:55,770  -->  00:03:01,940
Now the collaborator feature is only available in the pro version of Burp suite.
48

49

00:03:01,950  -->  00:03:04,370
So if you don't want to pay for the pro version,
49

50

00:03:04,370  -->  00:03:06,890
you're going to have to set up your own cloud instance
50

51

00:03:06,990  -->  00:03:09,610
and for this lab for this particular lab,
51

52

00:03:09,620  -->  00:03:12,030
they actually have it restricted to only work
52

53

00:03:12,030  -->  00:03:13,955
with Burp collaborator because they do I want
53

54

00:03:13,955  -->  00:03:17,705
you to be using their server to send requests to any server on the internet.
54

55

00:03:17,785  -->  00:03:18,895
So it's understandable.
55

56

00:03:19,215  -->  00:03:23,295
That's why if you want to complete the stab you will have to get the collaborator.
56

57

00:03:23,305  -->  00:03:27,455
But the collaborator is not necessary for discovering blind srf
57

58

00:03:27,455  -->  00:03:31,405
vulnerabilities you could use your own cloud instance on the internet
58

59

00:03:31,940  -->  00:03:35,960
to use the Burp collaborator. We're gonna go to the burp menu
59

60

00:03:36,120  -->  00:03:38,770
and we're gonna run collaborator client
60

61

00:03:39,270  -->  00:03:43,030
and it's asking you for the number of payloads to generate.
61

62

00:03:43,040  -->  00:03:44,730
So we're just gonna set it to one.
62

63

00:03:44,790  -->  00:03:47,670
We're going to click on copy to clipboard and this
63

64

00:03:47,670  -->  00:03:51,270
will copy the payload U R L in my clipboard.
64

65

00:03:51,280  -->  00:03:54,170
So if I send their quest to this payload.
65

66

00:03:54,170  -->  00:03:58,490
So as you can see we have a payload now that's created by bourbon collaborator.
66

67

00:03:58,590  -->  00:04:02,630
If I hit enter nothing is gonna load just the value of the payload
67

68

00:04:02,740  -->  00:04:06,120
but if we go back in here and click on paul. Now
68

69

00:04:06,400  -->  00:04:10,010
you will see the requests that were sent to this payload and
69

70

00:04:10,010  -->  00:04:13,000
it will even show you the I P that sent this request.
70

71

00:04:13,380  -->  00:04:18,269
So this is similar to you put in an address of a server that you created
71

72

00:04:18,269  -->  00:04:23,190
on the cloud and then reading the logs to see if it got any requests.
72

73

00:04:23,510  -->  00:04:25,770
So it's just an easier way of doing this.
73

74

00:04:26,160  -->  00:04:30,520
That's the whole point of the burp collaborator. It gives you a U. R. L.
74

75

00:04:30,530  -->  00:04:34,480
That you could submit anywhere when you're testing for any kind of vulnerability.
75

76

00:04:34,490  -->  00:04:41,460
And then you can simply click on paul now to see if that U. R. L. Received any requests.
76

77

00:04:42,650  -->  00:04:46,740
So now what we're gonna do, we're gonna actually clear all of this
77

78

00:04:46,890  -->  00:04:50,580
and we're gonna go back to the request that
78

79

00:04:50,580  -->  00:04:54,020
we intercepted and we're gonna remove all of this
79

80

00:04:54,790  -->  00:04:58,970
and I'm gonna paste my burp collaborator payload that I just got.
80

81

00:04:59,580  -->  00:05:01,530
So all we're doing instead of testing for
81

82

00:05:01,530  -->  00:05:05,150
local host admin or testing for internal services,
82

83

00:05:05,160  -->  00:05:09,210
we're actually putting the link for our burp collaborator U R.
83

84

00:05:09,210  -->  00:05:09,590
L.
84

85

00:05:09,600  -->  00:05:12,340
And then if a request is sent to this,
85

86

00:05:12,350  -->  00:05:13,890
I'll be able to see it in my
86

87

00:05:13,890  -->  00:05:16,630
collaborator because that's all what the collaborator does.
87

88

00:05:16,660  -->  00:05:18,710
So we're going to forward this
88

89

00:05:19,390  -->  00:05:23,630
Forward it again and let's bring back my collaborator
89

90

00:05:23,800  -->  00:05:26,400
and I'm gonna click on Paul now or it automatically
90

91

00:05:26,400  -->  00:05:28,820
did a poll because it does it every 60 seconds.
91

92

00:05:28,850  -->  00:05:32,340
And as you can see we actually got a request.
92

93

00:05:32,520  -->  00:05:35,700
So this indicates that this web application
93

94

00:05:35,710  -->  00:05:38,410
is vulnerable to a server side request forgery
94

95

00:05:38,440  -->  00:05:44,480
because we sent it a forged request to send another request to my web server.
95

96

00:05:44,520  -->  00:05:49,690
And then I checked the logs of my web server and noticed that this web
96

97

00:05:49,690  -->  00:05:53,360
application actually did fulfill my forged request
97

98

00:05:53,370  -->  00:05:56,540
and send the request to the Forest server
98

99

00:05:57,030  -->  00:05:57,990
and pen tests.
99

100

00:05:58,000  -->  00:05:59,800
And in many bug bounty programs,
100

101

00:05:59,810  -->  00:06:03,740
it's actually enough to simply show them that you managed to get the server to
101

102

00:06:03,740  -->  00:06:07,070
send the request to another server to display
102

103

00:06:07,070  -->  00:06:09,230
that there is a blind SSR vulnerability.

80
