gonna work because the admin didn't work when we loaded it like this.
55

56

00:03:08,660  -->  00:03:13,640
And as you can see sure enough it didn't work. So we're gonna do go to a PHP this time
56

57

00:03:13,800  -->  00:03:19,570
and let's see if that works and that actually works. But it's given us an air message.
57

58

00:03:19,580  -->  00:03:22,170
It's saying invalid return neural supplied.
58

59

00:03:22,780  -->  00:03:27,960
So this error message should give us a clue that this go dot PHP file
59

60

00:03:28,070  -->  00:03:32,580
probably takes some kind of value and it's some kind of A U. R. L.
60

61

00:03:32,580  -->  00:03:37,770
Because it's saying invalid return U. R. L. Now we haven't specified any return U. R.
61

62

00:03:37,770  -->  00:03:40,960
L. So it's probably expecting us to give it that.
62

63

00:03:41,370  -->  00:03:43,650
So at this stage there's gonna be a bit of guessing.
63

64

00:03:43,660  -->  00:03:46,110
So we're gonna put a question mark as usual when we want
64

65

00:03:46,110  -->  00:03:49,340
to supply information to the target and we're gonna try U.
65

66

00:03:49,340  -->  00:03:54,330
R. L. As a start. Let's just do https
66

67

00:03:55,320  -->  00:03:57,000
google dot com
67

68

00:03:58,100  -->  00:04:00,240
and enter and we're getting the same air
68

69

00:04:00,380  -->  00:04:03,940
so that means the parameter name that we used in here must
69

70

00:04:03,940  -->  00:04:07,870
be incorrect because the web application is still not accepting it.
70

71

00:04:08,350  -->  00:04:14,110
So instead of this let's go ahead and try to actually type return U. R. L.
71

72

00:04:14,120  -->  00:04:16,410
And these are actually the things that I tried myself
72

73

00:04:16,420  -->  00:04:19,649
before I discovered the bug and that still doesn't work.
73

74

00:04:19,880  -->  00:04:24,600
So I went ahead and actually did return underscore your l because that's what
74

75

00:04:24,600  -->  00:04:28,140
I would name it if I was actually programming this web page and again,
75

76

00:04:28,150  -->  00:04:29,570
it's not accepting this.
76

77

00:04:29,670  -->  00:04:30,910
So in that case,
77

78

00:04:30,910  -->  00:04:33,950
so in programming there's a number of ways to name
78

79

00:04:33,950  -->  00:04:36,430
variables if you want to include the space in it.
79

80

00:04:36,510  -->  00:04:39,550
Some people like to use underscore and others
80

81

00:04:39,550  -->  00:04:41,880
like to capitalize the next first letter.
81

82

00:04:41,890  -->  00:04:43,990
So instead of returning your l like this,
82

83

00:04:44,110  -->  00:04:47,530
they would do return capital U and then RL
83

84

00:04:47,630  -->  00:04:49,340
and if we do this one,
84

85

00:04:50,200  -->  00:04:50,820
perfect.
85

86

00:04:50,830  -->  00:04:54,770
As you can see, we get redirected to the website that we supplied,
86

87

00:04:54,780  -->  00:04:56,210
which was google dot com.
87

88

00:04:56,690  -->  00:04:58,580
So this on its own is actually a
88

89

00:04:58,580  -->  00:05:02,050
vulnerability that is called an open redirect vulnerability.
89

90

00:05:02,170  -->  00:05:06,870
I did not dedicate a lecture to it because of how simple it is. As you can see.
90

91

00:05:06,930  -->  00:05:10,120
And since this vulnerability actually left the
91

92

00:05:10,350  -->  00:05:15,140
top 10, because it is not very common, but that doesn't mean it doesn't exist.
92

93

00:05:15,140  -->  00:05:17,500
As you can see it exists because like I said,
93

94

00:05:17,510  -->  00:05:21,950
all of the vulnerabilities in this website are inspired by real vulnerabilities.
94

95

00:05:22,600  -->  00:05:26,870
So the impact of this vulnerability is gonna allow you as a hacker
95

96

00:05:27,060  -->  00:05:29,620
to craft a RL such as this
96

97

00:05:29,880  -->  00:05:33,900
and giving it to a user that trusts target dot com.
97

98

00:05:33,910  -->  00:05:36,210
So this could even be the admin of target
98

99

00:05:36,210  -->  00:05:38,800
dot com and obviously they trust their own website.
99

100

00:05:38,970  -->  00:05:42,620
So you can send it as an email or even send it to employees.
100

101

00:05:42,930  -->  00:05:46,420
And once they load Target dot com thinking they're going to end up on
101

102

00:05:46,420  -->  00:05:49,420
a trusted website because this website is
102

103

00:05:49,420  -->  00:05:52,060
vulnerable to an open redirect vulnerability.
103

104

00:05:52,240  -->  00:05:55,320
Once they execute or load this link,
104

105

00:05:55,330  -->  00:05:58,580
they're actually going to make a direct request to Evil dot
105

106

00:05:58,580  -->  00:06:01,690
com and they will end up loading Evil dot com.
106

107

00:06:02,210  -->  00:06:05,190
So the impact of this can be very severe because you
107

108

00:06:05,190  -->  00:06:08,820
could have malicious code running on evil dot com or you could
108

109

00:06:08,820  -->  00:06:11,940
have a clone of Target dot com and ask them to log
109

110

00:06:11,940  -->  00:06:15,620
in and then steal their credentials and the list goes on.
110

111

00:06:15,650  -->  00:06:19,660
So the possibilities of this being used by malicious hackers
111

112

00:06:19,670  -->  00:06:22,680
are endless and it can actually be very dangerous.
112

113

00:06:22,880  -->  00:06:24,420
And therefore in a bug hunt,
113

114

00:06:24,420  -->  00:06:28,830
you will definitely be awarded for discovering an open redirect vulnerability.
114

115

00:06:28,970  -->  00:06:32,030
And in a pen test, you should even pursue this even more.
115

116

00:06:32,040  -->  00:06:35,790
So you should actually start exploiting it and target users and
116

117

00:06:35,790  -->  00:06:39,060
admins and see how far you can take this exploit too.
117

118

00:06:39,070  -->  00:06:41,970
And obviously at the end, included in your report


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
1

00:00:00,060  -->  00:00:01,380
<v Narrator>Now, it's very important</v>
2

2

00:00:01,380  -->  00:00:02,670
to understand the difference
3

3

00:00:02,670  -->  00:00:05,310
between open redirect vulnerabilities
4

4

00:00:05,310  -->  00:00:08,100
and server-side request forgery.
5

5

00:00:08,100  -->  00:00:10,230
In an open redirect vulnerability,
6

6

00:00:10,230  -->  00:00:13,500
the result is the user is gonna make a request
7

7

00:00:13,500  -->  00:00:17,700
to the evil website specified in the malicious URL.
8

8

00:00:17,700  -->  00:00:21,810
Whereas in an SSRF or a server-side request forgery,
9

9

00:00:21,810  -->  00:00:25,080
the target web server is gonna make the request
10

10

00:00:25,080  -->  00:00:27,720
to the other server, assuming the evil server
11

11

00:00:27,720  -->  00:00:30,000
or any other kind of server.
12

12

00:00:30,000  -->  00:00:33,480
So what's happening in an open redirect vulnerability?
13

13

00:00:33,480  -->  00:00:35,370
We're sending a forged request
14

14

00:00:35,370  -->  00:00:40,020
to the target vulnerable website with a URL.
15

15

00:00:40,020  -->  00:00:42,390
And as a result, the target website
16

16

00:00:42,390  -->  00:00:46,170
is actually sending a response back to the user,
17

17

00:00:46,170  -->  00:00:50,163
telling the web browser to load the website we specified.
18

18

00:00:51,180  -->  00:00:53,370
Now, I have the load here just in red
19

19

00:00:53,370  -->  00:00:56,370
because there are a number of ways where a web server
20

20

00:00:56,370  -->  00:00:59,433
can tell a web browser to load a different website.
21

21

00:01:00,510  -->  00:01:02,880
So what's causing the web browser
22

22

00:01:02,880  -->  00:01:05,310
to load the specified website
23

23

00:01:05,310  -->  00:01:07,680
is the response that is coming back
24

24

00:01:07,680  -->  00:01:12,180
from the target website as a result of the initial request
25

25

00:01:12,180  -->  00:01:15,183
that the user sends with the URL specified.
26

26

00:01:16,020  -->  00:01:18,030
Therefore, to have a better understanding
27

27

00:01:18,030  -->  00:01:20,370
of this flow and how this is working,
28

28

00:01:20,370  -->  00:01:23,100
a good idea is to come back to Burp
29

29

00:01:23,100  -->  00:01:25,590
and enable the responses.
30

30

00:01:25,590  -->  00:01:27,300
So we're gonna go to options
31

31

00:01:27,300  -->  00:01:31,110
and click on intercept responses, we covered this before.
32

32

00:01:31,110  -->  00:01:34,710
So that we understand what exactly is being sent
33

33

00:01:34,710  -->  00:01:37,740
in this response that is causing the web browser
34

34

00:01:37,740  -->  00:01:40,800
to load a completely different website.
35

35

00:01:40,800  -->  00:01:43,260
So, let's go back to Burp,
36

36

00:01:43,260  -->  00:01:47,100
and let's go back to the interceptor.
37

37

00:01:47,100  -->  00:01:49,950
I'm gonna keep it off for now because I just wanna go back
38

38

00:01:49,950  -->  00:01:52,920
to the original website before I exploit it.
39

39

00:01:52,920  -->  00:01:57,900
We're gonna change this to return URL.
40

40

00:01:57,900  -->  00:02:01,260
I'm gonna copy it so I can load it easily in the future.
41

41

00:02:01,260  -->  00:02:03,330
I'm gonna turn on the interceptor,
42

42

00:02:03,330  -->  00:02:05,373
and we're gonna hit Enter to load it.
43

43

00:02:06,480  -->  00:02:08,730
And as you can see, the initial request is here.
44

44

00:02:08,730  -->  00:02:11,310
It's a get request to the go file.
45

45

00:02:11,310  -->  00:02:15,210
The return URL parameter is set to google.com,
46

46

00:02:15,210  -->  00:02:16,380
to what we specified.
47

47

00:02:16,380  -->  00:02:17,940
So, so far so good.
48

48

00:02:17,940  -->  00:02:22,230
So this is this request right here, going to the server.
49

49

00:02:22,230  -->  00:02:23,220
So that is fine.
50

50

00:02:23,220  -->  00:02:24,990
We're gonna forward it.
51

51

00:02:24,990  -->  00:02:28,170
And as a result, we're getting a response.
52

52

00:02:28,170  -->  00:02:30,720
This response is coming from the target website
53

53

00:02:30,720  -->  00:02:32,490
that we're exploiting.
54

54

00:02:32,490  -->  00:02:35,340
And as a result, if you look in here,
55

55

00:02:35,340  -->  00:02:39,420
you can see that it's specifying the location header.
56

56

00:02:39,420  -->  00:02:42,840
And it's setting that to the URL that we specified
57

57

00:02:42,840  -->  00:02:46,290
in our exploit, which is google.com.
58

58

00:02:46,290  -->  00:02:51,270
And for some reason, it's appending #type=0.
59

59

00:02:51,270  -->  00:02:55,020
So we actually never specified this in our payload.
60

60

00:02:55,020  -->  00:02:57,270
We never specified that in here.
61

61

00:02:57,270  -->  00:02:59,610
But for some reason in the response
62

62

00:02:59,610  -->  00:03:02,403
it's being appended to the specified URL.
63

63

00:03:03,330  -->  00:03:05,310
Now, because we see an equal sign,
64

64

00:03:05,310  -->  00:03:08,790
this means that this is another parameter that we can test,
65

65

00:03:08,790  -->  00:03:11,550
and therefore we should carry out more tests
66

66

00:03:11,550  -->  00:03:15,633
by submitting the type parameter to the URL in here.
67

67

00:03:16,602  -->  00:03:19,650
Now, if we stop the interceptor,
68

68

00:03:19,650  -->  00:03:23,220
you'll see that we will actually be redirected to google.com
69

69

00:03:23,220  -->  00:03:27,483
but the #=0 is still being appended to the URL.
70

70

00:03:28,536  -->  00:03:30,180
So this is actually something
71

71

00:03:30,180  -->  00:03:33,210
that is being sent back to the user.
72

72

00:03:33,210  -->  00:03:36,300
So the user is asking for google.com,
73

73

00:03:36,300  -->  00:03:40,500
and the response, instead of it saying load google.com,
74

74

00:03:40,500  -->  00:03:45,500
it's actually saying load google.com #type=0.
75

75

00:03:46,080  -->  00:03:47,700
So this is another parameter
76

76

00:03:47,700  -->  00:03:50,040
that we can test for other vulnerabilities.
77

77

00:03:50,040  -->  00:03:51,900
And we can manipulate just to see
78

78

00:03:51,900  -->  00:03:54,633
what impact it has on the result.
79

79

00:03:55,860  -->  00:04:00,030
So, we're gonna go back and go back in here as well,
80

80

00:04:00,030  -->  00:04:03,420
turn on the interceptor, load the same URL.
81

81

00:04:03,420  -->  00:04:05,670
And instead of setting it to zero,
82

82

00:04:05,670  -->  00:04:07,380
I'm just gonna set it to one.
83

83

00:04:07,380  -->  00:04:10,020
So it's always good to make small changes.
84

84

00:04:10,020  -->  00:04:12,990
Instead of starting to type text and starting to test
85

85

00:04:12,990  -->  00:04:14,760
for all of the other vulnerabilities
86

86

00:04:14,760  -->  00:04:17,190
that we know like XSS and all of that,
87

87

00:04:17,190  -->  00:04:19,050
let's just keep it as simple as possible.
88

88

00:04:19,050  -->  00:04:23,100
Let's just try to understand what this type parameter does.
89

89

00:04:23,100  -->  00:04:25,920
And instead of its default value, which is zero,
90

90

00:04:25,920  -->  00:04:28,230
we're just gonna set it to one.
91

91

00:04:28,230  -->  00:04:29,460
I'm gonna hit Enter,
92

92

00:04:29,460  -->  00:04:31,290
and we're gonna see the initial request.
93

93

00:04:31,290  -->  00:04:32,790
There's nothing strange about that.
94

94

00:04:32,790  -->  00:04:35,340
And here is our type parameter being appended to it,
95

95

00:04:35,340  -->  00:04:36,480
so that's good.
96

96

00:04:36,480  -->  00:04:38,790
What we're interested in is the response.
97

97

00:04:38,790  -->  00:04:43,590
So we're gonna forward, and we got our response now.
98

98

00:04:43,590  -->  00:04:45,390
And this time, as you can see,
99

99

00:04:45,390  -->  00:04:47,010
the response is a bit different.
100

100

00:04:47,010  -->  00:04:49,050
So this time in the response
101

101

00:04:49,050  -->  00:04:51,450
we actually have some JavaScript.
102

102

00:04:51,450  -->  00:04:54,570
So changing the value for the type parameter in here
103

103

00:04:54,570  -->  00:04:58,020
resulted in this change in the response.
104

104

00:04:58,020  -->  00:05:01,050
So initially we actually had location header
105

105

00:05:01,050  -->  00:05:03,210
followed by google.com.
106

106

00:05:03,210  -->  00:05:05,580
But this time we actually have this script,
107

107

00:05:05,580  -->  00:05:08,160
and the URL that we're specifying
108

108

00:05:08,160  -->  00:05:10,413
is actually being placed in here.
109

109

00:05:11,250  -->  00:05:13,380
So, whatever value we're putting in here
110

110

00:05:13,380  -->  00:05:16,470
after the URL is being put in here
111

111

00:05:16,470  -->  00:05:20,760
inside the two quotation marks which is inside a script tag.
112

112

00:05:20,760  -->  00:05:23,670
So if we can break outside of this quotation mark,
113

113

00:05:23,670  -->  00:05:26,970
we will be able to execute JavaScript.
114

114

00:05:26,970  -->  00:05:30,120
But if you look closer just for one more second,
115

115

00:05:30,120  -->  00:05:34,020
you will notice that this is specifying a href.
116

116

00:05:34,020  -->  00:05:37,170
And we covered earlier in the XSS section
117

117

00:05:37,170  -->  00:05:41,460
that inside href tags, we can actually inject JavaScript
118

118

00:05:41,460  -->  00:05:43,650
without even having to escape anything,
119

119

00:05:43,650  -->  00:05:45,720
without even having to close anything.
120

120

00:05:45,720  -->  00:05:48,270
And as I mentioned before, it's always best
121

121

00:05:48,270  -->  00:05:51,810
to try to keep our changes as small as possible,
122

122

00:05:51,810  -->  00:05:53,940
because that way we have higher chances
123

123

00:05:53,940  -->  00:05:57,810
of injecting code or exploiting vulnerabilities.
124

124

00:05:57,810  -->  00:06:01,050
So instead of trying to exit this quotation mark
125

125

00:06:01,050  -->  00:06:02,940
and then injecting code in here,
126

126

00:06:02,940  -->  00:06:05,370
I'm gonna try to inject code within this
127

127

00:06:05,370  -->  00:06:07,950
because we already have a href.
128

128

00:06:07,950  -->  00:06:12,243
And we know that we can do that by typing JavaScript:
129

129

00:06:13,380  -->  00:06:15,840
followed by the JavaScript that we want to run.
130

130

00:06:15,840  -->  00:06:18,993
And in this case, it's alert two.
131

131

00:06:20,040  -->  00:06:22,050
I'm gonna keep the type equals one,
132

132

00:06:22,050  -->  00:06:24,030
because we know if we set it to zero,
133

133

00:06:24,030  -->  00:06:25,950
the method is gonna be set to header,
134

134

00:06:25,950  -->  00:06:28,470
and we won't even have this script right here.
135

135

00:06:28,470  -->  00:06:31,740
So I'm keeping the type to one so that my code in here,
136

136

00:06:31,740  -->  00:06:34,470
the JavaScript alert will actually be placed
137

137

00:06:34,470  -->  00:06:36,240
inside this quotation mark.
138

138

00:06:36,240  -->  00:06:38,340
And because this is inside the href,
139

139

00:06:38,340  -->  00:06:41,043
my JavaScript will hopefully be executed.
140

140

00:06:41,910  -->  00:06:44,880
So, I'm actually gonna drop this,
141

141

00:06:44,880  -->  00:06:47,940
and I'm gonna paste the exploit that I just created,
142

142

00:06:47,940  -->  00:06:49,860
and I'm gonna hit Enter.
143

143

00:06:49,860  -->  00:06:51,540
And I'm gonna turn off my interceptor
144

144

00:06:51,540  -->  00:06:53,760
'cause I want the result to go ahead.
145

145

00:06:53,760  -->  00:06:56,100
And as you can see, we have our first request,
146

146

00:06:56,100  -->  00:06:57,360
so that is fine.
147

147

00:06:57,360  -->  00:06:59,340
We're gonna click on forward.
148

148

00:06:59,340  -->  00:07:02,700
And here is the response that is coming back to the browser.
149

149

00:07:02,700  -->  00:07:06,453
And as you can see, my code is being injected in here.
150

150

00:07:07,860  -->  00:07:10,920
But literally just looking at the code, we can see
151

151

00:07:10,920  -->  00:07:14,160
that this is not gonna work because as you can see
152

152

00:07:14,160  -->  00:07:15,990
we have our JavaScript in here.
153

153

00:07:15,990  -->  00:07:18,960
So that is fine and that is perfect, and it should work.
154

154

00:07:18,960  -->  00:07:22,260
But the problem is we have these values being appended
155

155

00:07:22,260  -->  00:07:23,730
to it at the end.
156

156

00:07:23,730  -->  00:07:26,130
But these values are not JavaScript,
157

157

00:07:26,130  -->  00:07:29,700
and therefore they will probably break my code.
158

158

00:07:29,700  -->  00:07:31,920
But let's forward it and see what happens.
159

159

00:07:31,920  -->  00:07:34,290
And as you can see, it didn't work.
160

160

00:07:34,290  -->  00:07:36,360
So again, as mentioned earlier,
161

161

00:07:36,360  -->  00:07:38,430
when we have values that are being appended
162

162

00:07:38,430  -->  00:07:41,490
to our code that we don't like, what we do is
163

163

00:07:41,490  -->  00:07:44,910
we put a semicolon to finish a JavaScript statement,
164

164

00:07:44,910  -->  00:07:46,980
because that's the way that JavaScript works.
165

165

00:07:46,980  -->  00:07:48,840
And we put two forward slashes
166

166

00:07:48,840  -->  00:07:51,240
to comment the rest of the code,
167

167

00:07:51,240  -->  00:07:54,090
so that anything that follows the exploit
168

168

00:07:54,090  -->  00:07:56,940
that I just added will be treated as a comment
169

169

00:07:56,940  -->  00:07:58,980
and will be ignored.
170

170

00:07:58,980  -->  00:08:00,510
So we're gonna hit Enter again.
171

171

00:08:00,510  -->  00:08:01,740
That's the first request.
172

172

00:08:01,740  -->  00:08:03,840
We're gonna forward it, it's fine.
173

173

00:08:03,840  -->  00:08:05,430
That's our second request.
174

174

00:08:05,430  -->  00:08:06,270
And as you can see,
175

175

00:08:06,270  -->  00:08:08,940
we have this being injected in the href.
176

176

00:08:08,940  -->  00:08:12,300
This is normal JavaScript code, so it should work.
177

177

00:08:12,300  -->  00:08:15,150
And anything that comes after it, which is this part
178

178

00:08:15,150  -->  00:08:18,360
is gonna be ignored because it is preceded
179

179

00:08:18,360  -->  00:08:21,180
with the two forward slashes, which will set it
180

180

00:08:21,180  -->  00:08:24,693
as a comment, basically telling the browser to ignore it.
181

181

00:08:25,530  -->  00:08:27,870
So let's forward this, and perfect.
182

182

00:08:27,870  -->  00:08:30,660
As you can see, we got our alert message
183

183

00:08:30,660  -->  00:08:32,820
executed on the target.
184

184

00:08:32,820  -->  00:08:36,300
So now we have discovered a reflected XSS
185

185

00:08:36,300  -->  00:08:40,680
in this target website in the go.php file.
186

186

00:08:41,790  -->  00:08:43,740
So a lot of people would've left this
187

187

00:08:43,740  -->  00:08:47,040
once they discovered the open redirect vulnerability,
188

188

00:08:47,040  -->  00:08:48,930
because that is an actual vulnerability
189

189

00:08:48,930  -->  00:08:51,870
and it is an actual ball, and you will get a bounty on it.
190

190

00:08:51,870  -->  00:08:54,540
But as you can see, if you spend a little bit more time
191

191

00:08:54,540  -->  00:08:56,760
trying to properly understand the flow
192

192

00:08:56,760  -->  00:08:58,950
and exchange of information that is happening
193

193

00:08:58,950  -->  00:09:02,010
between the client and the server, as you can see,
194

194

00:09:02,010  -->  00:09:05,760
we managed to discover a very dangerous reflected XSS.
195

195

00:09:05,760  -->  00:09:07,710
And in this case you have actually discovered
196

196

00:09:07,710  -->  00:09:09,213
two books instead of one.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
1

00:00:00,480  -->  00:00:02,670
<v Instructor>Now let's come back to the homepage</v>
2

2

00:00:02,670  -->  00:00:06,930
and start actually testing the features that we used before.
3

3

00:00:06,930  -->  00:00:08,520
As we know, all of these links
4

4

00:00:08,520  -->  00:00:10,320
will actually make us leave this page
5

5

00:00:10,320  -->  00:00:13,560
and take us to other pages, which we will test eventually,
6

6

00:00:13,560  -->  00:00:15,900
but for now, I wanna stick to this page
7

7

00:00:15,900  -->  00:00:19,053
and we're gonna try to login our register in here.
8

8

00:00:20,370  -->  00:00:22,560
As we did before, let's try to log in
9

9

00:00:22,560  -->  00:00:25,470
with a wrong username and password.
10

10

00:00:25,470  -->  00:00:29,040
And if we click on login, you will notice
11

11

00:00:29,040  -->  00:00:32,340
that we actually have a parameter called act in here.
12

12

00:00:32,340  -->  00:00:34,080
Now, in other cases, you might not see
13

13

00:00:34,080  -->  00:00:37,740
this parameter in here, but the fact that we are logging in
14

14

00:00:37,740  -->  00:00:40,380
means that information is being exchanged
15

15

00:00:40,380  -->  00:00:42,180
and sent to the web server,
16

16

00:00:42,180  -->  00:00:44,310
and, therefore, if you don't see that in here,
17

17

00:00:44,310  -->  00:00:46,860
you should intercept the connection with Burp
18

18

00:00:46,860  -->  00:00:48,900
and then analyze what's happening.
19

19

00:00:48,900  -->  00:00:52,080
Analyze the data that is being sent and modified.
20

20

00:00:52,080  -->  00:00:53,880
Well, in our case, we already see this.
21

21

00:00:53,880  -->  00:00:56,250
We already see act is equal to log in.
22

22

00:00:56,250  -->  00:00:59,370
So we already have a value in here, which is login,
23

23

00:00:59,370  -->  00:01:02,130
that we can test for all of the vulnerabilities
24

24

00:01:02,130  -->  00:01:04,260
that we learned before.
25

25

00:01:04,260  -->  00:01:07,620
And before I do that, I'm actually just gonna put something
26

26

00:01:07,620  -->  00:01:10,770
very distinctive so I can find it and see
27

27

00:01:10,770  -->  00:01:13,230
if this value, that is being typed in here,
28

28

00:01:13,230  -->  00:01:16,200
if it's being reflected on the page somewhere.
29

29

00:01:16,200  -->  00:01:20,343
So we're typing in login, and let me just type testtest.
30

30

00:01:21,810  -->  00:01:23,040
And I'm gonna copy this term
31

31

00:01:23,040  -->  00:01:24,900
because I'm gonna need to search for it,
32

32

00:01:24,900  -->  00:01:26,223
and I'm gonna hit enter.
33

33

00:01:27,690  -->  00:01:29,550
So, as you can see, the page loads normally.
34

34

00:01:29,550  -->  00:01:32,820
There's nothing weird in the page at all,
35

35

00:01:32,820  -->  00:01:35,310
but, as we know, this might be injected
36

36

00:01:35,310  -->  00:01:38,190
somewhere in the page that is not visible in here.
37

37

00:01:38,190  -->  00:01:41,430
We're gonna have to look at the page source to see it.
38

38

00:01:41,430  -->  00:01:44,370
So you could either right-click and View Page Source
39

39

00:01:44,370  -->  00:01:46,710
or you could Inspect any element,
40

40

00:01:46,710  -->  00:01:48,960
and that will reveal the page source.
41

41

00:01:48,960  -->  00:01:51,660
And from here, you can do Control + F
42

42

00:01:51,660  -->  00:01:54,093
and then paste whatever you're looking for.
43

43

00:01:55,350  -->  00:01:57,750
Now this will only search within the code,
44

44

00:01:57,750  -->  00:02:00,810
but you can see, even at the start before the code
45

45

00:02:00,810  -->  00:02:04,290
of the page starts, before even the HTML tag,
46

46

00:02:04,290  -->  00:02:06,750
you actually have a comment in here.
47

47

00:02:06,750  -->  00:02:10,080
And in the comment you have whatever we injected,
48

48

00:02:10,080  -->  00:02:13,230
the logintesttest, is being inserted
49

49

00:02:13,230  -->  00:02:15,330
in this part of the page.
50

50

00:02:15,330  -->  00:02:17,760
So a better way of finding this would be to right-click
51

51

00:02:17,760  -->  00:02:19,110
and View Page Source.
52

52

00:02:19,110  -->  00:02:21,510
And if you do Control + F and paste it,
53

53

00:02:21,510  -->  00:02:24,903
you will find it in here at the very top of the page.
54

54

00:02:26,340  -->  00:02:28,560
Now, previously, we learned we can use
55

55

00:02:28,560  -->  00:02:32,070
double forward slashes as a comment for JavaScript.
56

56

00:02:32,070  -->  00:02:34,110
We also learned that we can use a hash,
57

57

00:02:34,110  -->  00:02:35,910
and double forward slashes, and others,
58

58

00:02:35,910  -->  00:02:38,760
for SQL when we're doing SQL injections.
59

59

00:02:38,760  -->  00:02:41,790
And in HTML, you can also add comments
60

60

00:02:41,790  -->  00:02:44,100
using this syntax right here.
61

61

00:02:44,100  -->  00:02:47,280
So this would identify the start of a comment,
62

62

00:02:47,280  -->  00:02:50,940
and this will identify the end of the comment.
63

63

00:02:50,940  -->  00:02:53,070
And everything that you see within it
64

64

00:02:53,070  -->  00:02:55,680
is actually a HTML comment
65

65

00:02:55,680  -->  00:02:58,620
and, therefore, whatever you type in here,
66

66

00:02:58,620  -->  00:03:02,910
will never be considered as part of the page.
67

67

00:03:02,910  -->  00:03:05,940
So similar to everything that we learned before
68

68

00:03:05,940  -->  00:03:08,430
when discovering bugs, we wanna break out
69

69

00:03:08,430  -->  00:03:10,530
of the constraint that we are in.
70

70

00:03:10,530  -->  00:03:12,930
And currently, the constraint is the fact
71

71

00:03:12,930  -->  00:03:17,930
that whatever we're typing is being placed inside a comment.
72

72

00:03:18,060  -->  00:03:21,000
So if we wanted to break out of this comment
73

73

00:03:21,000  -->  00:03:24,660
and have this text being considered as part of the page,
74

74

00:03:24,660  -->  00:03:27,600
we're just gonna proceed it with these characters
75

75

00:03:27,600  -->  00:03:29,790
so that it is outside of the comment
76

76

00:03:29,790  -->  00:03:32,400
and that the comment ends in here.
77

77

00:03:32,400  -->  00:03:34,413
So I'm literally just gonna copy this,
78

78

00:03:35,430  -->  00:03:38,400
and we're gonna paste them right before
79

79

00:03:38,400  -->  00:03:40,203
the value that I am injecting.
80

80

00:03:41,100  -->  00:03:44,040
I'm gonna hit enter and perfect.
81

81

00:03:44,040  -->  00:03:47,280
As you can see now, I have my logintesttest
82

82

00:03:47,280  -->  00:03:50,910
is actually being considered as part of the page.
83

83

00:03:50,910  -->  00:03:54,210
So now that we are able to break outside of the comment
84

84

00:03:54,210  -->  00:03:58,260
and our text being placed inside the body of the page,
85

85

00:03:58,260  -->  00:04:02,040
let's try and see if we have the HTML injection.
86

86

00:04:02,040  -->  00:04:04,170
So to do that, as we did before,
87

87

00:04:04,170  -->  00:04:09,170
we're gonna be put in a HTML tag before and after our text.
88

88

00:04:09,450  -->  00:04:12,240
So I'm just using the &lt;b&gt; tag in here.
89

89

00:04:12,240  -->  00:04:14,730
And then if I hit enter, as you can see,
90

90

00:04:14,730  -->  00:04:16,770
the text is bold now.
91

91

00:04:16,770  -->  00:04:19,470
So instead of being a normal text, now it's bold,
92

92

00:04:19,470  -->  00:04:22,320
and we don't see the HTML tags that I inserted.
93

93

00:04:22,320  -->  00:04:25,830
And, again, if I just right-click and Inspect this element,
94

94

00:04:25,830  -->  00:04:28,620
you can see right now it's actually being placed
95

95

00:04:28,620  -->  00:04:31,860
inside the &lt;b&gt; tags that I added myself.
96

96

00:04:31,860  -->  00:04:35,640
And, therefore, because I am able to inject HTML code
97

97

00:04:35,640  -->  00:04:39,360
in here, that means I have discovered a HTML injection,
98

98

00:04:39,360  -->  00:04:41,340
and in most bug bounty programs,
99

99

00:04:41,340  -->  00:04:43,563
you will be awarded a bounty for this.
100

100

00:04:44,490  -->  00:04:47,280
So now that we discovered the HTML injection,
101

101

00:04:47,280  -->  00:04:50,190
as mentioned earlier, we always try to see
102

102

00:04:50,190  -->  00:04:54,390
if we can escalate this to an XSS.
103

103

00:04:54,390  -->  00:04:56,580
And to do that, we're simply gonna replace
104

104

00:04:56,580  -->  00:05:01,110
this with a script, and we're gonna type
105

105

00:05:01,110  -->  00:05:04,683
our alert code in here, so let's say alert2.
106

106

00:05:06,420  -->  00:05:09,900
And then finally, we're gonna close it with another script.
107

107

00:05:09,900  -->  00:05:13,320
And I'm actually gonna remove this last tag from here
108

108

00:05:13,320  -->  00:05:16,560
because, as you can see, the web application
109

109

00:05:16,560  -->  00:05:20,790
is automatically appending the closing tag in here.
110

110

00:05:20,790  -->  00:05:22,620
And, as we mentioned earlier,
111

111

00:05:22,620  -->  00:05:26,700
it is always best to do the least amount of changes
112

112

00:05:26,700  -->  00:05:29,250
and let the web application close tags for you,
113

113

00:05:29,250  -->  00:05:33,630
or close the comment, or just let it complete your exploit.
114

114

00:05:33,630  -->  00:05:35,850
So that's why I'm leaving the script tag open.
115

115

00:05:35,850  -->  00:05:37,410
You can obviously test with both,
116

116

00:05:37,410  -->  00:05:39,260
but that's why I'm doing it this way.
117

117

00:05:40,110  -->  00:05:42,033
So we're gonna send it as is.
118

118

00:05:44,820  -->  00:05:47,910
And, as you can see, we have our alert being injected
119

119

00:05:47,910  -->  00:05:51,240
in here, but if we inspect the element,
120

120

00:05:51,240  -->  00:05:54,090
you will see that we have the alert on its own,
121

121

00:05:54,090  -->  00:05:57,420
and the script tags that we added are being stripped.
122

122

00:05:57,420  -->  00:06:00,180
So that tells me that this website is using
123

123

00:06:00,180  -->  00:06:03,600
some kind of a filtering that is stopping script tags
124

124

00:06:03,600  -->  00:06:06,630
from being injected in the HTML.
125

125

00:06:06,630  -->  00:06:08,610
But that's not enough to make me give up
126

126

00:06:08,610  -->  00:06:11,790
because, as we saw earlier in the XSS section,
127

127

00:06:11,790  -->  00:06:14,310
there is so many ways to bypass filters,
128

128

00:06:14,310  -->  00:06:16,770
and we even covered how to use the Intruder
129

129

00:06:16,770  -->  00:06:19,470
to automatically go through a large list
130

130

00:06:19,470  -->  00:06:22,980
of tags, and parameters, and then exploits.
131

131

00:06:22,980  -->  00:06:25,140
We covered all of that step-by-step.
132

132

00:06:25,140  -->  00:06:26,880
So if you don't remember how to do it,
133

133

00:06:26,880  -->  00:06:29,850
please go back and revise that lecture.
134

134

00:06:29,850  -->  00:06:32,730
But what I'm gonna try, if you wanna keep this manual,
135

135

00:06:32,730  -->  00:06:34,140
if I'm actually playing with this,
136

136

00:06:34,140  -->  00:06:37,890
I actually would try a few manual things before I go to Burp
137

137

00:06:37,890  -->  00:06:39,960
because that will send a lot of requests,
138

138

00:06:39,960  -->  00:06:42,750
and cause a lot of logs, and, in many cases,
139

139

00:06:42,750  -->  00:06:44,970
I'd be able to get it manually.
140

140

00:06:44,970  -->  00:06:49,970
So a very common or fun tag that we can try is the image tag
141

141

00:06:49,980  -->  00:06:52,230
because that doesn't require closing.
142

142

00:06:52,230  -->  00:06:55,680
And within the image tag, we can actually do the source,
143

143

00:06:55,680  -->  00:06:58,620
so the src to something that does not exist.
144

144

00:06:58,620  -->  00:07:02,040
So, for example, x, and then say onerror,
145

145

00:07:02,040  -->  00:07:03,900
so whenever you get an error,
146

146

00:07:03,900  -->  00:07:06,780
I want you to execute some JavaScript code.
147

147

00:07:06,780  -->  00:07:09,420
And, in this case, I want you to do an alert
148

148

00:07:09,420  -->  00:07:10,893
and show me number two.
149

149

00:07:11,790  -->  00:07:13,110
So very, very simple.
150

150

00:07:13,110  -->  00:07:14,790
We're injecting an image tag,
151

151

00:07:14,790  -->  00:07:16,740
we're giving it the source of the image
152

152

00:07:16,740  -->  00:07:19,170
to something that is wrong, that doesn't exist,
153

153

00:07:19,170  -->  00:07:21,960
and we're saying when that happens onerror,
154

154

00:07:21,960  -->  00:07:25,350
I want you to execute this JavaScript code.
155

155

00:07:25,350  -->  00:07:27,303
Let's hit enter and see if this works.
156

156

00:07:28,560  -->  00:07:31,530
And, as you can see, it is injecting the image,
157

157

00:07:31,530  -->  00:07:33,930
and the image is not loading because, you know,
158

158

00:07:33,930  -->  00:07:36,840
the source that I gave it is wrong, which is x,
159

159

00:07:36,840  -->  00:07:40,650
but for some reason, it's filtering or removing
160

160

00:07:40,650  -->  00:07:43,080
the onerror event that we have in here
161

161

00:07:43,080  -->  00:07:45,690
because we don't see it in here.
162

162

00:07:45,690  -->  00:07:48,060
So, again, we covered how to do that with Burp
163

163

00:07:48,060  -->  00:07:49,800
before using a cheat sheet.
164

164

00:07:49,800  -->  00:07:51,900
How we could build it one step at a time.
165

165

00:07:51,900  -->  00:07:54,450
So inject the image first, inject the source second,
166

166

00:07:54,450  -->  00:07:56,280
and then inject different events
167

167

00:07:56,280  -->  00:07:58,980
until you see the one that passes it.
168

168

00:07:58,980  -->  00:08:01,020
But if we're playing with this manually,
169

169

00:08:01,020  -->  00:08:03,510
just quickly, just to get a feel of it,
170

170

00:08:03,510  -->  00:08:06,000
we can see that the onerror is being filtered,
171

171

00:08:06,000  -->  00:08:09,330
but we can also see that the web application
172

172

00:08:09,330  -->  00:08:13,980
is appending this to our image, to our payload.
173

173

00:08:13,980  -->  00:08:16,800
So I actually don't need to close this tag,
174

174

00:08:16,800  -->  00:08:20,490
and hopefully if I don't do this, the filter will fail.
175

175

00:08:20,490  -->  00:08:23,070
So let's do it and perfect.
176

176

00:08:23,070  -->  00:08:26,910
As you can see, we managed to get the target web application
177

177

00:08:26,910  -->  00:08:30,720
to execute the JavaScript that we specified in here.
178

178

00:08:30,720  -->  00:08:32,760
And this is why manual testing
179

179

00:08:32,760  -->  00:08:35,340
is actually superior to automation.
180

180

00:08:35,340  -->  00:08:38,160
So use an Intruder and all that is actually really good
181

181

00:08:38,160  -->  00:08:39,810
because it's actually a hybrid
182

182

00:08:39,810  -->  00:08:42,690
between manual testing and automation.
183

183

00:08:42,690  -->  00:08:45,780
So once you start playing with the code enough,
184

184

00:08:45,780  -->  00:08:49,050
you can actually have your Intruder submitting the exploits
185

185

00:08:49,050  -->  00:08:52,470
without the closing tag because you know that your target
186

186

00:08:52,470  -->  00:08:54,093
does not need the closing tag.

3

4

10

11

14

15

16

17

17

18

19

19

23

00:00:49,050  -->  00:00:50,520

24

that can give us a lot of clues.

25

26

00:00:55,560  -->  00:00:56,992
and that's the order ID of it.

27

00:00:56,992  -->  00:01:01,410
All of these input boxes are being populated by values
28

28

00:01:01,410  -->  00:01:04,470
that we sent originally in the original form.
29

29

00:01:04,470  -->  00:01:07,080
So, if we, for example, right click this,
30

30

00:01:07,080  -->  00:01:09,120
and inspect the element,
31

31

33

33

00:01:15,300  -->  00:01:16,950
So whatever we're putting in here,
34

34

00:01:16,950  -->  00:01:19,380
is being reflected in the page somewhere.
35

35

00:01:19,380  -->  00:01:22,260
It is inside an input box inside the value.
36

36

00:01:22,260  -->  00:01:24,150
But, as we learned previously,
37

37

39

So same goes for the email address,

41

and also same goes for the date.

43

50

51

51

52

54

So, if we intercept the original request,

55

and change its value in Burp to whatever we want,
56

56

57

57

00:02:18,930  -->  00:02:22,083

58

So, let's go back, close this,

59

and let's go back to the original booking page.

60

So we're clicking on, "Book Now."

61

62

63

actually really doesn't matter this time,

64

65

00:02:40,710  -->  00:02:42,990

66

66

67

67

68

70

this is not a very common vulnerability.

74

75

00:03:06,750  -->  00:03:09,840
But, what's interesting is what we have in here,
76

76

00:03:09,840  -->  00:03:10,740

79

80

all of these values are being injected

81

82

And therefore, to test for JavaScript injection,
83

83

00:03:27,450  -->  00:03:30,750
we're gonna, first of all, close the value with a quote,
84

84

00:03:30,750  -->  00:03:33,930

85

00:03:33,930  -->  00:03:36,150
and then add our script,

86

00:03:36,150  -->  00:03:39,390
and we're gonna do alert two,

87

00:03:39,390  -->  00:03:41,103
and we're gonna finish the script.

88

Now, ideally, you'd wanna test one parameter at a time,
89

89

00:03:45,990  -->  00:03:49,230
to see how the website is behaving.
90

90

00:03:49,230  -->  00:03:51,960
But I'm actually just gonna send it to the three parameters.

91

00:03:51,960  -->  00:03:53,280

92

92

00:03:53,280  -->  00:03:54,690
when I'm testing really quickly.

93

94

94

00:03:58,200  -->  00:03:59,070
on its own.

95

And as we saw earlier, once you get some clues,

from the cheat sheet and build it up one element at a time,

98

99

99

until you discover the vulnerability.

100

101

103

103

104

And we're gonna stop intercepting it,
105

105

because we don't wanna do that.
106

106

00:04:23,670  -->  00:04:25,650
And perfect, as you can see,

107

00:04:25,650  -->  00:04:28,290
we are getting our code to be executed.
108

108

00:04:28,290  -->  00:04:31,470
So we have our JavaScript code injected in the page
109

109

00:04:31,470  -->  00:04:33,360
and therefore we have discovered,
110

110

00:04:33,360  -->  00:04:36,266
a reflected XSS vulnerability.
111

111

00:04:36,266  -->  00:04:38,037
Now, if I click on, "Okay,"
112

112

00:04:38,037  -->  00:04:39,467
as you can see now,
113

113

00:04:39,467  -->  00:04:42,090
I can see the JavaScript in here
114

114

00:04:42,090  -->  00:04:45,450
is actually being placed within the input box.
115

115

00:04:45,450  -->  00:04:47,610
So that means this input is not vulnerable
116

116

00:04:47,610  -->  00:04:50,670
because the web application knows that this is just part
117

117

00:04:50,670  -->  00:04:53,160
of the input, it is not code.
118

118

00:04:53,160  -->  00:04:56,760
Same goes for this one. It's being placed within the input.

119

00:04:56,760  -->  00:05:00,420
It knows that this is not code, it's part of the input.
120

120

00:05:00,420  -->  00:05:03,510
But in the date, we actually don't see anything in here.
121

121

00:05:03,510  -->  00:05:07,860
The script that we pasted is not actually being displayed
122

122

00:05:07,860  -->  00:05:08,940
in here.
123

123

And if we right click and inspect the element,

124

you will see we have the date that was selected.
125

125

00:05:16,140  -->  00:05:18,690
And then because we closed the value,
126

126

00:05:18,690  -->  00:05:20,700
and we opened up our own script,
127

127

00:05:20,700  -->  00:05:23,338
you can see our own script is being treated
128

128

00:05:23,338  -->  00:05:26,190
as code, as part of the page,
129

129

00:05:26,190  -->  00:05:29,073
and therefore it's being executed on the page.
130

130

00:05:29,910  -->  00:05:31,170
So there you go.
131

131

00:05:31,170  -->  00:05:33,330
The date parameter, even though it seems like
132

132

00:05:33,330  -->  00:05:35,550
you can't put any code or any text in it,
133

133

00:05:35,550  -->  00:05:37,140
once intercepted in Burp,
134

134

00:05:37,140  -->  00:05:39,870
we're able to put anything and inject anything within it.
135

135

00:05:39,870  -->  00:05:41,066
And as you can see,
136

136

the programmer probably filtered these inputs thinking
137

138

139

But as you can see, we managed to inject code
140

140

in it by simply intercepting the data in Burp.

Because if you paid close attention

3

to when we were using that feature originally

4

you would remember that there was a lot of
4

5

data being exchanged between the client and the server
5

6

00:00:15,420  -->  00:00:18,225
until we ended up at the confirmed page.
6

7

00:00:18,235  -->  00:00:21,925
And therefore that means there is more tests to be carried out.

8

00:00:21,935  -->  00:00:24,955

8

9

9

10

in the previous lecture so we're gonna ignore them this time.

And now we have another request. So it's a get request.

It's being sent to confirm two dot PHP and we have a parameter called order I. D.

22

23

00:01:16,293  -->  00:01:18,813
So this is pretty interesting because this parameter
23

24

00:01:18,813  -->  00:01:20,843
is actually in the middle of a flow.

25

00:01:20,903  -->  00:01:25,203
I'm actually just going to send it to the repeater real quick just to see if
25

26

00:01:25,203  -->  00:01:29,596

00:02:14,100  -->  00:02:17,660
And with experience, once you see something that ends with an equal sign,
40

41

00:02:17,785  -->  00:02:23,675
That is a clue that it might be encoded with an encoding that is called Base 64.
41

42

00:02:23,685  -->  00:02:27,585
Now the nice thing about Burp, you can simply highlight any text you want
42

43

00:02:27,865  -->  00:02:31,825
right click and then you can convert that. So you can encode or decode
43

44

00:02:32,025  -->  00:02:33,115
the selected text.
44

45

00:02:33,125  -->  00:02:36,015
And as you can see we have base 64 in here and if I'm
45

46

00:02:36,015  -->  00:02:41,145
suspecting that this text is base 64 I'm actually going to click on decode
46

47

00:02:41,295  -->  00:02:45,710
to get the original value. And as you can see the original value is an actual number.
47

48

00:02:46,030  -->  00:02:49,350
And that number corresponds to the order number.
48

49

00:02:49,360  -->  00:02:51,800
So now we understand that the confirmed dot
49

50

00:02:51,800  -->  00:02:54,860
PHP file takes a parameter called order I.
50

51

00:02:54,860  -->  00:03:00,240
D. That is followed by the actual order I. D. But the order I. D. Is encoded.
51

52

00:03:00,250  -->  00:03:03,240
So in this case instead of seeing the actual number
52

53

00:03:03,240  -->  00:03:06,160
we were seeing the encoded version of the order I.
53

54

00:03:06,160  -->  00:03:06,540
D.
54

55

00:03:07,210  -->  00:03:11,360
So just by being curious and just employing this researcher
55

56

00:03:11,360  -->  00:03:15,240
or bug hunter mentality before you even try to discover
56

57

all of the vulnerabilities that we learned before and before
57

58

you start trying to send some payloads in here.

67

00:03:53,562  -->  00:03:54,672
base 64
68

69

00:03:54,982  -->  00:03:56,612
this time we're going to encode it.
69

70

00:03:56,622  -->  00:04:01,402
And as you can see now the text actually seems similar to the original one that we had
70

71

00:04:01,532  -->  00:04:05,462
and it also ends with an equal sign because that's what I said before.
71

72

00:04:05,472  -->  00:04:08,002
When you see text that ends with an equal sign,
72

73

00:04:08,012  -->  00:04:11,722
it's a hint that it might be encoded in base 64.
73

74

00:04:11,912  -->  00:04:13,902
So let's send it like this
74

75

00:04:14,520  -->  00:04:16,240
and it looks like it worked.
75

76

00:04:16,339  -->  00:04:21,490
As you can see we're able to access an order that was submitted by someone else.
76

77

00:04:21,500  -->  00:04:23,810
So in this case someone that is named Deidra
77

78

00:04:23,820  -->  00:04:27,470
and we can see her email in here and therefore
78

79

00:04:27,480  -->  00:04:31,530
we're actually able to load orders that were submitted
79

80

00:04:31,540  -->  00:04:34,810
by another user which we're not supposed to see.
80

81

00:04:34,860  -->  00:04:38,795
And therefore this is an example of a broken Access control.
81

82

00:04:38,805  -->  00:04:41,325
And as mentioned before this is a very very common
82

83

00:04:41,325  -->  00:04:44,985
bug of our 94% of websites are vulnerable to it.
83

84

00:04:44,995  -->  00:04:49,455
So the fact that we discovered in here is not surprising at all and
84

85

00:04:49,455  -->  00:04:53,455
that's why in pretty much any bug bounty program that I've seen before,
85

86

00:04:53,465  -->  00:04:57,095
it will actually be rewarded a bounty for discovering such bugs
86

87

00:04:57,105  -->  00:05:00,890
because we're able to load data that belongs to other users.
87

88

00:05:01,120  -->  00:05:03,630
Another thing that should draw your attention is
88

89

00:05:03,630  -->  00:05:05,450
the fact that we're seeing the order I.
89

90

00:05:05,450  -->  00:05:06,190
D. In here.
90

91

00:05:06,200  -->  00:05:11,800
So I want to see if this value can be manipulated by changing the value in here.
91

92

00:05:11,810  -->  00:05:17,770
So let me just type some normal text like test test and then we're going to encode it
92

93

00:05:18,990  -->  00:05:20,640
To base 64
93

94

00:05:21,350  -->  00:05:22,650
and we're going to send it
94

95

00:05:22,850  -->  00:05:24,530
and the website fails to load.
95

96

00:05:24,540  -->  00:05:28,520
So this tells me that this order idea that we're seeing in here
96

97

00:05:28,640  -->  00:05:30,630
is being read from somewhere else.
97

98

00:05:30,760  -->  00:05:35,500
And therefore if I put any text in here, including javascript or html text,
98

99

00:05:35,510  -->  00:05:37,720
it's simply just going to break the application.
99

100

00:05:37,760  -->  00:05:40,970
It's not actually going to be displayed in here and therefore we
100

101

00:05:40,970  -->  00:05:45,030
can't find html injection or an EX Ss vulnerability in here.
101

102

00:05:45,040  -->  00:05:46,540
But as you said earlier,
102

103

00:05:46,550  -->  00:05:49,560
we do have a broken access control vulnerability in
103

104

00:05:49,560  -->  00:05:52,730
which you will definitely be awarded a bounty for.

within all of the files that it has intercepted.

51

00:02:12,450  -->  00:02:15,660

53

00:02:15,660  -->  00:02:17,700
not even in cyber security only,

54

00:02:17,700  -->  00:02:18,930

58

59

I know, all sounds confusing,

60

61

00:02:34,530  -->  00:02:35,880
to discover endpoints,
62

62

00:02:35,880  -->  00:02:37,593
and you'll see how easy it is.
63

63

00:02:38,970  -->  00:02:42,990
So basically, the goal is, I wanna see any other parameters
64

64

00:02:42,990  -->  00:02:46,440
that I can give values to and manipulate
65

65

00:02:46,440  -->  00:02:49,380
in order to discover bugs and vulnerabilities.
66

66

00:02:49,380  -->  00:02:52,407
So we learned before, even at the start of the course,
67

67

00:02:52,407  -->  00:02:55,590
we always look for something like a question mark,
68

68

00:02:55,590  -->  00:02:57,000
followed by some text,
69

69

00:02:57,000  -->  00:02:59,250
followed by an equal sign.
70

70

00:02:59,250  -->  00:03:01,530
So, what I wanna do basically,
71

71

00:03:01,530  -->  00:03:02,610
I wanna tell burp,
72

72

00:03:02,610  -->  00:03:05,790
to find any sequence of characters,
73

73

00:03:05,790  -->  00:03:07,830
that matches this pattern.
74

74

00:03:07,830  -->  00:03:09,450
I want a question mark,
75

75

00:03:09,450  -->  00:03:11,310
followed by some text in here,
76

76

00:03:11,310  -->  00:03:13,740
so let's just say X, X, X, X, X,
77

77

00:03:13,740  -->  00:03:15,406
and followed by an equal sign.
78

78

00:03:15,406  -->  00:03:17,700
I wanted to search all of the files
79

79

00:03:17,700  -->  00:03:20,250
that it intercepted in the target website.
80

80

00:03:20,250  -->  00:03:21,630
And if it find any of them,
81

81

00:03:21,630  -->  00:03:23,100
I wanted to show it to me,
82

82

00:03:23,100  -->  00:03:25,650
so that I can test these parameters
83

83

00:03:25,650  -->  00:03:28,020
for more bugs and vulnerabilities.
84

84

00:03:28,020  -->  00:03:31,770
And we can do that very easily using regular expressions.
85

85

00:03:31,770  -->  00:03:33,750
So first of all, I'm just gonna show you,
86

86

00:03:33,750  -->  00:03:35,280
we're gonna go to the target.
87

87

00:03:35,280  -->  00:03:36,960
We're gonna click on the filter,
88

88

00:03:36,960  -->  00:03:40,740
and we're gonna enable the Regex, text box in here.
89

89

00:03:40,740  -->  00:03:42,060
So, we're telling burp,
90

90

00:03:42,060  -->  00:03:43,950
I'm not gonna search for a normal text,
91

91

00:03:43,950  -->  00:03:46,110
I'm gonna search for regex.
92

92

00:03:46,110  -->  00:03:49,260
And like I said, we wanted to start with a question mark.
93

93

00:03:49,260  -->  00:03:51,570
So, we're gonna put a question mark,
94

94

00:03:51,570  -->  00:03:54,743
and then we wanna match as many characters as possible,
95

95

00:03:54,743  -->  00:03:57,400
followed by an equal sign.
96

96

00:03:57,400  -->  00:04:00,645
And here's where you're gonna see the power of regex.
97

97

00:04:00,645  -->  00:04:02,040
We're gonna tell burp,
98

98

00:04:02,040  -->  00:04:05,130
to match as many characters as possible
99

99

00:04:05,130  -->  00:04:06,270
between a question mark,
100

100

00:04:06,270  -->  00:04:11,270
and between an equal sign by basically saying, dot star.
101

101

00:04:11,940  -->  00:04:15,660
So, dot means that I want to match any type of character,
102

102

00:04:15,660  -->  00:04:19,320
and star means any number of characters.
103

103

00:04:19,320  -->  00:04:20,580
So if I just put one dot,
104

104

00:04:20,580  -->  00:04:22,440
it's gonna match only one character,
105

105

00:04:22,440  -->  00:04:24,570
but I don't care how many characters are there.
106

106

00:04:24,570  -->  00:04:26,310
As long as they are enclosed
107

107

00:04:26,310  -->  00:04:29,731
between a question mark and an equal sign.
108

108

00:04:29,731  -->  00:04:31,680
Now, as you can see,
109

109

00:04:31,680  -->  00:04:34,110
in regex, you can use dots and stars,
110

110

00:04:34,110  -->  00:04:35,940
so you can use special characters
111

111

00:04:35,940  -->  00:04:37,830
to indicate different meanings.
112

112

00:04:37,830  -->  00:04:40,560
So the question mark can actually be used
113

113

00:04:40,560  -->  00:04:43,653
to stop matching before the end of the line.
114

114

00:04:44,850  -->  00:04:48,333
So, to stop matching once this pattern is found,
115

115

00:04:49,350  -->  00:04:52,170
but we actually don't want to say that in here.
116

116

00:04:52,170  -->  00:04:53,460
We want this question mark
117

117

00:04:53,460  -->  00:04:56,010
to be part of the regular expression.
118

118

00:04:56,010  -->  00:04:58,410
Like we said, because we're looking for a question mark,
119

119

00:04:58,410  -->  00:05:00,343
followed by a series of characters,
120

120

00:05:00,343  -->  00:05:02,520
followed by an equal.
121

121

00:05:02,520  -->  00:05:04,710
And therefore, to tell burp,
122

122

00:05:04,710  -->  00:05:07,920
to not treat this question mark as a special character,
123

123

00:05:07,920  -->  00:05:10,634
and to actually treat it as part of the pattern,
124

124

00:05:10,634  -->  00:05:14,430
I'm gonna have to put a backward slash before it.
125

125

00:05:14,430  -->  00:05:16,800
And finally, we wanna tell burp,
126

126

00:05:16,800  -->  00:05:18,270
to not continue matching,
127

127

00:05:18,270  -->  00:05:21,060
because remember, it's actually gonna search for this
128

128

00:05:21,060  -->  00:05:24,390
in code, and there could be a lot of lines of code.
129

129

00:05:24,390  -->  00:05:27,480
So, I wanted to stop looking for this pattern.
130

130

00:05:27,480  -->  00:05:29,610
Once this pattern is satisfied,
131

131

00:05:29,610  -->  00:05:31,830
I don't want it to continue matching all the way
132

132

00:05:31,830  -->  00:05:33,720
up to the end of the line.
133

133

00:05:33,720  -->  00:05:36,323
And that's why I'm actually gonna use a question mark
134

134

00:05:36,323  -->  00:05:37,293
in here.
135

135

00:05:38,610  -->  00:05:41,460
So basically, just to make this simpler,
136

136

00:05:41,460  -->  00:05:45,510
we're using a dot, star to say, I wanna search
137

137

00:05:45,510  -->  00:05:47,760
for any sequence of characters,
138

138

00:05:47,760  -->  00:05:49,950
for any pattern that matches this,
139

139

00:05:49,950  -->  00:05:53,130
as long as it starts with a question mark,
140

140

00:05:53,130  -->  00:05:55,410
and ends with an equal sign,
141

141

00:05:55,410  -->  00:05:57,810
which is exactly, what we want,
142

142

00:05:57,810  -->  00:06:00,330
when we are looking for parameters
143

143

00:06:00,330  -->  00:06:03,660
that we can manipulate to discover bugs.
144

144

00:06:03,660  -->  00:06:07,170
And then we're using the backward slash to say,
145

145

00:06:07,170  -->  00:06:09,840
this question mark should not be treated
146

146

00:06:09,840  -->  00:06:11,370
as a special character,
147

147

00:06:11,370  -->  00:06:13,800
it's actually part of the pattern.
148

148

00:06:13,800  -->  00:06:16,560
And we're actually using a special character
149

149

00:06:16,560  -->  00:06:19,290
as a question mark in here to say,
150

150

00:06:19,290  -->  00:06:23,880
that I want to stop as soon as this pattern is discovered,
151

151

00:06:23,880  -->  00:06:27,090
I don't want you to continue matching the whole line
152

152

00:06:27,090  -->  00:06:28,863
that contains this pattern.
153

153

00:06:30,060  -->  00:06:32,940
Then you can modify the settings in here
154

154

00:06:32,940  -->  00:06:34,770
if you wanna match any other files,
155

155

00:06:34,770  -->  00:06:37,620
like CSS files, starlink files, and so on,
156

156

00:06:37,620  -->  00:06:39,690
but I'm happy with what I have in here.
157

157

00:06:39,690  -->  00:06:40,800
And once you're happy,
158

158

we're gonna click on apply, and perfect,
159

159

00:06:43,303  -->  00:06:46,500
that should have filtered what I'm looking for.

160

So, as you can see, we have all of the files

161

that contain what I'm looking for right now.

69

70

72

73

74

75

75

00:03:21,330  -->  00:03:24,570

76

79

00:03:33,420  -->  00:03:34,253
The only problem
80

80

00:03:34,253  -->  00:03:37,320
is it's being enclosed between two quotations
81

81

00:03:37,320  -->  00:03:39,930

82

00:03:39,930  -->  00:03:41,700
So whatever we type in here

83

is only gonna be treated like a normal text
84

84

or normal string.

86

But we covered numerous methods
87

87

of breaking out of quotations before

91

92

93

through it step by step.

94

00:04:04,770  -->  00:04:06,960
So let's copy this to a text editor

95

96

00:04:08,970  -->  00:04:12,120
and then we will inject it through the promoCode parameter.
97

97

00:04:12,120  -->  00:04:14,910

98

00:04:14,910  -->  00:04:17,460

99

99

00:04:17,460  -->  00:04:19,920
And as you know, the first thing that we need to do
100

100

00:04:19,920  -->  00:04:22,920
is to break out of these two quotation marks
101

101

00:04:22,920  -->  00:04:25,050
so that we can inject code.
102

102

00:04:25,050  -->  00:04:27,090
And that's why in our UKONLY,
103

103

00:04:27,090  -->  00:04:30,150
we're gonna add our own quotation mark.
104

104

00:04:30,150  -->  00:04:33,360
And then we're gonna add the semicolon to end the statement
105

105

00:04:33,360  -->  00:04:35,130
because as you know in JavaScript,
106

106

00:04:35,130  -->  00:04:36,660
and as we covered before,
107

107

00:04:36,660  -->  00:04:39,300
each statement needs to end in a semicolon.
108

108

00:04:39,300  -->  00:04:42,570
So that ends all of the statement right here.
109

109

00:04:42,570  -->  00:04:45,600
And then we can start injecting our code.
110

110

00:04:45,600  -->  00:04:48,720
So we're gonna do alert two
111

111

00:04:48,720  -->  00:04:51,150
and then we're gonna put a semicolon as well
112

112

00:04:51,150  -->  00:04:52,770
to end our code.
113

113

00:04:52,770  -->  00:04:55,440
And finally, we have these two characters
114

114

00:04:55,440  -->  00:04:57,150
that will actually break our code
115

115

00:04:57,150  -->  00:04:59,520
because right now, they are irrelevant
116

116

00:04:59,520  -->  00:05:01,410
and that's why in our injection,
117

117

00:05:01,410  -->  00:05:04,110
we're also gonna add two forward slashes
118

118

00:05:04,110  -->  00:05:07,410
to comment out the characters that come after it
119

119

00:05:07,410  -->  00:05:09,420
so that the browser ignores them
120

120

00:05:09,420  -->  00:05:12,990
and does not execute them as part of the code.
121

121

00:05:12,990  -->  00:05:15,420
So the browser is gonna have this original statement,
122

122

00:05:15,420  -->  00:05:16,530
which is valid.
123

123

00:05:16,530  -->  00:05:18,570
And then it's gonna have another statement,
124

124

00:05:18,570  -->  00:05:20,340
which is the one that we are injecting,
125

125

00:05:20,340  -->  00:05:22,290
which is also valid.
126

126

00:05:22,290  -->  00:05:24,750
So hopefully, if there is no security used,
127

127

00:05:24,750  -->  00:05:26,100
and there is no filters,
128

128

00:05:26,100  -->  00:05:28,650

129

129

to execute the alert code in here
130

130

00:05:31,020  -->  00:05:35,520
and therefore, I will have a stored XSS in the menu page.
131

131

00:05:35,520  -->  00:05:37,320
So let's go ahead and test this out.
132

132

133

134

135

136

136

00:05:49,950  -->  00:05:51,780

137

00:05:51,780  -->  00:05:54,393

138

141

142

00:06:02,340  -->  00:06:04,893
So let's refresh the source code of the menu.
143

143

00:06:06,540  -->  00:06:09,120
And if we look in here at the bottom,
144

144

00:06:09,120  -->  00:06:12,900
you can see that the UKONLY has been injected as is
145

145

00:06:12,900  -->  00:06:14,880
and the single quote that we're injecting
146

146

00:06:14,880  -->  00:06:17,340
is actually closing the original single quotes.
147

147

00:06:17,340  -->  00:06:19,860
So that's not filtered and that's perfect
148

148

00:06:19,860  -->  00:06:22,590
but the problem is the alert got filtered.
149

149

00:06:22,590  -->  00:06:25,020
We don't have an alert in here.
150

150

00:06:25,020  -->  00:06:27,540
And also, the double forward slashes
151

151

00:06:27,540  -->  00:06:30,690
were actually replaced with double backward slashes
152

152

00:06:30,690  -->  00:06:32,940
and therefore, they broke the code
153

153

00:06:32,940  -->  00:06:36,690

154

154

00:06:36,690  -->  00:06:39,273
and that's why our injection did not work.
155

155

00:06:40,110  -->  00:06:41,400
So we have two problems.
156

156

00:06:41,400  -->  00:06:44,400
First of all, we can't use comments in here.
157

157

00:06:44,400  -->  00:06:46,560
The double slashes are being filtered
158

158

00:06:46,560  -->  00:06:49,053
and also, the alert is being filtered.
159

159

00:06:50,430  -->  00:06:53,100
Now, you can try to use different types of comments
160

160

00:06:53,100  -->  00:06:56,130
but I've tested it and again, none of them is gonna work.
161

161

00:06:56,130  -->  00:06:58,290
Therefore, instead of using comments,
162

162

00:06:58,290  -->  00:07:01,170

163

163

00:07:01,170  -->  00:07:04,140

164

164

00:07:04,140  -->  00:07:06,480

165

165

00:07:06,480  -->  00:07:08,580
so that they do not break my code
166

166

00:07:08,580  -->  00:07:11,730
and so that I will not need to use a comment.
167

167

00:07:11,730  -->  00:07:13,680

168

168

00:07:13,680  -->  00:07:16,710
I'm just gonna say I'm gonna create a variable
169

169

00:07:16,710  -->  00:07:18,300
and I'm gonna call it x
170

170

00:07:18,300  -->  00:07:22,440

171

171

00:07:22,440  -->  00:07:26,220
and this way, when the program adds the single quote
172

172

00:07:26,220  -->  00:07:28,980
and the semicolon here at the end,
173

173

00:07:28,980  -->  00:07:32,970

174

174

00:07:32,970  -->  00:07:36,300
and therefore, this statement is not broken anymore.
175

175

00:07:36,300  -->  00:07:37,680
And this is gonna work
176

176

00:07:37,680  -->  00:07:40,953
without the need to comment out the rest of the statement.
177

177

00:07:42,330  -->  00:07:45,510
Now, the other problem that I have with the page is the fact
178

178

00:07:45,510  -->  00:07:48,123
that alert is actually being filtered.
179

179

00:07:48,990  -->  00:07:51,450
So we can use a trick that we actually covered
180

180

00:07:51,450  -->  00:07:54,780
in the directory traversal vulnerability section.
181

181

00:07:54,780  -->  00:07:57,390
And what we did in that section
182

182

00:07:57,390  -->  00:08:00,750
is the ../ were being filtered
183

183

00:08:00,750  -->  00:08:02,430
and instead, we doubled it.
184

184

00:08:02,430  -->  00:08:05,400
So whenever the ../ is filtered,
185

185

00:08:05,400  -->  00:08:08,130
we still have a ../
186

186

00:08:08,130  -->  00:08:11,040
So what we can do, we can write another alert
187

187

00:08:11,040  -->  00:08:12,840
inside the big alert
188

188

00:08:12,840  -->  00:08:15,420
and then the small alert is gonna be filtered
189

189

00:08:15,420  -->  00:08:16,920
and once that is removed,
190

190

00:08:16,920  -->  00:08:19,263
the result is gonna be a normal alert.
191

191

00:08:20,580  -->  00:08:22,710
So all I'm gonna do right here
192

192

00:08:22,710  -->  00:08:26,013
is basically write another alert in here.
193

193

00:08:27,060  -->  00:08:30,300
And this code right now, this payload hopefully will work

because we know the single quote

195

00:00:00,450  -->  00:00:01,950
<v Instructor>As mentioned previously,</v>
2

2

00:00:01,950  -->  00:00:04,650
testing every single visible functionality
3

3

00:00:04,650  -->  00:00:06,810
and pressing every visible button
4

4

00:00:06,810  -->  00:00:09,690
does not guarantee that you covered everything
5

5

00:00:09,690  -->  00:00:11,280
and you tested everything.
6

6

00:00:11,280  -->  00:00:15,780
And we saw how we could actually analyze the code in here,
7

7

00:00:15,780  -->  00:00:19,230
and use RegEx to actually discover endpoints
8

8

00:00:19,230  -->  00:00:20,610
that we've never seen before
9

9

00:00:20,610  -->  00:00:23,610
that are buried and hidden within the code.
10

10

00:00:23,610  -->  00:00:26,760
So in this lecture, I wanna continue on that idea
11

11

00:00:26,760  -->  00:00:29,070
of trying to discover hidden features
12

12

00:00:29,070  -->  00:00:31,980
and hopefully hidden endpoints and parameters
13

13

00:00:31,980  -->  00:00:35,250
so that we can test them and hopefully discover more bugs.
14

14

00:00:35,250  -->  00:00:36,570
To do that, in this lecture,
15

15

00:00:36,570  -->  00:00:37,680
we're actually gonna rely
16

16

00:00:37,680  -->  00:00:39,970
on a Chrome extension called LazySec.

17

00:00:39,970  -->  00:00:41,910
Three is another one called Unhide.
18

18

00:00:41,910  -->  00:00:44,220
There is actually a lot of these extensions,
19

19

00:00:44,220  -->  00:00:46,260
and basically, once you have it installed,
20

20

00:00:46,260  -->  00:00:47,700
and installing it is very easy,
21

21

00:00:47,700  -->  00:00:49,320
just click on Add to Chrome,

22

00:00:49,320  -->  00:00:50,550
you will have it in here,

23

00:00:50,550  -->  00:00:52,020
and once you have it in here,
24

24

00:00:52,020  -->  00:00:53,970
you can go to the target website,
25

25

00:00:53,970  -->  00:00:55,770
and click it and hopefully,

26

00:00:55,770  -->  00:00:57,630
it will display some useful elements
27

00:00:57,630  -->  00:01:00,060
or useful links or useful input boxes

29

29

00:01:02,610  -->  00:01:04,713
and therefore, help you discover a bug.
30

30

00:01:05,760  -->  00:01:09,420
So let's have a quick example here in the Home page.

133

00:07:00,570  -->  00:07:03,810
you can actually inject JavaScript code directly
175

175

00:07:03,810  -->  00:07:07,020
into the href without having to escape this,
176

176

without even having to complete this element.
177

177

00:07:10,290  -->  00:07:14,580
So all we have to do is type javascript,
178

178

followed by the JavaScript that we want to run,
179

179

00:07:17,100  -->  00:07:19,170
and we're just gonna do a simple alert.
180

180

00:07:19,170  -->  00:07:21,000

181

181

00:07:21,000  -->  00:07:22,650
We're gonna hit Enter.
182

182

00:07:22,650  -->  00:07:24,543
And let's click on Login.
183

183

00:07:25,560  -->  00:07:27,180
Click the link and sure enough,
184

184

00:07:27,180  -->  00:07:29,670
our JavaScript code gets executed,
185

185

00:07:29,670  -->  00:07:31,740
so now we actually discovered two bugs.
186

186

00:07:31,740  -->  00:07:34,110
We discovered an open redirect
187

187

00:07:34,110  -->  00:07:36,870
and a reflected XSS vulnerability
188

188

00:07:36,870  -->  00:07:38,610
and you should actually get bounties
189

189

00:07:38,610  -->  00:07:39,990
for both of these bugs,
190

190

00:07:39,990  -->  00:07:43,410
and we managed to do all of this using a hidden element
191

191

00:07:43,410  -->  00:07:44,910
that we've never seen before,
192

192

00:07:44,910  -->  00:07:46,560

193

193

00:07:46,560  -->  00:07:48,300

194

194

00:07:48,300  -->  00:07:50,130

195

195

00:07:50,130  -->  00:07:52,620
that will display hidden elements for us.
196

196

00:07:52,620  -->  00:07:54,780
We did better research based on everything
197

197

00:07:54,780  -->  00:07:57,090
that we learned so far, and as you can see,
198

198

00:07:57,090  -->  00:08:00,513
we ended up discovering two more bugs in the target website.

16

Now, because the name in here is not very meaningful,

39

50

52

53

54

56

61

62

62

76

77

78

79

81

84

85

105

00:04:53,430  -->  00:04:55,380

108

109

109

110

110

111

111

00:05:12,240  -->  00:05:14,760
followed by the code that we want to execute and,

113

Hit enter.

114

And perfect.

115

As you can see, our code is getting executed on the page,
116

116

00:05:24,630  -->  00:05:26,340
which means that we discovered
117

117

00:05:26,340  -->  00:05:28,770
a Reflected XSS vulnerability.
118

118

00:05:28,770  -->  00:05:32,430
So now, we have two vulnerabilities discovered in this page
119

119

00:05:32,430  -->  00:05:35,700
and both of these vulnerabilities are within elements
120

120

121

122

122

00:05:41,160  -->  00:05:44,520
by viewing hidden elements using this extension right here
123

123

00:05:44,520  -->  00:05:47,130
and then analyzing the JavaScript code.
124

124

00:05:47,130  -->  00:05:50,400
And also, a very important take on lesson from this
125

125

00:05:50,400  -->  00:05:52,980
is that you should not worry about the code
126

126

00:05:52,980  -->  00:05:55,680
even if you don't know that programming language.
127

127

00:05:55,680  -->  00:05:58,560
You can always just follow it step-by-step
128

128

00:05:58,560  -->  00:06:01,080
and look up whatever functions or methods
129

129

00:06:01,080  -->  00:06:04,110
that you do not understand and learn that way.
130

130

00:06:04,110  -->  00:06:06,150
This is a very important trait
131

131

00:06:06,150  -->  00:06:08,160
that really good bug hunters have.
132

132

00:06:08,160  -->  00:06:12,210
A lot of the time they approach systems and code bases
133

133

00:06:12,210  -->  00:06:15,360
and applications and frameworks that they do not understand.
134

134

00:06:15,360  -->  00:06:17,910
So they might discover a hint that there is a bug
135

135

00:06:17,910  -->  00:06:20,100
and then they discover that they don't really know
136

136

00:06:20,100  -->  00:06:22,650
how this system works or how this framework works
137

137

00:06:22,650  -->  00:06:25,260
and they actually go ahead and learn about the framework
138

138

00:06:25,260  -->  00:06:27,300
and read the documentation and see
139

139

00:06:27,300  -->  00:06:30,300
if that can help them understand the target application
140

140

00:06:30,300  -->  00:06:33,090
and then they go ahead and apply what they learned in order
141

141

00:06:33,090  -->  00:06:35,340
to discover their bugs and vulnerabilities.
142

142

00:06:35,340  -->  00:06:37,650
That's why a lot of bug hunters are referred to
143

143

00:06:37,650  -->  00:06:41,130
as researchers because there is a lot of research involved
144

144

00:06:41,130  -->  00:06:42,580
when it comes to bug hunting.

# Section 22: Participating in Bug Bounty Programs

1
1

00:00:00,570  -->  00:00:02,970
<v Narrator>At this stage, you should be at a level</v>
2

2

00:00:02,970  -->  00:00:06,900
where you are able to discover bugs in real-life platforms.
3

3

00:00:06,900  -->  00:00:09,510
So I showed you how to discover a large number
4

4

00:00:09,510  -->  00:00:11,220
of bugs and vulnerabilities.
5

5

00:00:11,220  -->  00:00:13,050
I showed you how you could discover them
6

6

00:00:13,050  -->  00:00:14,250
in different scenarios.
7

7

00:00:14,250  -->  00:00:17,790
I showed you how you can bypass security and filtering.
8

8

00:00:17,790  -->  00:00:21,570
And at the end, I even showed you my own strategy
9

9

00:00:21,570  -->  00:00:23,640
to testing websites and applications
10

10

00:00:23,640  -->  00:00:26,280
and discovering bugs and vulnerabilities in them.
11

11

00:00:26,280  -->  00:00:28,980
So at this stage, you should be able to go ahead
12

12

00:00:28,980  -->  00:00:31,530
and discover your own bugs and vulnerabilities
13

13

00:00:31,530  -->  00:00:34,440
and hopefully cash some money for that.
14

14

00:00:34,440  -->  00:00:36,990
So the only thing that we haven't covered so far,
15

15

00:00:36,990  -->  00:00:40,020
how do you go ahead and start hunting for bugs?
16

16

00:00:40,020  -->  00:00:42,870
Now, before I show you where to start and how to start,
17

17

00:00:42,870  -->  00:00:47,010
it's very important to understand where not to start.
18

18

00:00:47,010  -->  00:00:48,360
So you want to make sure
19

19

00:00:48,360  -->  00:00:52,080
that you do not test applications without permission.
20

20

00:00:52,080  -->  00:00:53,850
So you can't simply just go ahead,
21

21

00:00:53,850  -->  00:00:56,490
load the website, and start discovering bugs in it
22

22

00:00:56,490  -->  00:00:58,320
and then start contacting the admin
23

23

00:00:58,320  -->  00:01:00,090
of the website out of nowhere.
24

24

00:01:00,090  -->  00:01:01,230
This is illegal.
25

25

00:01:01,230  -->  00:01:03,750
You're testing a website with no permission.
26

26

00:01:03,750  -->  00:01:05,790
So you have to understand that.
27

27

00:01:05,790  -->  00:01:09,120
Instead, you should test applications and websites
28

28

00:01:09,120  -->  00:01:12,900
that actually have a real, proper bug bounty program.
29

29

00:01:12,900  -->  00:01:14,670
And there is so many of them.
30

30

00:01:14,670  -->  00:01:16,920
So all you have to do is simply go on Google
31

31

00:01:16,920  -->  00:01:19,230
and look for bug bounty programs,
32

32

00:01:19,230  -->  00:01:21,870
or you could look for bug bounty platforms.
33

33

00:01:21,870  -->  00:01:25,110
And these platforms provide everything that you need
34

34

00:01:25,110  -->  00:01:26,880
in order to start bug hunting,
35

35

00:01:26,880  -->  00:01:29,430
from the process of discovering websites
36

36

00:01:29,430  -->  00:01:31,500
with proper bug bounty programs,
37

37

00:01:31,500  -->  00:01:32,880
to submitting the bugs,
38

38

00:01:32,880  -->  00:01:37,050
to communicating with the website security team or owners,
39

39

00:01:37,050  -->  00:01:39,090
all the way up to the point
40

40

00:01:39,090  -->  00:01:41,670
where you actually get paid your bounty.
41

41

00:01:41,670  -->  00:01:44,760
So if you're a beginner, I highly recommend starting here.
42

42

00:01:44,760  -->  00:01:47,280
But just for the sake of completion,
43

43

00:01:47,280  -->  00:01:48,390
I'm just gonna go quickly
44

44

00:01:48,390  -->  00:01:50,910
and just run a quick Google search just to see
45

45

00:01:50,910  -->  00:01:54,420
what bug bounty programs are available right now.
46

46

00:01:54,420  -->  00:01:57,120
So as you can see, I just typed in bug bounty programs.
47

47

00:01:57,120  -->  00:01:59,220
This is an ad for a bug bounty platform,
48

48

00:01:59,220  -->  00:02:01,320
and we will talk about that in a second.
49

49

00:02:01,320  -->  00:02:04,050
But as you can see, you have a lot of websites
50

50

00:02:04,050  -->  00:02:06,240
that are listing other websites
51

51

00:02:06,240  -->  00:02:08,310
that have a valid bug bounty program,
